rows.plain: Lower the chance that the control recipe loaded into the equipment differs from the approved instructions for the process. This helps ensure the equipment runs the cycle as intended.
rows.imp_rat: A loaded recipe differing from approved spec means the executed cycle isn't the validated one; central and severe because unvalidated cleaning can fail undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management and change control reliably ensure the loaded recipe matches the approved spec in managed systems. Electronic recipe management loads approved recipes quickly without manual entry. Version-controlled recipe systems reduce operator judgement about which recipe is correct. Preventing wrong-recipe execution avoids costly failed cleans.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing a cleaning or sterilization cycle to a circuit other than the scheduled circuit
rows.plain: A cleaning or germ-killing cycle should be sent only to the pipe system scheduled for it. This helps prevent the wrong part of the equipment from being cleaned or treated.
rows.imp_rat: Routing a cycle to an unscheduled circuit means the intended circuit is left uncleaned while another is unexpectedly exposed, directly defeating the cleaning job and risking cross-contamination, so both proxies are high.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven valve sequencing and circuit interlocks make wrong-circuit routing uncommon in well-configured plants. Automated valve routing to the scheduled circuit avoids manual line-up delays. Routing logic reduces dependence on an operator manually and correctly selecting the target circuit. Misrouting wastes a full cycle of chemical and energy, so correct routing directly protects cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of initiating cleaning while product remains in equipment required to be empty
rows.plain: Do not start cleaning while product is still inside equipment that is supposed to be empty. This prevents product from being lost, mixed, or exposed to cleaning materials.
rows.imp_rat: Initiating cleaning while product remains in supposedly empty equipment risks contamination and spec failure; severity high though the specific trigger condition is less frequent.
rows.sat_rat: Level checks and interlocks catch most cases, but manual confirmation still allows occasional cleaning with product present. A pre-clean check adds minor time but prevents major rework delays. Confirming equipment is empty relies on operator verification and interlocks, needing modest attention. Cleaning over remaining product wastes product and agent and causes deviations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of active cycle medium leaking across isolation boundaries into an unselected sterile process path
rows.plain: Prevent the active process liquid from leaking across a separation into a sterile path that was not selected. This helps keep the wrong liquid from entering a sterile process.
rows.imp_rat: Active cycle medium leaking across isolation boundaries into an unselected sterile path can contaminate a supposedly clean line and defeat segregation, central to multi-path system integrity (relevance 8). Cross-contamination of a sterile path carries severe product-safety consequences (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Double-block-and-bleed and mix-proof valve technology make cross-boundary leakage into sterile paths uncommon in modern designs. Valve sequencing enforces isolation without adding meaningful cycle time. Engineered mix-proof valves remove most operator dependence on manual isolation judgement. Cross-contamination events are rare but costly, so the engineered protection avoids large sporadic losses.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of microbial under-recovery from sampled equipment surfaces
rows.plain: Make sure samples taken from equipment surfaces do not miss or undercount the germs that are actually there. This gives a more reliable picture of cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Microbial under-recovery from surfaces understates true bioburden; moderately central to valid verification and severe because it can pass contaminated equipment as clean.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery-factor correction is established practice but real surface recovery remains variable and often optimistic. Recovery studies take time but the loss is measurement accuracy, not schedule. Executing and interpreting recovery-efficiency studies requires trained microbiologists. Under-recovery causes hidden failures with large downstream cost, only partly offset.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contaminants entering an opened SIP circuit during steam-trap service
rows.plain: Prevent dirt or other unwanted material from entering opened sterilization pipes while the drain devices are being serviced. This helps keep the system clean and safe to use afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Contaminants entering an opened SIP circuit during steam-trap service directly reintroduces bioburden the system exists to eliminate; relevance is high for the sterilization job and severity is severe because it can breach the sterile boundary undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Opening the circuit for trap service is an established contamination pathway that procedures reduce but do not eliminate. Steam-trap service on an opened SIP circuit requires cooldown and re-sterilization, extending downtime. Keeping an opened circuit clean during trap work depends on technician discipline and aseptic handling. Trap rebuild parts and re-sterilization are modest but recurring costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unplanned cleaning-agent circulation interruptions during an active phase
rows.plain: Reduce unexpected stops in the circulation of cleaning liquid while a cleaning step is underway. Continuous circulation helps the cleaning step work properly.
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned circulation interruptions during an active phase truncate cleaning central to efficacy; high severity because contact/exposure shortfalls leave contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Modern CIP skids run reliably, but pump trips and valve faults still cause occasional unplanned interruptions. Interruptions directly add downtime, but the need is about avoiding the failure. Preventing interruptions depends on system robustness more than operator skill. Interruptions waste agent and force reruns, incurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a stored program that is obsolete or unapproved for the current equipment and residue
rows.plain: Choose a current, approved cleaning process that suits the machine and the leftover material being removed. This helps ensure the equipment is cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Choosing an obsolete or unapproved stored program for the current equipment and residue risks an ineffective clean; this is central to reliable outcomes and can leave hazardous residue, so both proxies are high.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and version management reduce obsolete-recipe use, yet stale entries and manual overrides create recurring exposure. Selecting from a validated recipe library is quick when version control is in place. Operators rely on the system to hide obsolete recipes rather than judging approval status themselves. An obsolete program can cause failed cleaning and costly re-runs or product loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sequence enters a cleaning phase while its prescribed circulation path is unavailable
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the cleaning process starts when the required route for circulating cleaning fluid is not available. This helps ensure the equipment is cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Entering a phase while its circulation path is unavailable means cleaning agent never reaches the target; central to coverage and severe because affected sections stay uncleaned.
rows.sat_rat: Valve-position permissives reliably gate phase entry in well-configured systems, though gaps remain for unmonitored paths. Path-availability permissives are checked instantly at phase entry. Permissive logic reduces operator need to verify paths manually. Blocking entry with an unavailable path avoids wasted cleaning agent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of interpreting cycle readings against incorrect validated limits
rows.plain: Make sure process readings are compared with the correct approved limits for that cycle. Using the wrong limits could make a problem appear acceptable when it is not.
rows.imp_rat: Interpreting cycle readings against incorrect validated limits can wrongly pass an unclean or unsterile cycle, striking at the verification heart of the CFJ; severity is high because a false pass releases contaminated product.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-embedded validated limits reduce misinterpretation, though version control and manual review still cause occasional wrong-limit use. Automated limit checking against stored recipes is instantaneous. Interpreting readings against correct validated limits requires trained personnel where limits are manually referenced. Using wrong limits rarely wastes material directly but can pass bad cycles or force reruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a filtration or treatment component being incorrectly installed during replacement
rows.plain: Make sure replacement parts that clean or filter the water are put in the right way. Incorrect installation could leave the water insufficiently treated.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly installed filtration or treatment components are central to reliable media conditioning, and misinstallation can bypass filtration or cause leaks, risking contamination and compromised sterilization outcomes.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and directional markings help, but misinstalled treatment components remain an intermittent, real failure mode. Filter/treatment component replacement is a bounded task within normal maintenance time. Correct orientation and installation depend on the technician following the procedure attentively. An incorrectly installed component wastes rework and can cause treatment failures costing product.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of test overrides or simulated inputs left active after testing
rows.plain: Make sure temporary test settings and pretend signals are turned off after testing. Otherwise, the machine might respond as if a problem or signal were still being tested.
rows.imp_rat: Test overrides or simulated inputs left active after testing can silently defeat interlocks during real cycles; it is a maintenance-hygiene issue but a latent override causing an undetected unsafe cycle is highly consequential.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on manual checklists and human recall, so residual active overrides after testing remain a recurring, high-consequence failure mode. Reverting overrides adds little time when a closeout checklist exists, but the check itself is often skipped under pressure. Catching a stray forced input or simulated signal depends heavily on an experienced technician's memory and diligence rather than a robust system. An override left active is cheap to fix if caught but can trigger costly batch loss or contamination if missed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of using cleaned fixed equipment beyond its validated clean-hold boundary
rows.plain: Reduce how often cleaned equipment is used after the approved time limit for staying clean has passed. This helps ensure the equipment is still in an acceptable condition.
rows.imp_rat: Using equipment beyond its validated clean-hold boundary reintroduces bioburden into the process; central to sustained cleanliness and severe as re-contaminated equipment enters use.
rows.sat_rat: Established clean-hold time limits and labeling work well, though occasional lapses in busy operations still occur. Clean-hold timers and expiry labels are tracked, but manual monitoring can lag when schedules slip. Enforcing hold boundaries relies on procedural discipline and operator attention more than automation. Exceeding hold time forces re-cleaning, a modest but recurring resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of circulation interruptions that shorten cleaning-agent contact with fixed equipment and piping during a cycle
rows.plain: Reduce how often flow stops during cleaning, because interruptions shorten the time the cleaning liquid touches equipment and pipes. Consistent contact helps cleaning work as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Circulation interruptions shortening contact time directly reduce cleaning efficacy central to the job; severity high as insufficient contact leaves residue/bioburden.
rows.sat_rat: Automated sequencing with flow/pressure interlocks reliably enforces contact time in most systems, though valve faults and pump trips still cause occasional interruptions. Interruptions extend or force restart of cycles, directly costing time when they occur. PLC sequencing and interlocks handle contact-time enforcement with little operator intervention. Restarts waste chemical, water, and energy, adding avoidable cost when interruptions happen.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the source of an alarm or out-of-spec condition during the cycle
rows.plain: Make sure workers can correctly identify what caused a warning or problem during the process. This helps them fix the right issue instead of guessing.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying an alarm source is central to CIP/SIP because troubleshooting and cycle recovery depend on it; if wrong, operators waste time and may take unsafe actions, though a competent operator can often recover, moderating severity.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-source resolution is often coarse, so misattribution of the fault to the wrong subsystem is a common recurring gap. Locating an out-of-spec source across piping and valves still adds troubleshooting time despite alarm logs. Pinpointing which sensor, valve or circuit caused the fault demands seasoned diagnostic skill not every shift has. Chasing the wrong source wastes labor and can prompt needless re-runs, a real recurring expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that instrument readings misrepresent actual CIP cycle conditions
rows.plain: Make sure the instruments show the true conditions during the equipment cleaning cycle. Incorrect readings could make a cleaning cycle appear successful when it was not.
rows.imp_rat: Instrument readings misrepresenting actual conditions undermine both control and release decisions; central to a monitored process and severe as false confidence releases unclean equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Routine calibration and verification keep instrument accuracy generally trustworthy, though undetected drift remains an occasional risk. Instrument calibration is periodic, so drift can go unnoticed between calibrations. Detecting sensor misrepresentation requires metrology skill and awareness beyond routine operation. Calibration programs and redundant sensors add ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between indicated and actual cycle parameter values
rows.plain: Keep the values shown by the equipment as close as possible to the values that are actually occurring. This helps ensure the process is running as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between indicated and actual cycle parameters undermines confidence that lethality/cleaning was actually achieved — a core assurance mechanism (relevance 8). Undetected deviation can pass a failed cycle as good, with serious downstream safety impact (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Routine calibration programs keep indicated-vs-actual deviation modest, but sensor drift and placement errors leave recurring discrepancies. Calibrated live readouts give near-real-time parameter feedback with negligible lag. Trusting indicated values requires a calibration/verification discipline that leans on metrology skill. Instrument drift between calibrations can cause failed batches or reruns, an occasional resource cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of doubt that specified circulation requirements cover residue-prone equipment and piping paths across sterile fill-finish cycles
rows.plain: Reduce the time spent unsure whether the required circulation reaches equipment and pipes where residue may collect during sterile production. Clear confirmation helps show that all relevant paths are cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that circulation requirements cover residue-prone paths in sterile fill-finish undermines assurance at high stakes; moderately central and severe given sterile-product risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration that the solution temperature remains below the setpoint range during the cycle
rows.plain: Reduce the time that the solution is colder than the allowed temperature range during the process. This helps keep the process within its required conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Duration below setpoint range directly threatens thermal lethality/cleaning efficacy — time-below-temperature is a core failure mode (relevance 8). Sustained under-temperature can leave the system non-sterile with serious safety consequences (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Time-at-temperature gating ensures the cycle does not credit sub-setpoint periods, keeping outcomes reliable. Below-setpoint duration is the core loss here, and heat-up curves plus hold-timer gating minimize wasted sub-temperature time. Little operator skill is needed since controllers automatically hold timing until setpoint is reached. Prolonged below-setpoint operation wastes energy and can extend cycles, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a program outside its validated equipment configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the selected cleaning program is made for the machine being cleaned. This helps prevent the wrong cleaning process from being used.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a program outside its validated equipment configuration means the cycle may not clean or sterilize correctly, defeating the job and creating a validated-state breach with compliance and product-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-equipment binding in the control system catches most mismatches, but manual selection lists still permit occasional errors. Program-to-equipment validation checks are near-instant selections at the HMI. Configuration matching offloads the operator from having to remember which recipe is valid for which equipment. Running an invalid program risks scrapped batches and revalidation, a significant avoided cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of filter housing leakage or filtration bypass after reassembly
rows.plain: Prevent the filter holder from leaking or allowing liquid to go around the filter after the filter is put back together. This helps ensure the product is properly filtered.
rows.imp_rat: Filter housing leakage or filtration bypass after reassembly compromises the integrity the system is meant to assure; it is important and a bypass allowing contaminants through carries serious product-safety consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Standard integrity/pressure tests catch many failures, yet filtration bypass from imperfect reseating still occurs occasionally, leaving real residual gaps. Reassembly and leak testing of filter housings is a routine, reasonably quick procedure during maintenance. Correct seating and torque require trained hands, but the tasks are well-documented and within normal technician competence. Pressure or integrity testing after reassembly costs modest labor and consumables relative to the risk of bypass.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a cycle phase advances before required cleaning conditions stabilize
rows.plain: Do not move to the next cleaning step until the required cleaning conditions have settled and stayed steady. This helps ensure the cleaning process works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: A phase advancing before conditions stabilize undermines effective cleaning central to CFJ; high severity as unstable conditions yield inadequate cleaning.
rows.sat_rat: Setpoint-stabilization gating is common in modern controllers though not every parameter is always gated, leaving some residual risk. Stabilization holds add short delays but occur automatically within the recipe. Stabilization conditions are configured in the recipe, limiting operator skill demand. Waiting for stabilization costs little beyond marginal cycle time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical cleaning-cycle parameters outside approved limits at cycle completion
rows.plain: Make sure the important cleaning settings are within their approved ranges when cleaning is finished. This helps ensure the equipment is safe and ready for use.
rows.imp_rat: Critical parameters outside approved limits at cycle completion means the cycle did not run as validated; central to proving the job and severe as it signals a failed clean.
rows.sat_rat: Cycle-completion parameter evaluation against limits is a standard, reliable feature of modern CIP recipes. End-of-cycle parameter checks are automated and quick. Automated acceptance evaluation reduces the interpretation burden on operators. Catching out-of-limit completion prevents releasing a bad clean at low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification samples from fixed equipment with bioburden results above acceptance limits
rows.plain: Fewer samples taken from permanently installed equipment should show more unwanted microorganisms than allowed after cleaning. This indicates that the cleaning process is working effectively.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification samples with bioburden over limits indicate the cleaning job is not validated as effective; high severity as it invalidates process and risks contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Structured qualification protocols with defined sampling make above-limit results uncommon, though hard-to-reach points still occasionally fail. Qualification bioburden sampling and incubation extend timelines, but this is planned into the validation schedule. Aseptic swab and rinse sampling plus microbial recovery require skilled, trained personnel to avoid false results. Requalification sampling campaigns consume analyst time and lab resources but are periodic rather than continuous.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incorrect flow routing sends cleaning material to an open equipment boundary
rows.plain: Prevent cleaning fluid from being sent to an open equipment connection by mistake. This helps keep workers from being exposed to the fluid.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect routing sending cleaning material to an open boundary risks chemical release and cross-contamination; less central to routine cleaning but severity high for safety and product.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks and valve position feedback reduce misrouting, but manual connection points leave residual risk. Misrouting to an open boundary is a setup-time risk. Preventing this depends on operator routing accuracy and boundary awareness. Sending agent to an open boundary causes spills and cleanup cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualification samples from fixed equipment with residue results above acceptance limits
rows.plain: Make sure samples taken from permanent equipment after cleaning do not contain too much leftover material. This shows whether the cleaning method works well enough.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification samples with residue over limits show the cleaning process is not proven adequate; high severity for validation and contamination consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Well-designed qualification protocols keep residue exceedances uncommon, though worst-case surfaces sometimes reveal gaps. Residue qualification sampling and analysis add lead time but are scheduled within validation activities. Correct swab technique and recovery studies require experienced analysts to avoid understating residue. Qualification residue testing is resource-intensive but bounded to defined validation runs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the sequence follows a phase branch incompatible with the unit cleaning configuration
rows.plain: Use a cleaning-step sequence that matches how the equipment is set up. This helps ensure all needed parts are cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Following a phase branch incompatible with the unit configuration applies wrong cleaning logic; central to correct execution and severe as it can leave equipment inadequately cleaned.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks against unit configuration exist but coverage is uneven, leaving moderate risk of incompatible branching. Configuration checks against unit state add minor setup time. Matching phase branch to unit configuration requires system knowledge. An incompatible branch wastes a full cycle if executed.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the observed alarm trigger setpoint and the specified setpoint under simulated fault conditions
rows.plain: Make sure an alarm goes off at nearly the specified level when a problem is deliberately simulated.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between observed and specified alarm trigger setpoints under simulated fault is central because alarms are the safety net that detects failed conditions, and a mis-set trigger can let a genuinely failed cycle pass undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Periodic calibration and loop checks keep setpoint deviation small and are a mature, standard practice. Verifying alarm setpoints under simulated fault is a defined calibration step of predictable duration. Simulating faults and reading setpoints requires trained instrumentation staff. Calibration labor and instruments are a routine, budgeted maintenance expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between evidence-generating exposure conditions and actual CIP conditions
rows.plain: The test conditions used to produce supporting evidence should more closely match the actual conditions used for cleaning equipment in place. A close match makes the evidence more trustworthy for judging whether cleaning controls unwanted microorganisms.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between evidence-generating exposure and actual CIP conditions means evidence doesn't reflect reality; moderately central and severe because it can overstate cleaning adequacy.
rows.sat_rat: Coupon and in-situ studies approximate real conditions but lab-to-plant gaps persist, moderating confidence. Aligning study exposure to real CIP conditions adds design time but delay isn't the core error. Replicating actual flow, temperature and contact in evidence generation needs specialist validation skill. Mismatched evidence forces expensive repeat studies when reality diverges.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of each circulation phase completed with cleaning-agent flow outside specified limits
rows.plain: Keep the cleaning liquid flowing within the required range throughout every part of the cleaning cycle. This helps ensure that all parts are cleaned properly.
rows.imp_rat: Circulation phase completed with flow outside limits directly compromises cleaning coverage/efficacy; high severity because low flow leaves dead zones with residue.
rows.sat_rat: Flow meters with control loops keep flow within limits well on instrumented systems. Flow is controlled continuously, not a delay factor. Automated flow control reduces the skill needed to maintain flow within limits. Out-of-spec flow risks failed cleans and reruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which cleaning-agent concentration deviates from its target range during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the cleaning liquid at the intended strength throughout the cleaning cycle. If it is too strong or too weak, the cleaning may not work as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration deviating from target range during the cycle degrades cleaning power central to CFJ; high severity for residue and chemical-carryover consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Conductivity-based concentration control is mature and holds target reliably in most CIP skids. Inline conductivity feedback maintains concentration continuously without added time. Automated dosing control removes most manual titration skill. Closed-loop dosing minimizes chemical overuse and out-of-range costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a safety interlock fails to halt the CIP/SIP system at the specified limit
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the emergency safety control fails to stop the cleaning system when it reaches the required limit. This helps prevent the system from continuing in an unsafe condition.
rows.imp_rat: A safety interlock failing to halt the CIP/SIP system at the specified limit is central to safe operation but is a protective layer rather than the core cleaning/sterilizing function itself (relevance 7). When it occurs, consequences can be severe: overpressure, chemical/thermal exposure, or equipment damage endangering personnel (severity 9).
rows.sat_rat: Fail-safe interlock design and mandated periodic proof-testing keep undetected interlock failures rare, though drift between tests remains a residual gap. Interlock function testing is a quick discrete check during maintenance but must be repeated at each intervention. Verifying safety-interlock logic requires controls technicians who understand the fail-safe design and test procedures. Redundant sensors and safety-rated PLCs add hardware cost that facilities routinely accept for SIL-rated safety.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of difference between the indicated and actual solution temperature during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the temperature shown by the equipment as close as possible to the solution’s actual temperature during the process. This helps ensure the solution is treated correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Difference between indicated and actual solution temperature is a specific parameter-accuracy error directly tied to whether thermal lethality/cleaning was met (relevance 8). A false high reading can mask an under-temperature cycle, risking non-sterile output (severity 8).
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated RTDs at validated points make indicated-vs-actual temperature difference small and well controlled in mature installations. Temperature sensors respond quickly so indicated temperature tracks actual with little delay. Standard RTD/thermocouple calibration is routine and needs modest specialist skill. Temperature measurement error occasionally causes reheats or failed cycles wasting energy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required alarms or safety interlocks left untested during the maintenance activity
rows.plain: Reduce the number of required alarms and safety controls that are not checked during maintenance. This helps confirm they will work when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving required alarms or safety interlocks untested during maintenance undermines the protective layer of the whole system; it is important to safe operation and, if a latent failure later occurs, consequences can be severe.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and CMMS prompts help ensure coverage, but untested interlocks slipping through remains a recognized recurring gap. Testing every alarm and interlock during maintenance is time-consuming and sometimes deferred under schedule pressure. Systematic verification of all interlocks requires trained staff working through checklists methodically. Test time and documentation labor are the main costs, generally accepted for compliance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which resumed phase conditions deviate from approved recipe limits
rows.plain: When a paused process is restarted, return it to the approved conditions. This helps ensure the process continues safely and produces the intended result.
rows.imp_rat: Resumed phase conditions deviating from approved recipe limits means the restart isn't validated; moderately central and severity high because deviant resumes can leave contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Resume-condition control is weakly automated, so deviations at resume are a recurring, poorly-guarded risk. Verifying resumed-phase conditions against limits adds troubleshooting time. Judging whether resume conditions are within limits demands experienced operators. Conservative restarts to stay in limits waste utilities and chemicals.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical parameter excursion is overlooked amid simultaneous cycle notifications
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an important measurement changing beyond its safe range is missed among many alerts. This helps people notice problems quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a parameter excursion amid many notifications is an alerting/monitoring gap that indirectly bears on cycle validity (moderate relevance); a missed excursion can release an unsterilized batch (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Poorly prioritized alarm floods remain a recognized weakness, so critical excursions can be buried despite alarm-management standards. Alarms surface in real time but excursion triage still competes with other notifications during busy periods. Distinguishing a critical parameter excursion amid a flood of simultaneous alarms depends heavily on operator vigilance and experience. Missed excursions cause costly reprocessing or product loss, and alarm rationalization projects add expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cleaning phases executed outside the prescribed order
rows.plain: Reduce how often cleaning steps are carried out in the wrong order. Following the right order helps ensure the equipment is properly cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: Phases executed outside prescribed order can defeat the cleaning chemistry central to CFJ; high severity because misordered phases leave residue or incompatible chemicals.
rows.sat_rat: Automated recipe sequencing reliably enforces prescribed order once configured correctly. Sequenced execution runs phases in order without operator scheduling time. Order enforcement is automated, requiring little operator skill during the cycle. Enforced ordering avoids rework costs from out-of-order execution.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of bias in qualification results caused by sample collection or recovery
rows.plain: Make test results less affected by how samples are collected or removed for testing. This helps the results more accurately show how well the cleaning process works.
rows.imp_rat: Bias from sample collection/recovery distorts qualification results; moderately central to trustworthy verification and severe because biased data can falsely pass a dirty system.
rows.sat_rat: Recovery correction factors mitigate known bias, yet inconsistent sampling technique remains a persistent source of unreliable results. Recovery studies are done once but sampling bias issues can force time-consuming reinvestigation. Sampling and recovery bias is highly technique-dependent, hinging on scarce, well-trained personnel. Recovery validation studies add cost but are standard practice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cleaning cycles supplied with water, cleaning agent, or supporting utilities outside validated specifications
rows.plain: Run cleaning cycles only with water, cleaning products, and other supporting supplies that meet their approved requirements. This helps ensure the cleaning process works as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Supplying water/agent/utilities outside validated spec undermines the cleaning inputs central to CFJ; high severity because bad inputs cause systematic cleaning failure.
rows.sat_rat: Continuous conductivity, TOC, and utility monitoring make supply outside specification uncommon and quickly caught. Utility and water-quality data are typically available continuously so out-of-spec supply is detected without significant delay. Utility monitoring is well-established and requires little specialized judgement beyond standard trending. Water-for-injection and utility monitoring systems are expensive to maintain but are standard infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cleaning-agent concentration departure from the specified condition during a cycle
rows.plain: Keep the strength of the cleaning liquid as close as possible to the required level throughout the cleaning process. Consistent strength helps produce reliable cleaning results.
rows.imp_rat: Concentration departure from specified condition during a cycle degrades cleaning power central to CFJ; severity high for residue and chemical-carryover risk.
rows.sat_rat: Conductivity-based concentration control is a mature, widely deployed feedback loop that holds agent strength close to setpoint reliably. Inline conductivity feedback adjusts concentration continuously without adding cycle time. Automated dosing loops need little operator judgement once configured. Overdosing wastes chemical while dilution risks reruns, but closed-loop control keeps this reasonably bounded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible cleaning agents mixing in the circuit during dosing
rows.plain: Prevent cleaning chemicals that should not be combined from mixing in the equipment. This helps avoid unsafe reactions and ineffective cleaning.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible cleaning agents mixing during dosing is moderately central to the cleaning job but severely dangerous, as it can generate toxic gas or violent reactions harming personnel and equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Dosing interlocks and single-agent recipes make incompatible mixing rare, though shared lines create residual crossover risk. Sequenced dosing adds no meaningful time when interlocks prevent simultaneous incompatible additions. Interlocked dosing reduces reliance on operator chemistry knowledge during the cycle. Incompatible mixing can generate hazardous reactions and scrap chemical batches, an occasional severe cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant equipment paths, product-contact surfaces, residue challenges, or bioburden conditions omitted from the documented evaluation
rows.plain: Include every relevant equipment route, product-touching surface, leftover material, and germ-related condition in the written evaluation. This makes the evaluation complete and easier to defend.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting relevant paths/surfaces/challenges from documented evaluation leaves unproven areas; moderately central and severity high because unassessed sites may carry contamination.
rows.sat_rat: P&IDs and riboflavin coverage studies help, but dead legs and hidden surfaces are routinely under-documented, leaving a persistent reliability gap. Mapping every equipment path and contact surface is laborious and extends evaluation timelines. Identifying hard-to-clean spots and worst-case surfaces requires deep process and engineering knowledge concentrated in few experts. Overlooked paths discovered later cause expensive revalidation and equipment access work.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which circulation temperature deviates from its target range during the cycle
rows.plain: Keep the circulating liquid or air temperature close to the required range throughout the process. This helps ensure the process works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Circulation temperature deviating from target during the cycle weakens cleaning efficacy central to CFJ; high severity because thermal shortfall leaves residue/bioburden.
rows.sat_rat: Temperature regulation is among the best-controlled CIP parameters, reliably held within range. Temperature control loops reach and hold setpoint automatically with minimal delay. PID temperature control needs little operator intervention once tuned. Steady temperature control avoids energy waste and reclean cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of cleaning cycles operated outside validated critical-parameter ranges
rows.plain: Keep cleaning cycles within the tested ranges for the settings that matter most, such as time or temperature. This helps ensure the cleaning works as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Operating outside validated critical-parameter ranges undermines the assured cleaning outcome; severity high because excursions can leave residues and void validation.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked control loops and parameter monitoring make cycles running outside validated ranges rare where automation is deployed. Automated CIP recipes enforce parameter ranges in real time so out-of-range operation is flagged during the cycle rather than after delay. PLC-driven recipes and alarm logic reduce reliance on operator judgement to hold critical parameters within validated ranges. Automation and control instrumentation are capital-intensive but standard in modern CIP skids, spreading cost across many cycles.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that pressurized cleaning material contacts personnel when a process boundary is opened
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that pressurized cleaning liquid or gas sprays onto workers when they open part of the process equipment. This helps prevent injuries and harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Pressurized cleaning material contacting personnel when a boundary is opened is peripheral to cleanliness but catastrophic for worker injury from caustic/hot chemicals.
rows.sat_rat: Depressurization interlocks and permit procedures make this uncommon but human bypass still occurs. Pressurized-contact incidents are safety events, not delays. Depressurization procedures require operator discipline before opening boundaries. Injury and incident handling carry high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of liquid retained in circuit low points after drainage
rows.plain: Leave as little liquid as possible in the lowest parts of the piping after it is drained. This helps prevent leftover material from collecting or causing problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Liquid retained in circuit low points after drainage undermines effective cleaning and can harbor residue (high relevance), with meaningful but recoverable contamination consequences (moderately high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Sloped piping and drain valves handle most drainage, but pooling in imperfectly sloped runs still occurs and is only partly verifiable. Draining low points is a quick gravity or blow-down step during preparation. Locating and opening drain points follows the P&ID and requires modest familiarity with the circuit. Minimal chemical or water loss and no significant resource cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous cleaning or sanitizing agents during recovery
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that workers are exposed to dangerous cleaning or disinfecting chemicals while recovering from a process interruption. This helps protect their health and safety.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to hazardous agents during recovery is peripheral to the cleaning outcome but severity high for personnel chemical injury.
rows.sat_rat: PPE and procedures mitigate exposure, but recovery interventions are variable and error-prone. Exposure risk during recovery is a safety concern, not a delay. Avoiding exposure depends heavily on operator PPE discipline and procedural judgement during non-routine recovery. Exposure incidents carry injury and downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which pass-or-fail thresholds disregard microbial recovery limitations
rows.plain: Make sure pass-or-fail limits account for the fact that tests may not find every germ that is present. This helps avoid treating an uncertain result as proof that everything is safe.
rows.imp_rat: Pass/fail thresholds disregarding microbial recovery limitations can falsely pass contaminated equipment; moderately relevant to valid criteria and severe for undetected bioburden.
rows.sat_rat: Many programs still set limits without formally accounting for recovery efficiency, a common gap. Incorporating recovery limits into thresholds adds analysis time only. Translating recovery limitations into pass/fail logic needs statistical and micro expertise. Thresholds ignoring recovery let failures pass, with real downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between actual cleaning-phase exposure and specified cleaning-phase exposure
rows.plain: Keep the product exposed to the cleaning conditions for the amount of time and intensity that was specified. This helps ensure the cleaning process is neither too weak nor too strong.
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between actual and specified cleaning-phase exposure directly affects whether cleaning is achieved; high severity because under-exposure leaves contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-driven phase timers deliver specified exposure consistently, making this well served on automated systems. Exposure time is timer-driven within the recipe, so it is not a delay concern. Automated phase timing removes operator dependence for exposure control. Correct exposure avoids reruns and wasted agent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical cleaning parameters configured outside approved limits
rows.plain: Set all important cleaning controls within their approved limits. This helps the cleaning process work as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Critical parameters configured outside approved limits sets up the cycle to run wrong; central to correct execution and severe because misconfiguration causes systematic cleaning failure.
rows.sat_rat: Change control and range checks catch most misconfigurations, though undetected ones do occur. Configuration review is a front-loaded activity not affecting cycle time. Correct parameter configuration relies on engineering and validation review. Misconfigured parameters are cheap to fix in review, costly in production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incompatible recovered solutions are commingled during capture or return
rows.plain: Keep different recovered liquids that should not be mixed separate when they are collected or returned. This helps prevent unwanted reactions or makes the recovered liquids unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Commingling incompatible recovered solutions is moderately central to media recovery operations, but severity is high because incompatible chemical mixing can generate hazardous reactions and render reused media unsafe.
rows.sat_rat: Where recovery/reuse exists, segregation controls are inconsistent and commingling errors still occur on manually valved return systems. Segregated capture routing usually diverts solutions promptly without added delay. Correct routing of incompatible recovered streams depends on operator understanding of the recovery scheme. Commingling incompatible solutions ruins recoverable media and creates disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a critical CIP residue or bioburden risk is ranked below a less consequential concern
rows.plain: Make sure serious cleaning residue or harmful germ risks are not treated as less important than minor concerns. This helps direct attention to the risks that could cause the most harm.
rows.imp_rat: Ranking a critical residue/bioburden risk below a lesser one misdirects controls; moderately relevant to prioritization and severe because the top risk may go unaddressed.
rows.sat_rat: Subjective, judgement-driven risk ranking produces inconsistent prioritization, so critical risks are sometimes ranked too low. Risk-ranking exercises are periodic and not time-critical, so timing is rarely the limiting factor. Correctly ranking a critical residue risk above a lesser one depends heavily on scarce expert judgement and consistent risk criteria. Risk assessment consumes expert time but little material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in acceptance criteria for repeatable cleaning and sanitizing outcomes
rows.plain: Make the rules for judging whether cleaning and germ-killing are successful clear, so the same good result can be achieved every time.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous acceptance criteria impair consistent pass/fail judgments; moderately central to repeatable outcomes and severity high because ambiguity can let unclean equipment be released.
rows.sat_rat: Health-based exposure limits and guidance improve consistency, but criteria for visual/micro/edge cases remain interpretive and vary by site. Ambiguous acceptance criteria trigger investigations and rework that consume significant time. Setting defensible, unambiguous cleaning limits still relies heavily on scarce validation and regulatory expertise interpreting guidance case by case. Disputes and re-derivation of limits absorb expert labor and delay cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the paused cycle resumes before the temporary intervention is complete
rows.plain: Make sure the paused machine does not start again until the temporary work is finished. This helps prevent injury, damage, or an incomplete repair.
rows.imp_rat: Resuming before a temporary intervention is complete is moderately central to interruption handling, and premature resume can trap personnel hazards or leave the system in an unsafe configuration during restart.
rows.sat_rat: Permit-to-work and lockout practices help but hand-off gaps mean premature resume remains a real, recurring risk. Verifying intervention completion before resume adds procedural delay. Coordinating that a temporary maintenance intervention is finished before resume relies on manual communication and vigilance. Resuming over an incomplete intervention risks equipment damage and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cleaning-agent concentration deviation from specified limits during circulation
rows.plain: Keep the cleaning liquid at the required strength while it moves through the system. This helps the cleaning work properly.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning-agent concentration deviation directly reduces cleaning efficacy central to the job; severity high since under-concentration leaves residue and over leaves chemical.
rows.sat_rat: Inline conductivity control keeps concentration within limits reliably on modern systems, a mature and well-served capability. Concentration is controlled continuously so it does not add delay. Automated dosing and conductivity feedback reduce the skill needed to hold concentration. Tight concentration control avoids over-dosing chemical waste and under-dosing reruns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of critical CIP parameter excursions outside validated ranges
rows.plain: Have fewer times when important cleaning settings fall outside the tested and approved limits. This helps ensure the equipment is cleaned properly every time.
rows.imp_rat: Critical parameter excursions outside validated ranges directly threaten the assured cleaning outcome; high severity as excursions can leave contamination and void validation.
rows.sat_rat: Automated CIP control with alarms and interlocks makes parameter excursions uncommon and quickly caught. Modern CIP skids monitor parameters in real time, flagging excursions immediately during the cycle. Automated control loops reduce reliance on operator vigilance to keep parameters in range. Automation adds capital cost but sharply reduces the expense of out-of-range failures.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cleaning-agent temperature departure from the specified condition during a cycle
rows.plain: Keep the cleaning liquid at the specified temperature throughout the cycle. This helps produce the same cleaning result each time.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature departure during a cycle directly weakens the cleaning outcome central to the job; high severity as thermal shortfall leaves residue/bioburden.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop temperature control with logged RTDs is among the most robust and consistently met CIP parameters. Temperature control via heat exchangers and RTDs is real-time and adds no cycle delay. PID temperature loops are standard and demand minimal specialized skill to maintain. Energy for heating is a routine cost that automated control keeps efficient.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approved cleaning or sanitizing agent supply is insufficient at cycle initiation
rows.plain: Make sure enough approved cleaning or sanitizing liquid is available before the cleaning run begins. A shortage could stop the run or leave the equipment inadequately cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: Insufficient agent supply at initiation compromises the cleaning inputs; moderately central and severe because a short supply can leave a cycle unable to clean fully.
rows.sat_rat: Level indications and pre-checks make supply shortfalls uncommon, so this is fairly well served. Checking agent supply is quick and prevents mid-cycle delays. Verifying sufficient supply is a routine check needing little expertise. Insufficient supply wastes a partial cycle and forces rerun cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an incorrect recipe revision is assigned to the target CIP circuit
rows.plain: Make sure the correct approved set of cleaning instructions is assigned to the intended equipment path. Using the wrong version could leave the equipment improperly cleaned.
rows.imp_rat: An incorrect recipe revision assigned to the circuit runs an unvalidated/wrong cycle; central to correct execution and severe because it can systematically fail cleaning.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic change control reliably links approved revisions to circuits, keeping mis-assignment rare. Recipe-revision assignment is quick within managed systems. Version control reduces operator judgement about revisions. Wrong-revision execution wastes cycles and triggers investigations.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of viable-microorganism under-recovery caused by residual sanitizing agent in verification samples
rows.plain: Prevent leftover sanitizing liquid from hiding living germs in test samples. Otherwise, a test might wrongly suggest that the area is free of germs.
rows.imp_rat: Under-recovery from residual sanitizing agent in samples understates viable microorganisms; moderately central to accurate verification and severe because it can falsely pass contaminated equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Neutralizer validation is a mature, expected practice that reliably addresses residual-agent carryover in most labs. Neutralization steps add minor time, not the main loss. Selecting and validating neutralizers requires specific microbiological know-how. Failure here masks survivors and risks costly releases of contaminated equipment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous cleaning agents, hot fluids, or steam during alarm response
rows.plain: Keep the operator from touching dangerous cleaning chemicals, hot liquids, or steam while responding to an alarm. This helps prevent burns, poisoning, or other injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to caustic agents, hot fluids, or steam during alarm response is only occasionally on the critical path of getting the cleaning job done, but when it occurs the consequence is serious injury, making severity very high.
rows.sat_rat: Established LOTO and PPE practice makes exposure during alarm response uncommon but not eliminated when operators intervene under pressure. PPE donning and lockout before approaching an alarming unit adds delay to the response. Safe alarm response depends heavily on trained procedure adherence and hazard awareness rather than engineered protection. PPE and safety infrastructure are modest recurring costs against the severity of a burn or chemical exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hot, pressurized, or chemically hazardous media during manual operation
rows.plain: Keep the technician or operator from coming into contact with hot, pressurized, or chemically dangerous materials while controlling the machine by hand. This helps prevent burns, other injuries, and harmful exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hot, pressurized, or chemically hazardous media is peripheral to whether cleaning/sterilization succeeds (moderate relevance) but carries catastrophic potential for burns or chemical injury when it occurs (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: During manual mode many interlocks are defeated, so exposure protection depends on procedure and is less reliable than automatic operation. Manual operations require depressurization and cooldown steps that add time before safe handling. Manual intervention inherently demands skilled, hazard-aware technicians since automation is bypassed. Safety gear for manual work is cheap versus the cost of an exposure incident.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the cycle is resumed from an operating state other than the approved state
rows.plain: Make sure an interrupted production run can restart only from the correct, approved condition. This helps prevent the process from resuming in an unsafe or incorrect state.
rows.imp_rat: Resuming a cycle from an unapproved operating state is fairly central to process integrity after interruptions, and doing so can invalidate the entire cleaning/sterilization result, causing releases of inadequately treated equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe state logic prevents many bad resumes, but manual restarts and override paths still allow resumption from an unapproved state. State-checking interlocks add little delay when resuming a paused cycle. Automated state verification reduces operator judgement about the correct resume state. Resuming from a wrong state can force a full re-run with associated waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of relevant fixed-equipment configurations and piping paths omitted from repeatability evaluation
rows.plain: Leave out fewer relevant equipment setups and pipe routes when checking whether the process gives the same results each time. This makes the repeatability check more complete.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting relevant configurations/paths from repeatability evaluation weakens coverage of the proof; moderately central and severity high because untested paths may not clean reliably.
rows.sat_rat: Bracketing/worst-case approaches are standard, but complex multi-product piping means some configurations are routinely omitted, undermining repeatability coverage. Enumerating all configurations and worst-case paths extends validation planning timelines. Worst-case grouping and matrixing of piping paths is a judgement-heavy exercise dependent on expert bracketing decisions. Missed configurations surface later as unplanned revalidation and investigation costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an adjustment drives pressure, temperature, flow, or cleaning-agent concentration beyond safe line limits
rows.plain: Make adjustments without pushing pressure, temperature, liquid movement, or cleaning-agent strength beyond safe limits. This helps prevent unsafe conditions while the process is running.
rows.imp_rat: An adjustment driving parameters beyond safe line limits risks equipment damage or rupture; less central to routine cleaning but severity high due to safety and asset harm.
rows.sat_rat: High/low limit interlocks reliably bound adjustments in most systems, though manual override paths can bypass them. Safety limit checks are instantaneous and do not add operator time. Configured safe-limit interlocks reduce reliance on operator caution during adjustments. Preventing unsafe excursions avoids equipment damage and lost cycles.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the water supply or process circuit during filtration element replacement
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of putting dirt or germs into the water or working fluid while changing the filter parts. This helps protect the supply and the process from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Introducing contamination during filtration element replacement affects the water supply/process circuit, an important but upstream integrity point (relevance 7). A compromised filter change can seed the circuit with contaminants, meaningfully impacting downstream sterility (severity 7).
rows.sat_rat: Post-change integrity testing and flushing give good assurance, though improper installation still occasionally introduces contamination. Filter element changes are routine and quick with sanitary connections, though flushing afterward adds some time. Sterile filter change-out with integrity testing follows well-defined steps but still needs careful aseptic handling. Filter cartridges and integrity-test consumables are a recurring but predictable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of cleaning-agent temperature deviation from specified limits during circulation
rows.plain: Keep the cleaning liquid close to its required temperature while it moves through the equipment. This helps the cleaning process work as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Temperature deviation directly impairs cleaning-agent efficacy central to residue/bioburden removal; severity high because cold cycles fail to clean.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop temperature control with RTDs holds temperature within limits very reliably on validated CIP systems. Heating to setpoint adds cycle time but is scheduled, not a deviation issue. Automated temperature control removes most operator finesse from holding temperature. Temperature control consumes energy but prevents costly failed cleans.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unsafe pressure buildup from a blocked flow path during valve sequencing
rows.plain: Prevent dangerous pressure from building up if a passage is blocked while valves are opened or closed in sequence. This protects the equipment and nearby people.
rows.imp_rat: Unsafe pressure buildup from a blocked path during valve sequencing is partly a process-control issue (moderate relevance) but can cause rupture and serious injury (high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Valve-sequence interlocks plus mechanical relief devices make unsafe blocked-path buildup uncommon, though sequencing logic errors remain possible. Pressure relief acts instantly without operator response time being a factor. Engineered relief devices remove reliance on operator vigilance for blocked-path pressure. Relief valves and rupture disks are inexpensive insurance against catastrophic damage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous pressure, heat, or cleaning chemicals during the phase transition
rows.plain: Protect the operator from dangerous pressure, heat, or cleaning chemicals while moving the machine to the next step. The change should only happen when it is safe to do so.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to hazardous pressure, heat, or chemicals during phase transitions is peripheral to completing the clean itself but carries severe injury potential when it happens, driving severity high while relevance stays moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Automated valve sequencing and pressure relief make exposure at transitions uncommon under normal operation. Interlocked transitions occur automatically without operators needing to be present, saving intervention time. Phase transitions are largely automated, reducing the manual finesse needed to stay clear of released heat or chemicals. Engineered interlocks add design cost but avoid injury and rework expense during transitions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of cleaning cycle interruptions caused by loss of required utilities
rows.plain: Reduce how often a cleaning cycle stops because electricity, air, water, or another needed service is lost. This helps cleaning finish on time and avoids repeated work.
rows.imp_rat: Utility-loss interruptions halt cycles and can truncate cleaning; moderately central and severity high because aborted cycles may leave partly cleaned equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Utility supply is generally stable but occasional steam, water, or air losses still interrupt cycles, an incompletely solved dependency. Utility loss halts the cycle causing delay, but the root need is reliability. Little operator skill can prevent utility failures from upstream systems. Utility-driven interruptions waste consumed agents and require reruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the condition that triggered the cycle alarm
rows.plain: Correctly identify what caused the cycle warning before taking action. This helps the worker respond to the real problem instead of making the situation worse.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the condition that triggered a cycle alarm can lead to the wrong corrective action and an undetected clean/sterilization failure; both relevance to safe operation and severity of a mis-handled deviation are high.
rows.sat_rat: Standard HMIs timestamp and describe alarms, yet coincident or vague alarms routinely make the real trigger ambiguous. Alarm banners appear instantly, but diagnosing the true triggering condition can still consume time. Cryptic or generic alarm text forces operators to lean on experienced technicians to interpret what actually tripped. Misreading an alarm can trigger unnecessary aborts and wasted chemical/energy, a recurring but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of technician exposure to hazardous pressure, chemicals, or unexpected component movement during repair
rows.plain: Protect the technician from dangerous pressure, chemicals, or unexpected movement while fixing pump and valve problems.
rows.imp_rat: Technician exposure to hazardous pressure, chemicals, or component movement during repair is a maintenance-safety error somewhat tangential to the cleaning outcome itself, but severity is maximal given potential for serious injury or death.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and stored-energy procedures make serious technician exposure uncommon, though lapses still cause incidents. Lockout-tagout and depressurization add setup time before repair can begin. Safe isolation requires trained technicians following LOTO and energy-control procedures. PPE, LOTO hardware, and training carry cost that is standard EHS spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that retained cleaning material contacts personnel during post-cycle access
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that leftover cleaning material touches workers when they enter after cleaning is finished. This helps protect workers from exposure.
rows.imp_rat: Retained cleaning material contacting personnel during post-cycle access is peripheral to the cleaning result but severity high due to chemical burn/exposure risk.
rows.sat_rat: Drain and rinse verification before access reduces exposure, but retained pockets still surprise operators occasionally. Retained-material contact is a safety issue, not primarily time. Safe post-cycle access relies on operator adherence to drain/verify steps. Chemical exposure causes injury cost and lost time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement between reported measurements and actual cleaning conditions during critical recipe phases
rows.plain: Make the recorded measurements agree more closely with the actual cleaning conditions during the most important parts of the cleaning process. This helps people make decisions using trustworthy information.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement between reported and actual conditions during critical phases misrepresents cleaning; moderately central and severe because it can mask a failing critical phase.
rows.sat_rat: Instrument accuracy is generally good, but during dynamic critical phases reported values can lag or diverge from reality. Discrepancy detection during critical phases can require additional cross-checking time. Recognizing that reported values misrepresent conditions requires experience. Acting on wrong readings risks a failed clean and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that incompatible cleaning materials react and release hazardous vapor during a CIP cycle
rows.plain: Prevent cleaning materials that do not work safely together from reacting and producing dangerous fumes during cleaning. This protects workers from breathing in harmful vapors.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible cleaning materials reacting to release hazardous vapor is peripheral to the cleaning outcome but catastrophic for personnel safety and facility harm.
rows.sat_rat: Segregated dosing and sequencing controls make incompatible mixing rare on designed systems. Incompatible-agent reactions are not a timing issue. Avoiding incompatible mixing requires chemical knowledge and procedural discipline. A hazardous reaction risks injury and expensive incident response.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that configuring the target cleaning sequence alters another sequence or shared control
rows.plain: Make sure changing the settings for one approved cleaning routine does not change another routine or a control shared by both. This prevents an unintended change to other cleaning work.
rows.imp_rat: Configuring one sequence altering another/shared control can corrupt unrelated cleaning cycles; moderately central and severity high due to unexpected failures across circuits.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration management catches most cross-impacts, but shared-control interactions still occasionally cause unintended changes. Verifying no cross-impact on shared control adds review time. Understanding shared-resource dependencies requires deep system knowledge. Unintended changes to other sequences can cause costly failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable residue and bioburden expectations omitted from the cleaning evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning review does not leave out any relevant expectations about leftover material or contamination. This helps ensure cleaning meets all applicable requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting applicable residue/bioburden expectations from evaluation leaves gaps in the cleaning proof; moderately central and severity high because unassessed conditions may fail undetected.
rows.sat_rat: Established regulatory frameworks and checklists cover most expectations, but evolving guidance means omissions still occur and are hard to catch. Compiling the full set of applicable expectations takes time but is a routine planning activity. Knowing which residue and bioburden expectations apply demands current regulatory and technical knowledge that not all reviewers possess. Missed expectations surfacing late drive costly rework of the evaluation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to released hot or caustic process media during the abort
rows.plain: Protect workers from hot or harmful process liquids that may be released when a cycle is stopped early.
rows.imp_rat: Release of hot or caustic media during an abort is an edge-case relative to routine cleaning completion, yet exposure causes serious burns or chemical injury, keeping severity very high and relevance moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Aborts are lower-frequency and less validated than normal sequences, leaving a real chance of uncontrolled media release. An abort dumps energized media quickly, giving little time buffer for safe operator positioning. Safe abort handling depends on the operator understanding where hot caustic media will be released. Controlled drain routing is inexpensive but abort waste of chemicals adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which displayed parameter values differ from actual cycle conditions
rows.plain: The values shown on the controls should match the actual conditions inside the process. This helps workers know what is really happening and keep the process on track.
rows.imp_rat: Displayed values differing from actual conditions mislead operators; moderately central to control and severe because false displays can hide a failing cycle.
rows.sat_rat: Calibrated instruments and HMI generally show accurate values, but sensor drift or lag can cause discrepancies. Displayed values update in near-real-time on the HMI. Reading displays needs little skill, though spotting a faulty reading takes attention. Display errors themselves cost little unless they drive bad decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous pressure, temperature, or cleaning agents while the active cycle is paused
rows.plain: Keep workers safe from dangerous pressure, heat, or cleaning chemicals while the running cycle is temporarily stopped. Pausing the equipment should not expose them to these hazards.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure during a paused cycle is a relatively narrow situation for the cleaning job, but the consequence of contact with hot caustic media or pressure is severe personal harm, so severity dominates.
rows.sat_rat: Paused states are less well-guarded than transitions, so residual hazardous energy exposure remains a recurring concern. A paused cycle still holds hot pressurized media, and safe depressurization before access takes time. Whether a paused system is safe to approach relies on operator knowledge of the held state rather than clear engineered signals. Safety provisions for paused-state access are inexpensive relative to exposure consequences.

rows.source_job: Replace worn seals and valve seats
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a gasket, O-ring, or valve seat incompatible with its fluid-path location
rows.plain: Use only seals and valve parts that are suitable for the particular place where liquid flows. The wrong part could leak, fail, or affect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Installing a gasket or seat incompatible with its fluid-path location is a maintenance selection error (moderate relevance) that can cause leaks or dead-leg contamination affecting cleaning (moderately high severity).
rows.sat_rat: Spare-parts kits and BOMs reduce mismatches, yet incompatible seals in the wrong location remain a recurring maintenance error. Fetching and fitting the correct gasket or O-ring is fast when parts are properly kitted. Matching elastomer material to fluid-path location requires specific knowledge that is a common source of human error. A wrong O-ring is inexpensive itself but degradation or failure can cause costly downtime or contamination.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of credible residue-carryover or microbial-contamination pathways omitted from the assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the assessment does not leave out any realistic way that leftover material or germs could carry over. This gives decision-makers a more complete view of contamination risks.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting credible carryover/contamination pathways from assessment leaves risks uncontrolled; moderately relevant to risk coverage and severe because missed pathways cause contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control strategies and flow diagrams cover major pathways, but less obvious microbial routes are frequently missed. Tracing all carryover pathways is time-intensive during assessment. Identifying credible contamination pathways demands specialized contamination-control expertise. Undetected pathways emerging in production are costly to remediate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a pressure surge during the abort damages piping, vessels, or seals
rows.plain: Prevent a sudden rise in pressure while stopping the process from damaging pipes, containers, or seals. This helps stop equipment damage and possible leaks.
rows.imp_rat: A pressure surge during abort damaging piping, vessels, or seals is moderately central since aborts are an edge case, but severity is high because compromised pressure boundaries can cause equipment failure and leaks of hazardous cleaning media.
rows.sat_rat: Relief valves and staged abort routines make surge damage uncommon, though older manual valving can still produce water hammer. Controlled abort sequences ramp down pressure over programmed time to avoid transient surges. Automated abort logic handles depressurization without requiring operator finesse. Preventing surge damage avoids expensive repairs to piping, vessels and seals.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of ranking CIP sterility-assurance risks using incomplete or inaccurate residue, bioburden, deviation, or cycle evidence
rows.plain: Reduce the chance of deciding which cleaning risks matter most using missing or incorrect information about leftover material, germs, problems, or cleaning cycles. Good information helps the supervisor focus attention where it is most needed.
rows.imp_rat: Ranking sterility-assurance risks on incomplete/inaccurate evidence misdirects controls; moderately relevant to prioritization and severity high because it can leave key risks unmitigated.
rows.sat_rat: Fragmented evidence across disconnected systems frequently leaves risk rankings based on incomplete or inaccurate inputs. Assembling complete evidence before ranking can delay the assessment when data is dispersed across systems. Judging evidence sufficiency and integrating deviation and cycle data requires experienced quality professionals. Data-gathering labor is the main cost, not material resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of drained chemical released into the surrounding sterile manufacturing area
rows.plain: Limit the amount of leftover cleaning chemical released into the nearby clean production area when it is drained. This helps protect people, equipment, and the surrounding area.
rows.imp_rat: Drained chemical released into the surrounding sterile area is a containment issue somewhat aside from the clean itself, but severity is meaningful because it can contaminate the sterile environment and harm personnel.
rows.sat_rat: Aging valves, corroded drain points and undocumented residual pockets make uncontrolled release during disposal a recurring surprise with few standardized safeguards. Draining and neutralizing residual chemicals adds hours to decommissioning but is not the primary loss when a release contaminates the sterile area. Safe draining into containment relies on experienced decommissioning crews who know the system's low points and dead legs, which are scarce at end-of-life. Spill cleanup and re-qualification of a contaminated sterile zone are expensive, though the deeper harm is the breach itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leakage from the cleaning path under planned cycle conditions
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that liquid or cleaning material leaks from the cleaning route during the planned cleaning cycle. This helps the cleaning process work safely and as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Leakage from the cleaning path under planned conditions risks lost pressure/coverage and personnel/chemical exposure; moderately central to CFJ, severity high due to safety and process impact.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitary fittings and gasket programs make leaks under normal conditions uncommon but not rare. Leak-driven stops add unplanned delay. Leak prevention depends on seal maintenance rather than operator skill. Leaks waste agent and require cleanup and repair cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material residual residue or bioburden risks omitted from the recommendation
rows.plain: Make sure the recommendation considers all important risks from leftover materials and harmful microorganisms. This helps avoid choosing an option that could leave the product unsafe or unsuitable.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting material residual risks from the recommendation leaves real contamination pathways uncontrolled; moderately relevant and severe because missed risks manifest as contamination.
rows.sat_rat: Structured risk assessments capture the obvious risks, but subtle residual ones are still omitted more often than the role would like. Risk identification adds time but is folded into standard risk assessment cycles. Recognizing material residual risks needs seasoned cross-functional judgement that is unevenly available. Unflagged risks that materialize later carry high remediation and compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loss of containment at cleaning-cycle startup
rows.plain: Prevent the sealed cleaning process from losing its protective containment when it starts. This helps keep cleaning chemicals and contaminants from escaping or entering unexpectedly.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of containment at startup risks chemical release and personnel harm; peripheral to the cleaning outcome itself but severity high given hazardous-agent exposure.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled startup sequences and pressure ramps make startup containment loss uncommon. Startup containment loss causes stoppage and delay. Startup ramping is automated, reducing skill dependence. A startup breach spills agent and requires cleanup.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of circulation flow outside its target range during the cycle
