Decommissioning is planned over an extended window that allows staged hazard control. Relies on specialized decommissioning contractors whose expertise varies by site. Environmental controls, containment and disposal are significant cost drivers.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of an unexpected release of stored energy or pressure from the system during decommissioning
rows.imp_rat: Uncontrolled stored-energy release during decommissioning is dangerous, yet decommissioning is a one-time end-of-life task far from the core cleaning job; ranking caps it at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature LOTO and energy-isolation practice makes stored-energy release during decommissioning a well-controlled, infrequent event. Verifying stored energy is fully released adds steps but time is not the primary stake. Recognizing and isolating stored pressure and energy sources requires qualified decommissioning personnel. An uncontrolled release is expensive in damage and injury, but safety dominates the judgement.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of hazardous chemical release during residue removal or transfer
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous chemical release during residue removal is a real safety hazard (nontrivial severity), yet as a handling task peripheral to the cleaning job and ranked below the prior batch it is capped at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Hazmat handling procedures and PPE are mature, so releases during removal are moderately well controlled where crews are qualified. Controlled residue transfer takes longer than dumping but time is not the core stake. Safe hazardous-chemical handling during removal depends on trained hazmat-competent personnel. A release triggers costly cleanup and regulatory exposure, secondary to worker safety.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incompatible chemical residues mixing during disposal handling
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible residues mixing during disposal handling risks a reaction, but disposal is peripheral to the CFJ; ranking caps it at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation and compatibility rules exist but depend on accurate residue identification, so mixing incidents still occur during disposal handling. Segregating incompatible residues for disposal adds handling time but that is not the primary loss. Recognizing chemical incompatibilities during disposal handling requires chemical-safety knowledge unevenly present. A reaction event is costly, though hazard is the dominant concern.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of erasing records subject to retention requirements when purging the control system
rows.imp_rat: Erasing retention-required records during control-system purge is a compliance defect, but it occurs only during decommissioning and is far from the CFJ; capped at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Control-system purges frequently sweep up retention-subject records because holds and classifications are poorly maintained at end-of-life. Screening records before purge slows decommissioning but erasure of protected records is the real harm. Distinguishing retention-obligated records from purgeable data requires records-management judgement often absent from IT purge crews. Wrongful erasure risks fines far exceeding storage savings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel being exposed to hazardous residues while handling recoverable components
rows.imp_rat: Personnel exposure to hazardous residues while handling recoverable components is a safety issue giving some severity, yet its relevance to executing CIP/SIP cleaning is marginal and it sits low in the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-decon flushing helps, but recoverable parts frequently retain trapped residue and exposure during handling is a recurring gap. Handling recoverable components adds disassembly time as each part must be verified clean before removal. Depends heavily on technician knowledge of where residues hide in valves, pumps and gaskets, which is uneven across crews. Recovery of components offsets cost, but added decon steps and PPE add expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel exposure to biological or chemical hazards during decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Exposure to biological/chemical hazards during decontamination carries safety severity, but decontamination-phase personnel risk is tangential to the core cleaning job and the ceiling constrains it.
rows.sat_rat: Established decon protocols and monitoring substantially reduce biological/chemical exposure, though verification of full decon is imperfect. Decontamination cycles are time-boxed by validated routines, so scheduling is manageable. Requires trained hazmat/decon personnel and correct PPE selection, a real skill dependency. Decon chemicals, PPE and disposal add recurring cost that is budgeted for.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of archived system records becoming unreadable before retention obligations expire
rows.imp_rat: Archived records becoming unreadable before retention expiry is a compliance/records issue distant from the cleaning job; capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Media degradation and obsolete formats routinely render archived records unreadable before retention periods expire, with weak safeguards. Migrating or refreshing archives is a slow chore, but readability loss is the real failure, not the delay. Preserving readable records over decades needs data-preservation expertise rarely present in disposal teams. Long-term storage and format migration cost money that is often underfunded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of cleaning and sterilization unavailability during operational handover
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning/sterilization unavailability during operational handover is a transition-window delay somewhat relevant to availability but ranked low here; the outage is temporary and planned, so severity stays minimal under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Handover overruns still happen when hidden dependencies surface, but planned transition procedures are reasonably mature. Minimizing downtime during handover is fundamentally about duration, and scheduled changeover practices do compress it though gaps remain. Coordinated handover relies on planners who sequence work, a moderately available skill. Extended unavailability of CIP/SIP idles production, a cost that motivates tight scheduling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect process or utility connections during the transition
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect process/utility connections during transition carry contamination risk, giving moderate relevance, but as a lower-ranked item its scored severity is held down by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling and P&ID references help, but undocumented or aged connections still lead to occasional wrong hookups during transition. Verifying connection mapping during transition adds time but misconnection is the defining error. Correctly reconnecting process and utility lines needs technicians familiar with the system's schematics. A wrong connection can damage equipment or product, a cost secondary to the deviation itself.

rows.source_job: Migrate system configurations to replacement equipment
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity about which validated configuration version is approved for migration
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity over which validated configuration version is approved for migration is a documentation/handover concern peripheral to running cycles (low relevance); a wrong version could cause rework, a bounded severity under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration-version ambiguity is a chronic, poorly controlled problem at end-of-life, with stale or conflicting records leaving real uncertainty about what is approved. Tracing which validated configuration is the approved one wastes time hunting through revision logs but the delay is secondary. Correctly identifying the approved validated version demands validation-engineer expertise that is often gone by the disposal stage. Misidentifying the version is cheap to store but costly if the wrong config migrates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of residual hazardous contamination being omitted from the final condition record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting residual hazardous contamination from the final condition record can lead to exposure risk downstream, giving it some severity, but it is a decommissioning documentation error rather than a core operational one, so the ceiling caps it at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Final condition records frequently omit residual hazards because verification of complete cleaning is inconsistent. Final condition recording competes with teardown deadlines and is often compressed. Judging and documenting residual contamination correctly needs experienced assessors. Recording itself is cheap, but an omitted hazard notation creates large downstream liability cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of error in the documented remaining service life of critical components at retirement
rows.imp_rat: Error in documented remaining service life at retirement is a decommissioning-records defect peripheral to operational cleaning (low relevance); an inaccurate figure has limited downstream impact, capped by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented remaining-life figures are frequently incomplete or extrapolated, so the recorded value is often inaccurate at retirement. Reconstructing remaining service life at retirement is a slow forensic exercise, though duration is not the main harm. Estimating remaining life of critical components requires engineering judgement and maintenance-history literacy that is thin at disposal. Errors here mainly affect resale/salvage valuation rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage to system surfaces or components during marking removal
rows.imp_rat: Surface damage during marking removal is a retirement/marking-stage error peripheral to operational cleaning (low relevance); damaged surfaces have limited, localized consequences, capped by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Outcomes vary with who does the work, but established mechanical/solvent techniques keep damage within tolerable bounds most of the time. Careful marking removal takes longer than aggressive methods but time is not what is primarily lost. Avoiding surface damage while removing marks hinges on a technician's finesse and material knowledge, making this fundamentally a skill-dependent task. Damaged components lose salvage value, a real but secondary cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of label residue or removal debris left on cleanroom-facing or product-contact surfaces
rows.imp_rat: Label residue on product-contact surfaces touches contamination control (modest relevance) but is a rare, easily remediated debris issue with limited severity, kept at or below the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no mature standard for verifying a surface is truly residue-free at disposal, so debris regularly lingers on cleanroom-facing surfaces. Scraping and solvent-wiping label residue off product-contact surfaces is slow manual work but delay is not the core failure. Judging which cleaning agents won't damage passivated surfaces requires trained technicians, and that judgement is inconsistently available at disposal. Consumables and labor for residue removal are modest costs compared with the consequence of leftover debris.

rows.source_job: Notify regulatory bodies of system retirement
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a retirement filing being rejected because required system or validation details are incomplete or inaccurate
rows.imp_rat: A retirement filing rejected for incomplete details is an end-of-life administrative defect peripheral to the operational cleaning job (low relevance); rejection causes refiling effort, a bounded compliance-admin severity held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete or inaccurate submissions are a common cause of rejection because records at disposal are frequently fragmented and unverified. Assembling a complete retirement filing is time-consuming but rejection, not delay, is the defining failure. Compiling accurate system and validation details for a filing needs regulatory and documentation expertise that is often stretched. A rejected filing triggers rework costs but the money is secondary to the compliance risk.

rows.source_job: Notify regulatory bodies of system retirement
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the system being retired from regulated service before required regulatory clearance is obtained
rows.imp_rat: Retiring the system from regulated service before regulatory clearance is a compliance-standing error with potential regulatory consequences, but it is an end-of-life administrative event unrelated to active cleaning performance, so proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Formal regulatory decommissioning processes make premature retirement uncommon, though occasional early disconnection happens under schedule pressure. Regulatory clearance has defined submission timelines that are generally planned into the project. Requires regulatory-affairs expertise to assemble and file clearance dossiers. Compliance filing and any remediation to obtain clearance carry known costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between execution evidence generation and entry in the cleaning cycle record
rows.plain: Record proof of the cleaning work in the cleaning record as soon as the work is completed. This keeps the record accurate and prevents missing details.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between evidence generation and record entry is a minor timing/administrative issue, trivial on both proxies for the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Automated real-time capture minimizes this lag where deployed; manual entry still lags. This is explicitly the lag between evidence generation and its record entry. Timely entry depends on operator promptness, not high skill. Late entry delays review and release, an opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in parameter labels, units, or entries within the cleaning cycle record
rows.plain: Make the names, measurement units, and entered values in the cleaning record easy to understand. This helps people record and review the cleaning cycle correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in parameter labels/units within records is a minor clerical clarity issue, trivial in both relevance and severity to the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized record templates reduce ambiguity, though hybrid records still contain unclear entries. Ambiguous labels/units are not a timing issue. Clear entries depend on documentation discipline and standards. Ambiguity causes misinterpretation and rework cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of cleaning cycle records lacking correct performer attribution
rows.plain: Reduce the share of cleaning records that do not correctly name the person who performed the cleaning. Accurate names make it clear who completed each task.
rows.imp_rat: Records lacking correct performer attribution is a minor data-integrity/administrative gap, trivial in relevance and severity to physically cleaning equipment.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic signatures enforce attribution well where deployed, though paper records still miss it. Missing performer attribution is not a timing issue. Correct attribution depends on login/signoff discipline. Attribution gaps cause compliance findings and rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when accounting for a cycle-record gap during an inspection
rows.plain: The worker wants to avoid being caught off guard if an inspector asks about a missing record from a cleaning cycle. This matters because the records should show clearly what happened during cleaning.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed accounting for a record gap is an emotional error remote from the CFJ (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting cycle details needed for contemporaneous recording
rows.plain: The worker wants the details of a work cycle quickly so they can record what happened while it is happening. This helps keep the record complete and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety duration while awaiting recording details is tangential to the CFJ of residue removal (low relevance) and its impact is momentary stress (low severity), constrained to 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when challenged to reconcile conflicting cycle details
rows.plain: Stay calm when someone asks you to explain why details about the work do not match. This helps you correct the record and preserve trust in what was done.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure reconciling cycle details is an emotional-state error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with transient impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when pressed for a cleaning acceptance or repeat-cleaning decision
rows.plain: Stay calm when asked to decide whether cleaning is good enough or must be repeated. This helps the worker make a clear choice based on what is known.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when pressed for an acceptance decision is an emotional-state error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with transient consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting information needed to confirm cleaning acceptance
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while waiting for information that confirms the equipment or area is clean enough to use. This matters because they need to know the cleaning was successful before moving on.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting information to confirm acceptance is an emotional/timing outcome, trivial in relevance and severity to the CFJ.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that repeat cleaning may be needed after cleanliness acceptance
rows.plain: Quickly know whether something really needs to be cleaned again after it has already been accepted as clean. This avoids unnecessary extra cleaning and delays.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about needing repeat cleaning is a psychological-uncertainty error remote from executing circulation (low relevance) with minor consequence (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when normal and abnormal cycle indications conflict
rows.plain: Stay calm when the signs from a normal cycle conflict with the signs showing an unusual problem. This helps the worker judge what is really happening and respond safely.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over conflicting cycle indications is an emotional-state error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with transient impact (low severity), capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting confirmation of a suspected CIP deviation
rows.plain: The worker wants quick confirmation about whether there is really a problem with the cleaning process. This helps them know what to do instead of waiting and worrying.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting confirmation of a suspected deviation is an emotional/timing outcome, trivial on both proxies for the CFJ.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of exposure felt when unable to explain a CIP deviation while others await direction
rows.plain: Avoid feeling exposed when a cleaning problem must be explained while other people are waiting for instructions. This helps the person give clear direction during the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Exposure felt when unable to explain a deviation is an emotional-state error remote from cleaning (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a CIP inspection finding is attributed to the operator's execution or recordkeeping before inspectors and site reviewers
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that inspectors and site reviewers blame the operator when they find a problem with the equipment-cleaning process. The operator needs clear steps and records showing what was done.
rows.imp_rat: Attributing an inspection finding to operator recordkeeping is a reputational error remote from circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which conflict between the CIP record and operator account erodes credibility with inspectors and Quality reviewers
rows.plain: Keep the written production record and the operator's account from contradicting each other. Consistent information protects trust with inspectors and the people responsible for product quality.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility erosion from record-vs-account conflict is a reputational error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the operator is bypassed in CIP deviation assessments because execution judgment is not trusted
rows.plain: The worker should be included more often when judging unusual problems during cleaning. This matters because their knowledge of how the work was actually done can lead to better decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed in deviation assessments over doubted judgment is a role error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator is blamed before disposition stakeholders for an unresolved CIP record discrepancy
rows.plain: The operator wants unresolved differences in the cleaning records to be reviewed by the people responsible for deciding whether the product can be released before the operator is blamed. This helps ensure responsibility is assigned fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Blaming the operator for a record discrepancy is a reputational error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that failed cleaning acceptance is attributed to the operator's execution by production and Quality leadership
rows.plain: Avoid having production and quality leaders blame the operator when cleaning does not pass the required check. This helps show whether the real problem was the equipment or the cleaning process.
rows.imp_rat: Attributing failed acceptance to operator execution is a blame/reputation error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with production and Quality leadership when repeat cleaning follows the operator's cycle
rows.plain: The person doing the work wants to avoid losing the trust of production and quality leaders when equipment has to be cleaned again after the operator’s cleaning process. This matters because repeat cleaning can delay work and raise doubts about the process.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when repeat cleaning follows a cycle is a reputational error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the operator is bypassed in CIP readiness decisions because cleaning judgment is doubted
rows.plain: The worker should be included more often when deciding whether equipment is ready for cleaning. This matters because their practical knowledge can help prevent missed dirt or incomplete cleaning.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed in readiness decisions over doubted judgment is a role error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the operator's first-pass cleaning successes are dismissed as exceptions by production and Quality leadership
rows.plain: Production and quality managers should recognize the worker’s successful cleaning results as reliable, rather than treating them as unusual exceptions. This matters because repeated success shows the cleaning process can work consistently.
rows.imp_rat: First-pass successes dismissed as exceptions is a reputational error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time waiting for authorized access to control records and relevant system states
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for permission to view the records and current conditions needed to find the cause of a control problem. This helps the problem be diagnosed and fixed sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for authorized access to control records is an administrative-access delay, trivial to the physical cleaning job on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access to historians and control states is reliably available, though occasional permission delays occur. Authorized access to records and states is generally granted quickly in networked systems. Access itself needs no special skill once permissions exist. Fast access avoids idle investigation time cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required readiness records containing missing or inconsistent entries
rows.plain: Make sure the records showing that a work line is ready contain complete and consistent information. This helps people know whether work can safely resume.
rows.imp_rat: Readiness records with missing/inconsistent entries is a minor pre-check documentation gap, trivial in relevance and severity to core cleaning.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic checklists improve completeness but missing or inconsistent readiness entries still occur. Reviewing readiness records for completeness adds time. Ensuring consistent readiness entries requires attentive record discipline. Inconsistent readiness records trigger costly documentation follow-up.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of misalignment between measurement timestamps and recipe-phase timestamps
rows.plain: Make sure measurement times match the correct stages of the production process. This helps people trust that each measurement belongs to the right step.
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between measurement and recipe-phase timestamps is a minor data-synchronization defect, trivial to the CFJ on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Synchronized clocks help, but measurement-to-phase timestamp misalignment still occurs across systems. Timestamp alignment issues add reconciliation time in review. Correcting timestamp misalignment requires data-handling skill. Misalignment complicates investigations, adding effort cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of uncertainty when sequence status conflicts with intervention records at line release
rows.plain: Know sooner why the recorded production step does not match the records of changes or interruptions when the line is released. This helps keep the records clear and trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty when sequence status conflicts with records is a reconciliation error peripheral to physical cleaning (low relevance) with procedural consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of feeling exposed when CIP intervention records do not support an immediate audit response
rows.plain: Spend less time feeling exposed when cleaning records do not immediately answer an inspector’s questions. This helps the worker respond clearly and show that cleaning was carried out properly.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when records don't support an audit response is an emotional error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when explaining the rationale or chronology of a CIP intervention
rows.plain: Stay calm when explaining why a cleaning step was taken and when it happened. This helps show that the response was thoughtful and properly recorded.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure explaining intervention rationale is an emotional error remote from circulating agents (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure while colleagues await a response to an abnormal line condition
rows.plain: Stay calm while coworkers wait for an answer about an unusual problem on the production line. This helps the team respond quickly without making a careless decision.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure while colleagues await a response is an emotional error unrelated to circulating agents (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of alarm while awaiting confirmation that an adjustment restored the expected line condition
rows.plain: Make people less worried while they wait to confirm that a change fixed the production line. This helps them stay calm until the result is known.
rows.imp_rat: Reducing alarm while awaiting confirmation is an emotional-state error peripheral to circulating cleaning agents (low relevance); the consequence is transient discomfort (low severity). Capped at 1.0 by batch ordering.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubting personal judgment when line conditions change during adjustment
rows.plain: Avoid making the worker doubt their own decision when conditions change during an adjustment. They need to recognize changes early and feel confident about how to respond.
rows.imp_rat: Doubting personal judgment during adjustment is an emotional error remote from the CFJ (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity), capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting confirmation that an adjustment restored cleaning parameters to range
rows.plain: The worker wants quick confirmation that a change brought the cleaning settings back into the safe, expected range. This helps them trust that the work can continue correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting parameter-restoration confirmation is an emotional-state error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with transient effect (low severity), capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality or audit stakeholders publicly attribute incomplete CIP records to the operator
rows.plain: Quality or audit reviewers should not publicly blame the operator if records showing that equipment was properly cleaned are incomplete. The operator needs a reliable way to document the work.
rows.imp_rat: Publicly attributing incomplete records to the operator is a reputational error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of operator credibility lost when CIP records conflict with executed recipe sequences
rows.plain: Make sure cleaning records agree with the recipe steps that were actually carried out. Otherwise, people may doubt whether the operator followed the required process.
rows.imp_rat: Operator credibility lost from record-sequence conflict is a reputational error tangential to cleaning execution (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholders blame the operator for undocumented CIP recipe adjustments
rows.plain: The operator wants to avoid being blamed if changes to the equipment-cleaning program are not written down. Clear records show what was changed and help prove that the cleaning was properly controlled.
rows.imp_rat: Blaming the operator for undocumented adjustments is a reputational error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of CIP record-review decisions excluding the operator because stakeholders doubt the operator's procedural judgment
rows.plain: The operator wants to be included when records are reviewed and decisions are made, rather than being left out because people doubt their ability to follow procedures. This matters because the operator has direct knowledge of how the work is performed.
rows.imp_rat: Excluding the operator from record-review decisions is a role error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that auditors question the operator's integrity when CIP entries appear retrospective
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that inspectors doubt the operator’s honesty because cleaning records appear to have been written after the work was done. This helps show that the records accurately reflect when the cleaning happened.
rows.imp_rat: Auditors questioning operator integrity over retrospective entries is a reputational error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that Quality Assurance attributes a failed line-clearance check to the operator's recipe execution
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that quality reviewers blame the worker’s instructions or actions when a check meant to confirm the previous product was removed fails. The cleaning steps should make it clear what the worker must do correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Attributing a failed line-clearance to operator execution is a blame/reputation error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which Quality Assurance reviewers second-guess the operator's equipment-release judgments
rows.plain: Quality reviewers should trust the operator’s judgment about whether equipment is clean and ready to use. This avoids unnecessary doubt about work the operator has completed.
rows.imp_rat: QA second-guessing equipment-release judgments is a reputational/role error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that shift leadership bypasses the operator when deciding CIP recipe adjustments
rows.plain: The operator wants to be included when shift leaders decide how to change the cleaning program for equipment. This helps ensure the changes are practical and lead to properly cleaned equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Shift leadership bypassing the operator on recipe adjustments is a role error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholders publicly blame a missed equipment-readiness commitment on the operator
rows.plain: The operator wants to avoid being publicly blamed if equipment is not ready when promised. Clear responsibilities and reliable preparation help protect the operator from unfair blame.
rows.imp_rat: Blaming a missed readiness commitment on the operator is a reputational error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which repeat cleaning cycles define the operator as unreliable in performance reviews
rows.plain: Prevent repeated cleaning attempts from making the worker look unreliable in performance reviews. Getting the cleaning right the first time helps show dependable work.
rows.imp_rat: Repeat cycles defining the operator as unreliable in reviews is a reputational error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from change initiation to approval of the cleaning-impact determination
rows.plain: Approval of the decision about how a process change affects cleaning should happen sooner after the change is started. This helps the change move forward without avoidable delay.
rows.imp_rat: Time from change initiation to impact-determination approval is a workflow-timing delay, trivial to the physical cleaning job.
rows.sat_rat: The approval outcome is usually sound; the recurring problem is how long it takes. Cleaning-impact determinations often queue behind other change-control reviews, extending initiation-to-approval time. Delays stem partly from scarce qualified reviewers, but the core loss is elapsed time. Slow approvals delay beneficial changes, an opportunity cost more than a direct expense.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a change with no effect on validated cleaning status is classified as requiring revalidation
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a change that does not affect the already proven cleaning process is treated as needing a full repeat of the earlier testing. This avoids unnecessary work while keeping the cleaning process reliable.
rows.imp_rat: Classifying a no-effect change as requiring revalidation is a rework/efficiency miss, trivial severity and peripheral to circulation.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-based approaches help, but defensive over-classification requiring needless revalidation remains common. Over-classifying changes as needing revalidation triggers unnecessary time-consuming studies. Avoiding conservative over-classification requires confident expert judgement to justify no-revalidation. Unnecessary revalidation wastes substantial validation resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of composure loss when accountable parties give conflicting explanations of CIP cleaning evidence
rows.plain: Reduce worry when responsible people give different explanations of evidence about the equipment’s cleaning. Clear records and an agreed account help show what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Composure loss over conflicting explanations is an emotional error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with transient consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when active CIP cycle indicators conflict before the sterile fill-finish team
rows.plain: Stay calm when the signs from an active cleaning cycle disagree with each other in front of the team that fills containers under germ-free conditions. This helps the team make a careful decision about contamination risk.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when cycle indicators conflict is an emotional error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with transient impact (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when pressed for a defensible cleaning-excursion root-cause position
rows.plain: Stay calm when asked to give a well-supported explanation for the main cause of a cleaning problem. This helps ensure that the explanation is honest and useful for preventing a repeat.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over a root-cause position is an emotional error peripheral to residue removal (low relevance) with transient impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty about the next investigative direction while the response team awaits guidance
rows.plain: The response team needs to quickly know what to investigate next while waiting for guidance. Clear direction helps them keep moving during a possible problem.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about next investigative direction is a decision-flow error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with transient consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of calm erosion under conflicting interpretations of cleaning-excursion evidence
rows.plain: Reduce the loss of peace of mind when people interpret evidence about an unusual cleaning event differently. A shared understanding of the evidence helps people decide what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Calm erosion under conflicting evidence is an emotional error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with transient impact (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulators or quality leadership attribute a formal CIP validation observation to the job overseer's evidence governance
rows.plain: The overseer wants to avoid being blamed if leaders find a problem with proof that the equipment-cleaning process was properly tested. Well-organized evidence helps show that the cleaning process was checked carefully.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a validation observation to the overseer's evidence governance is a reputational/administrative error, not core to circulating agents (low relevance); consequences are contained (low severity). Ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the job overseer is bypassed in consequential CIP validation decisions because evidence ownership is not trusted
rows.plain: The person overseeing the work wants to remain involved in important decisions about proving that the cleaning process works. They do not want to be passed over because others distrust who controls the evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed in validation decisions over evidence trust is a role/reputation error remote from physical cleaning (low relevance) with bounded consequences (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that site quality leadership attributes a sterile-product contamination finding to the job overseer's CIP governance
rows.plain: The person overseeing cleaning wants to avoid site quality leaders blaming them if a sterile product is found to be contaminated. Their cleaning decisions must help prevent contamination and be easy to justify.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a sterile-product contamination finding to overseer governance is reputational rather than core cleaning (low relevance); consequences bounded here (low severity), capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which production or engineering excludes the cleaning-validation overseer from CIP change decisions affecting validated state
rows.plain: Make sure the person responsible for proving equipment is properly cleaned is included when production or engineering changes how cleaning is done. This helps prevent changes from weakening safety checks that were already approved.
rows.imp_rat: Exclusion from CIP change decisions is a role/governance error peripheral to physical cleaning (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which quality leadership publicly reverses the cleaning-validation overseer's CIP disposition judgments as inconsistent
rows.plain: Quality managers should be less likely to publicly reverse the person's decisions about whether a cleaning process was acceptable. This helps keep cleaning decisions consistent and protects confidence that the equipment is safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Public reversal of disposition judgments is a reputational/governance error peripheral to physical cleaning (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of CIP quality-oversight effort allocated to low-consequence concerns
rows.plain: Spend less quality-checking time on issues that would have little effect and focus more on serious risks. This helps oversight resources protect product quality where they matter most.
rows.imp_rat: Oversight effort spent on low-consequence concerns is a resource-allocation waste, trivial to the physical cleaning job on both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Without dynamic prioritization, effort allocation stays misaligned with actual risk. Misallocated effort consumes time that could serve higher-risk needs. Directing effort to high-consequence concerns requires good risk-prioritization skill. Oversight effort is frequently spent on low-consequence concerns, wasting scarce quality resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when cleaning-assurance evidence conflicts during sign-off
rows.plain: Stay calm when proof that cleaning was successful does not agree during final approval. This helps you decide whether the area is safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when evidence conflicts at sign-off is an emotional error tangential to residue removal (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when a clear justification for the cleaning-assurance conclusion is unavailable under scrutiny
rows.plain: The supervisor wants to avoid being caught off guard if someone questions why the cleaning process was judged reliable. This matters because there should be a clear explanation showing how that conclusion was reached.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed without justification under scrutiny is an emotional error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when cleaning-assurance indicators support conflicting release conclusions
rows.plain: Stay calm when cleaning results point to different decisions about whether work can be released. This helps you make a careful, clear decision instead of reacting emotionally.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when indicators support conflicting conclusions is an emotional error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when an inspector challenges the basis for a cleaning-cycle decision
rows.plain: Stay calm when an inspector questions why you chose a particular cleaning cycle. This helps you explain the facts and reasoning behind that choice.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when an inspector challenges a decision basis is an emotional error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when conflicting explanations of a cleaning-cycle record cannot be reconciled
rows.plain: They want to avoid feeling exposed when different explanations of a cleaning record do not agree. They need one clear, believable account of what happened.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed over irreconcilable record explanations is an emotional error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with regulators when the overseer's CIP sterility-assurance evidence is challenged during an inspection
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with government reviewers when inspection questions the evidence that cleaning made equipment safe from germs. The records should clearly support the supervisor's assurance that the cleaning was effective.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when sterility-assurance evidence is challenged is a reputational error, not the cleaning act (low relevance), with contained impact (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of the overseer being bypassed in batch-disposition decisions because quality leadership doubts the overseer's judgment of CIP control evidence
rows.plain: The overseer wants quality leaders to trust their judgment when deciding whether a completed batch can move forward, based on evidence that the cleaning process worked. This would prevent others from taking over those decisions unnecessarily.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed in batch disposition over doubted judgment is a role/reputation error peripheral to residue removal (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that inspection findings are attributed by regulators or quality leadership to weak CIP governance by the overseer
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that regulators or quality leaders say inspection problems were caused by weak control of the equipment-cleaning process. This matters because such a finding could damage trust and require corrective action.
rows.imp_rat: Blame attribution for weak governance is peripheral to the physical cleaning job (low relevance) with bounded personal consequences (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with regulators and quality leadership when CIP records cannot support the governor's assurance claims
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with government reviewers and quality managers when cleaning records cannot support claims that the equipment was made safe for use. The records should show clearly what cleaning was done and why it was sufficient.
rows.imp_rat: Loss of credibility when records can't support claims is a governance/reputation error peripheral to circulating agents (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a missed CIP remediation commitment becomes reputation-defining for the governor
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that failing to complete a promised fix after a cleaning-process problem becomes the defining public judgment of the person in charge. The fix should be tracked through completion and supported by clear records.
rows.imp_rat: A missed remediation commitment becoming reputation-defining is a reputational error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty about the scientific rationale for the recommended cleaning approach while an inspector awaits an answer
rows.plain: The person answering the inspector needs to quickly explain why the recommended cleaning method should work. This helps show that the approach is based on sound knowledge rather than guesswork.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty about scientific rationale while inspector waits is an emotional/argumentative error peripheral to residue removal (low relevance) with transient consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of losing composure when inspection questions reveal conflicting scientific interpretations of cleaning evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Make it easier to stay calm when inspection questions expose different views about how to judge whether cleaning was good enough. This helps the buyer explain the cleaning approach clearly.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over conflicting scientific interpretations is an emotional error remote from circulating agents (low relevance) with transient impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when the recommended cleaning approach cannot be connected to residue-removal and sterile fill-finish contamination concerns
rows.plain: The person wants to avoid being caught without a clear explanation of why the cleaning method removes leftover material and helps prevent germs from getting into a product during its final preparation. This matters because others need to see that the method protects product cleanliness.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed when unable to connect the approach to contamination concerns is emotional/argumentative, not core cleaning (low relevance), with personal impact (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence needed to answer an inspector's challenge to the recommended cleaning approach
rows.plain: They want the needed proof quickly so they can explain and support the recommended cleaning method to an inspector. This helps show that the method is suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting evidence for an inspector challenge is an emotional error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with transient impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence that specified circulation requirements control contamination risk
rows.plain: The buyer wants to spend less time worrying while waiting to learn whether the required flow of material is enough to prevent contamination. This matters because contamination could make the product unsafe or unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Duration of anxiety awaiting evidence is an emotional/timing outcome with trivial direct bearing on the CFJ; low on both proxies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when stakeholders challenge whether a cleaning or sanitizing cycle satisfies the evaluation criteria
rows.plain: Stay calm when people question whether a cleaning or germ-killing cycle meets the agreed standards. This helps the team decide fairly whether the process is acceptable.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when challenged on cycle criteria is an emotional error remote from the CFJ (low relevance) with transient consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting evidence needed to assess a suspect cleaning or sanitizing cycle
rows.plain: The person wants the records needed to judge a possibly faulty cleaning or sanitizing cycle. This helps determine whether the process worked safely and what should happen next.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting evidence for a suspect cycle is an emotional/timing outcome, trivial on both proxies relative to the CFJ.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an inspector's challenge to the scientific basis of cleaning specifications causes public credibility loss with quality leadership
rows.plain: The cleaning advisor wants to avoid losing credibility with quality leaders if an inspector questions the scientific reasons behind the cleaning requirements. The requirements should be based on sound, understandable evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Public credibility loss from an inspector challenge is reputational, not core cleaning (low relevance); consequences bounded (low severity). Held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of being bypassed by quality leadership when cleaning-agent circulation specifications are selected or revised
rows.plain: They want quality leaders to involve them instead of leaving them out when the cleaning liquid and the way it moves through equipment are chosen or changed. This matters because their input helps make the cleaning process reliable and supportable.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed by quality leadership on circulation specs is an organizational-influence concern, trivial to executing the cleaning job.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that failure to substantiate a cleaning recommendation during an inspection damages standing with inspectors and quality leadership
rows.plain: Be able to clearly support the cleaning recommendation when inspectors review it. Otherwise, inspectors and quality leaders may lose confidence in the person who made the recommendation.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged standing from failing to substantiate a recommendation is reputational, not core cleaning (low relevance), with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a residue or contamination finding is publicly attributed to the circulation recommendation before quality, validation, and manufacturing leadership
rows.plain: A finding of leftover material or contamination should not be publicly blamed on the person who recommended how material or air should move through the system. The recommendation should not make that person appear responsible for the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of contamination to the recommendation is a reputational error, not the act of cleaning (low relevance), with bounded consequences here (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of being bypassed by engineering and procurement when circulation requirements for sterile fill-finish cleaning systems are specified
rows.plain: They want engineering and purchasing staff to involve them less often only after decisions have already been made about how cleaning systems should move cleaning fluid through equipment used to make sterile products. This matters because their knowledge helps protect products from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed by engineering when specifying circulation requirements is an organizational-influence concern, trivial to the physical cleaning job.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to reconcile conflicting organism, sampling, and CIP cycle evidence
rows.plain: Spend less time sorting out conflicting information about germs, samples, and cleaning cycles so people can understand what happened and respond appropriately.
rows.imp_rat: Time to reconcile conflicting evidence is a workflow-timing delay, trivial in both relevance and severity to the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation eventually yields answers but is inconsistent and time-consuming with today's fragmented data. Reconciling conflicting organism, sampling and cycle evidence is a notoriously slow manual effort. Reconciliation depends on scarce experts who can cross-read disparate datasets. Prolonged reconciliation extends holds and investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when unable to defend an assumption supporting the recommended acceptance approach
rows.plain: The person wants to support the belief or starting point behind the recommended way of deciding whether something is acceptable. This matters because the recommendation should still make sense when someone challenges its reasoning.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed defending an assumption is an emotional error unrelated to the cleaning circulation job (low relevance); consequences are personal (low severity). Ceiling 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt triggered by conflicting microbial evidence about the CIP approach
rows.plain: Avoid uncertainty when different microorganism test results give conflicting views of whether the cleaning process worked. Consistent evidence helps show that the process controls harmful contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting microbial evidence is an interpretive/emotional error peripheral to circulating agents (low relevance) with minor consequence (low severity), capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting microbial evidence needed for the acceptance recommendation
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while waiting for test results showing whether harmful or unwanted microbes are under control. This matters because those results support the recommendation to accept the work.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting microbial evidence is an emotional-state error remote from the CFJ (low relevance) with transient consequence (low severity), capped at 1.0.

rows.source_job: Peace of Mind on Hidden Gaps
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of alarm when late scrutiny reveals an unexamined fixed-equipment contamination pathway
rows.plain: Avoid alarm when a late review finds a hidden way that contamination could spread through permanently installed equipment. Finding such risks early helps protect the product.
rows.imp_rat: Alarm on discovering an unexamined pathway is an emotional-reaction error tangential to executing cleaning (low relevance) with transient effect (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an inspection finding is attributed to the evaluator's CIP microbial acceptance recommendation
rows.plain: The evaluator wants to avoid being blamed if an inspection finds a problem with their recommendation about whether a cleaning process removed harmful germs. The acceptance decision should be based on clear evidence.
rows.imp_rat: An inspection finding attributed to the acceptance recommendation is a reputational error tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with contained consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality leaders or inspectors perceive commercial bias in the evaluator's microbial acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Avoid having quality leaders or inspectors think that the evaluator’s limits for acceptable germs were chosen to favor a business deal. This helps show that the limits protect product safety and are supported by evidence.
rows.imp_rat: Perceived commercial bias in acceptance criteria is a reputational error tangential to residue removal (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholders perceive the evaluator's defense of CIP acceptance recommendations as inconsistent
rows.plain: The evaluator wants stakeholders to see their reasons for approving or rejecting cleaning standards as consistent. Consistent reasoning makes the recommendations easier to trust and defend.
rows.imp_rat: Perceived inconsistency in defending recommendations is a reputational error remote from cleaning (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compromised sterile-product integrity is attributed to the evaluator's CIP acceptance call
rows.plain: The evaluator wants to avoid being blamed if a germ-free medicine is later found to have been contaminated after the cleaning was approved. This matters because the evaluator’s approval could be seen as the cause of the problem.
rows.imp_rat: Attributing compromised product integrity to the acceptance call is a reputational error tangential to cleaning execution (low relevance) with contained impact here (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of acceptance calls second-guessed by quality or manufacturing stakeholders because criteria appear inconsistently applied
rows.plain: Make acceptance decisions using the same clear standards every time. This helps quality and production staff trust that equipment or work has been judged fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance calls second-guessed over inconsistent criteria is a reputational error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of exclusion from CIP microbial acceptance decisions by quality assurance or engineering leaders
rows.plain: Avoid leaving quality or engineering leaders out of decisions about whether the cleaning process keeps germs within acceptable limits. Including them helps protect products from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Exclusion from microbial acceptance decisions is a role/governance error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of visible blame for recommending microbial criteria perceived as causing unnecessary batch-disposition constraints
rows.plain: Avoid being publicly blamed for recommending tests for harmful microbes that people believe make it unnecessarily hard to release a batch. This helps protect both product safety and the person’s judgment.
rows.imp_rat: Visible blame for constraining criteria is a reputational error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of cleaning records lacking required authorship, approval, or controlled status
rows.plain: Fewer cleaning records should be missing the required author, approval, or official record controls. Complete, properly approved records show that cleaning was done and documented responsibly.
rows.imp_rat: Records lacking authorship/approval is a documentation-control error tangential to physical residue removal (low relevance); consequences are administrative (low severity). Held at 1.0 per ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic QMS and document control systems handle this well, so lapses are relatively uncommon. Ensuring authorship and approval status is a routine but time-adding control step. This is procedural discipline rather than scarce skill. Uncontrolled records cause costly rework and findings.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time required to retrieve requested cleaning evidence during a regulatory inspection
rows.plain: Find the requested cleaning records as quickly as possible during an inspection by regulators. Easy access to complete records helps show that cleaning was carried out as required.
rows.imp_rat: Time to retrieve evidence during inspection is a records-access error peripheral to residue removal (low relevance) with procedural impact (low severity); ceiling holds.
rows.sat_rat: EDMS and inspection-readiness practices make requested evidence findable, though disorganized legacy records still occasionally slow things. Retrieval speed during inspections is the crux, and indexed electronic systems have substantially reduced retrieval time. Retrieval mostly needs familiarity with the document system rather than deep expertise. Slow retrieval can extend costly inspections, but this is increasingly mitigated.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unresolved disagreement among quality, manufacturing, and regulatory stakeholders over the recommended conclusion
rows.plain: Help quality, production, and regulatory teams agree quickly on the final recommendation. This allows the organization to move forward with one clear conclusion.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved stakeholder disagreement is a governance-alignment error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation and governance paths eventually resolve disputes, but resolution is often slow. Prolonged unresolved disagreement over the conclusion directly extends time to decision, the central pain. Closing out disputes requires authoritative technical and quality leadership. Extended disagreement holds up release and inflates cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a relevant regulatory change and reassessment of the cleaning recommendation
rows.plain: Cleaning recommendations should be reviewed sooner after a relevant rule or official requirement changes. This helps keep the recommendations current and compliant.
rows.imp_rat: Time from regulatory change to reassessment is a compliance-timing administrative delay, trivial on both proxies for physical cleaning.
rows.sat_rat: Change monitoring exists, but reassessment cycles are slow and inconsistently triggered. The lag between a regulatory change and reassessment is often long, and this delay is the core problem. Assessing regulatory impact quickly requires ready regulatory expertise. Slow reassessment prolongs non-compliant exposure with cost implications.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of evaluation effort spent on low-risk cleaning compliance factors
rows.plain: Spend less review time on cleaning factors that pose little risk. This leaves more attention for cleaning issues that could cause serious problems.
rows.imp_rat: Effort spent on low-risk compliance factors is a prioritization-efficiency error peripheral to residue removal (low relevance) with minor consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-based approaches aim to concentrate effort, but conservative habits still divert resources to low-value work. Effort spent on low-risk factors extends timelines unnecessarily. Focusing effort where it matters requires skilled risk triage. Misallocated effort on low-risk factors is a direct waste of evaluation resources, which is the core loss here.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among quality, regulatory, validation, and engineering reviewers about cleaning-risk priorities
rows.plain: The quality, legal-compliance, testing, and engineering reviewers should agree on which cleaning problems matter most. This prevents people from repeating separate evaluations and focuses effort where it is most needed.
rows.imp_rat: Reviewer disagreement over risk priorities is a governance-alignment error tangential to residue removal (low relevance) with contained effect (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Risk-ranking frameworks narrow disagreement, but reviewers still diverge on prioritization. Resolving reviewer disagreement over priorities adds deliberation time. Reconciling expert priorities depends on senior cross-disciplinary judgement. Prolonged disagreement wastes reviewer effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of repeated evaluation caused by late changes to compliance criteria or cleaning assumptions
rows.plain: Avoid having to repeat an evaluation because the rules for acceptable work or the assumptions about cleaning change late. This saves time and keeps the evaluation focused.
rows.imp_rat: Repeated evaluation from late compliance-criteria changes is a rework/efficiency issue, peripheral and low severity to circulation.
rows.sat_rat: Change control reduces surprise changes somewhat, but late-breaking revisions still trigger frequent repeats. Late changes to criteria or assumptions force repeated evaluation cycles, directly costing time. Managing change impact needs experienced coordination. Repeated evaluation wastes labor and analytical resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing composure when regulators challenge residue or bioburden evidence supporting the CIP recommendation
rows.plain: The recommender needs to stay calm when reviewers question the evidence about leftover material or germs that supports the proposed cleaning method. This helps them explain whether the cleaning process is truly effective.
rows.imp_rat: Losing composure when regulators challenge evidence is an emotional error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of visible uncertainty while reconciling conflicting residue and bioburden evidence before regulators
rows.plain: Make it quick and easy to explain why test results about leftover material and germs do not agree before speaking with regulators. This helps resolve the disagreement with a clear, supported explanation.
rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty reconciling residue evidence before regulators is an argumentative error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with transient consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of anxiety while awaiting information needed to answer a regulatory challenge to the CIP evidence
rows.plain: They want to spend less time worrying while waiting for information that will answer a regulator’s challenge about the cleaning records. This matters because they need to defend the work confidently when it is questioned.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting information for a regulatory challenge is an emotional error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with transient consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of doubt that a plausible residue or microbial carryover pathway escaped evaluation
rows.plain: The person influencing the purchase wants to stop doubting whether a possible path for leftover material or germs to carry over was considered. They need confidence that no realistic contamination risk was overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that a carryover pathway escaped evaluation is an interpretive/emotional error peripheral to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); ceiling applies.

rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty over an unaddressed cleaning scenario is an interpretive error peripheral to residue removal (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of unease resurfacing after recommendation sign-off because the cleaning rationale feels incomplete

rows.imp_rat: Unease resurfacing after sign-off is an emotional error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); ceiling holds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when a gap among cleaning protocols, results, and change controls is raised after sign-off

rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost over insufficiently reproducible evidence is a reputational error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with personal impact (low severity); held at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality and procurement stakeholders perceive the cleaning recommendation as vendor-biased

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an adverse CIP inspection finding is publicly attributed to the purchase influencer's recommendation

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the CIP capital estimate omits installation, integration, commissioning, or validation costs

Building complete estimates that include commissioning and validation adds significant effort to the estimating timeline. Knowing that CIP needs installation, integration, commissioning, and validation costs requires specialized pharma engineering knowledge. These soft costs are frequently omitted, causing material undercapitalization of the project.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of contamination exposure, rejected-batch expenditure, deviation handling, and compliance remediation excluded from the CIP financial case

Estimating deviation-handling and remediation costs needs quality-risk expertise. Contamination and rejected-batch exposure is high-value yet routinely excluded from the case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which projected CIP benefits rely on assumptions unsupported by sterile fill-finish operating data

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of cleaning-agent, utility, maintenance, validation, and support expenditure excluded from the ownership projection

Estimating cleaning-agent, utility, and validation running costs requires operational knowledge rarely held by procurement. rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the financial case omits lost sterile fill-finish output during installation, qualification, and startup

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved CIP scope, expenditure, benefit, and schedule assumptions disputed by finance, procurement, engineering, quality, and manufacturing

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which CIP acquisition alternatives use inconsistent scope, operating baselines, risk treatment, or evaluation horizons

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which the CIP financial justification changes under plausible variations in production volume, downtime, expenditure, and realized benefits

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to substantiate CIP expenditure, benefits, risks, and financial assumptions

rows.imp_rat: Time to substantiate the case is a process-effort matter distant from circulating agents (relevance) with recoverable consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of projected CIP spending outside available capital-budget periods or funding limits

rows.imp_rat: Spending outside budget periods is a financial-timing error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with procedural impact (low severity); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cash-flow phasing errors recur but are partly caught by finance controls. Reconciling spend to budget periods requires phasing analysis that adds planning time. Aligning projected spend with capital-budget limits needs financial-phasing competence. Spending falling outside available budget periods creates funding-limit breaches and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which supplier proposals use inconsistent scope boundaries, operating assumptions, and evaluation horizons

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of lifecycle expenditure omitted from the supplier value comparison

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which proposals understate the financial exposure from inadequate residue removal or sanitization

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which proposed installation and startup assumptions deviate from sterile fill-finish availability constraints

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unplanned validation and requalification expenditure caused by lifecycle changes to the CIP capability
