rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of lifecycle expenditure above the financial case caused by replacement-part and support price escalation
rows.plain: Keep total spending from rising above the approved financial plan because replacement parts and support become more expensive. This helps keep the equipment affordable to own over time.
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle expenditure above case from price escalation is a financial error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with procedural consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation surprises are common because forecasts rarely index future prices reliably. Forecasting future price escalation requires market analysis that extends estimating work. Projecting spare-part and support escalation needs procurement market intelligence not always available. Escalation of replacement-part and support prices routinely exceeds the financial case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the CIP capability requires major replacement before its planned end of service
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that the cleaning capability needs a major replacement before its planned retirement. This helps avoid unexpected spending and interruptions.
rows.imp_rat: Early major replacement risk relates to asset reliability but is ceiling-capped (relevance); consequences are large but bounded here (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Service-life expectations are reasonably established through supplier data and specification. Premature major replacement causes long unplanned outages. Assessing service-life adequacy needs engineering evaluation of the design. Early replacement represents a large unplanned capital cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of operator, maintenance, quality, and validation labor consumed by recurring CIP activities
rows.plain: Reduce the time and cost that operators, repair workers, quality staff, and approval staff spend on repeated cleaning activities. This makes regular production less burdensome and expensive.
rows.imp_rat: Recurring CIP labor consumption relates to operating efficiency but is capped here (relevance); consequences are recoverable labor cost (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Labor demand is reasonably predictable but not systematically reduced through procurement choices. Recurring CIP labor is an ongoing operating burden, not a procurement-cycle issue. Reducing labor through automation specification requires engineering foresight at purchase. Operator, maintenance, and validation labor is a significant recurring cost seldom minimized in the buy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which the CIP capital request falls below required return, payback, risk, or strategic investment criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the request for the cleaning system meets the organization’s expectations for profit, payback time, risk, and strategic value. Otherwise, it may not receive funding approval.
rows.imp_rat: Falling below investment criteria is a case-quality matter tangential to circulating agents (relevance) with bounded approval consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Structured investment criteria are established, so requests are usually shaped to meet them. Aligning the request to return and payback thresholds adds iteration to the submission. Meeting hurdle-rate and strategic criteria requires financial-appraisal competence. A request below investment criteria wastes preparation effort and risks rejection.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of acquisition, integration, qualification, implementation, and initial operating expenditure omitted from the capital request
rows.plain: Include all expected costs for buying, connecting, testing, setting up, and initially running the system in the funding request. This reduces the risk of asking for too little money.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting expenditure categories from a capital request is a budgeting-completeness error peripheral to the cleaning job (low relevance) with procedural consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete capital requests are a recurring, well-known failure mode with only partial checklist mitigation. Assembling a complete lifecycle expenditure picture is time-intensive and often rushed under submission deadlines. Capturing integration, qualification, and initial operating costs requires cross-functional expertise seldom concentrated in one estimator. Omitting these expenditure categories from the capital request systematically understates true cost and triggers later overruns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty in the economic benefits attributed to the CIP acquisition
rows.plain: Make it clearer what financial benefits the new cleaning equipment is expected to provide. This helps decision-makers judge whether the purchase is worth funding.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty in economic benefits is a case-confidence issue remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded decision consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Benefit uncertainty is inherent and only partly reduced by current analytical practice. Reducing benefit uncertainty requires additional analysis time. Quantifying economic benefits with confidence needs financial and operational expertise. Uncertain benefits risk misallocated capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sufficient capital is unavailable in the required funding cycle
rows.plain: Make sure enough money is available during the budget period when it is needed. Otherwise, the purchase could be delayed or stopped.
rows.imp_rat: Capital availability in a funding cycle is a financing timing error remote from bioburden removal (low relevance) with deferrable consequences (low severity), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established annual budgeting processes make capital availability reasonably dependable but competing priorities still cause shortfalls. Capital cycles are fixed and known well in advance, so timing is somewhat predictable. Aligning acquisition to the funding cycle requires financial-planning coordination that finance teams routinely perform. When capital is unavailable in-cycle the project stalls, incurring carrying and re-planning costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved quality, validation, contamination-control, and data-integrity concerns in the capital submission
rows.plain: Resolve quality, testing, contamination-prevention, and record-accuracy concerns before submitting the investment request. This makes the request more complete and credible.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved quality/contamination concerns in the submission is a readiness gap indirectly tied to cleaning (relevance); consequences are review delay under the ceiling (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Quality concerns in capital submissions recur despite quality review gates. Closing out quality and data-integrity concerns before submission takes review time. Resolving validation and contamination-control concerns needs quality expertise. Unresolved concerns can force resubmission and delay funded scope.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting scope, expenditure, benefit, and schedule assumptions among capital-request reviewers
rows.plain: Make the people reviewing the major purchase agree about what work is included, what it will cost, what it will achieve, and when it will happen. This gives them a shared basis for deciding whether to approve it.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting assumptions among reviewers is a capital-request coordination issue peripheral to cleaning (relevance); consequences are review delay (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Reviewer assumption conflicts recur in capital requests despite review cycles. Aligning reviewers' assumptions requires multiple coordination meetings. Reconciling cross-functional assumptions demands facilitation and financial-modeling skill. Conflicting assumptions produce rework and mis-scoped budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration for which the CIP capital request remains pending beyond the required decision date
rows.plain: Decide on the cleaning-system funding request by the date it is needed, rather than leaving it waiting longer. This helps the planned work move forward on time.
rows.imp_rat: Request pending past decision date is a schedule matter peripheral to cleaning (relevance) with recoverable delay consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Approval delays past target dates are common in capital governance. Capital requests pending beyond the decision date directly delay commitment. Shepherding requests through approval requires stakeholder-management skill. Prolonged pending status delays benefits and can raise costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of capital-submission revisions caused by changes in acquisition scope, expenditure, or implementation assumptions
rows.plain: Avoid revising a major funding request because the items being bought, their cost, or the plan for putting them into use changes. The request should be based on a settled and realistic plan.
rows.imp_rat: Capital-submission revisions from scope changes is administrative rework distant from the CFJ (relevance) with recoverable consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Submission churn from shifting assumptions is a recurring, only partly controlled problem. Repeated capital-submission revisions from scope changes directly extend the approval cycle. Stabilizing scope before submission requires disciplined project-definition skill. Revision cycles consume analyst effort and can delay funding, raising carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which authorized funding falls short of the expenditure required for the approved CIP acquisition scope
rows.plain: Keep the approved money from falling short of what is needed to buy everything included in the approved improvement project. This helps prevent delays or missing equipment because the funding is insufficient.
rows.imp_rat: Funding shortfall against acquisition scope is a budgeting error remote from actually circulating cleaning agents (low relevance); impact is procedural (low severity). Held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: The mismatch between authorized funds and actual expenditure is a recurrent, poorly-controlled failure across capital projects. Funding gaps typically surface late in procurement, forcing time-consuming supplementary approvals that delay commitment. Estimating the true expenditure required for CIP scope demands seasoned capital-planning judgement that many procurement teams lack. Authorized funding routinely falls short because CIP integration and validation costs are chronically underestimated, leaving the executor to absorb overruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that capital authorization expires before the CIP acquisition commitment is made
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that approved funding runs out before a cleaning-system purchase is formally committed. This matters because the needed system may not be bought in time.
rows.imp_rat: Authorization expiring before commitment is a procurement-timing error remote from bioburden removal (low relevance) with deferrable consequence (low severity); ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard capital-approval tracking usually catches expiry but long CIP procurement lead times occasionally outrun the authorization. Authorization windows and expiry dates are documented, giving the executor a clear clock to work against. Tracking authorization validity is a routine administrative task requiring modest procurement skill. Lapsed authorization forces costly re-approval and can inflate re-quoted prices.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of receiving too few compliant bids from qualified suppliers to sustain competition
rows.plain: Avoid receiving too few acceptable offers from capable suppliers to allow a fair comparison. Enough qualified suppliers should be invited so the organization can choose competitively.
rows.imp_rat: Too few compliant bids threatens competition, only indirectly tied to cleaning performance (relevance); consequences are pricing/selection risk bounded here (severity).
rows.sat_rat: The pool of qualified pharma CIP suppliers is limited, so sustaining competition is often difficult. Building a qualified competitive field takes market-development time. Cultivating enough qualified CIP suppliers needs category and market knowledge. Too few compliant bids weakens price competition and raises cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical sterile fill-finish cleaning, sanitization, validation, and documentation requirements omitted from the solicitation
rows.plain: Make sure the request sent to suppliers includes all important requirements for cleaning, making the area safe from germs, checking that these steps work, and keeping the needed records. This helps suppliers price and address the work correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting requirements from the solicitation is a procurement-completeness error remote from circulating agents (low relevance) with procedural impact (low severity); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Omitted critical requirements in solicitations are a common defect that quality review only partially catches. Drafting a complete sterile-cleaning solicitation is slow when validation and documentation needs must be enumerated. Specifying critical cleaning, sanitization, and validation requirements demands scarce quality and validation expertise. Missing requirements surface as costly change orders and requalification later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the CIP scope, interfaces, acceptance criteria, and commercial requirements presented to suppliers
rows.plain: Make it clear to suppliers what cleaning system is included, how it must connect with other systems, what counts as acceptable, and what business terms apply. This lets suppliers prepare fair, comparable bids.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in scope presented to suppliers is a solicitation-clarity issue peripheral to circulating agents (relevance); its downside is procurement rework (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Specification ambiguity presented to suppliers is a common root cause of downstream error. Writing an unambiguous specification takes substantial upfront time. Precisely defining scope, interfaces, and acceptance criteria demands cross-functional technical expertise. Ambiguous solicitations produce mispriced bids and costly clarifications.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in the scope, interfaces, exclusions, and commercial assumptions underlying supplier bids
rows.plain: Make supplier bids easier to compare by making clear what work is included, what connects to what, what is left out, and what business assumptions each bid uses. This helps the company choose fairly among competing offers.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in supplier bid scope is a procurement-comparison issue tangential to the CFJ of circulating agents (relevance); its consequences are recoverable estimating noise (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Bid-to-bid variation in scope and exclusions is a chronic comparability problem. Reconciling divergent bid assumptions consumes significant evaluation time. Normalizing scope and commercial assumptions across bids demands sharp analytical skill. Assumption variation hides cost differences and causes later change orders.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of bids that fail mandatory quality, validation, data-integrity, and regulatory conditions
rows.plain: Fewer supplier offers should fail required quality, accuracy, safety, or legal checks. This helps ensure that only suitable offers are considered.
rows.imp_rat: Bids failing mandatory conditions is a procurement-screening matter peripheral to circulating agents (relevance); consequences are re-solicitation effort (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory-condition screening is routine but inconsistent bids still require heavy filtering. Screening bids against mandatory conditions adds evaluation steps. Judging compliance with quality, validation, and data-integrity conditions needs specialist reviewers. Non-compliant bids that slip through cause costly remediation later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of accepting bids from suppliers without demonstrated pharmaceutical CIP qualifications
rows.plain: The buyer wants to reduce the chance of choosing suppliers that cannot show they know how to clean pharmaceutical equipment properly. This helps prevent cleaning problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Accepting unqualified suppliers matters more but the ranking ceiling caps it; relevance to the cleaning job is indirect via supplier selection (relevance) and consequences are governed here (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Established supplier prequalification in GMP procurement reliably screens out unqualified vendors. Prequalification screening adds a step but is typically bounded. Assessing pharmaceutical CIP qualifications requires quality-audit expertise that is available in regulated buyers. Accepting unqualified suppliers risks expensive rework and compliance failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to resolve internal scope inputs, evaluation criteria, and approvals before solicitation release
rows.plain: Spend less time settling what is needed, how offers will be judged, and who must approve the request before asking suppliers for competing bids.
rows.imp_rat: Time to resolve internal inputs before solicitation is a process-timing matter peripheral to cleaning (relevance) with recoverable delay consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Internal alignment is achievable but consistently slower than planned. Resolving internal scope inputs and approvals before release is a well-known bottleneck that delays solicitation. Coordinating cross-functional inputs requires facilitation skill. Delays here defer the whole acquisition, adding carrying cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplier questions receiving inconsistent or incomplete answers before bid submission
rows.plain: Give every supplier the same complete answers to questions before they submit offers. This lets suppliers prepare comparable offers based on the same information.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent answers to supplier questions is a solicitation-process issue remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Managed Q&A processes exist but consistency still slips under time pressure. Inconsistent answers to supplier questions prolong the bidding period. Coordinating consistent Q&A responses requires organized procurement administration. Inconsistent answers cause mispriced bids and later cost disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of unauthorized disclosure of confidential facility, production, product, or security information during bidding
rows.plain: Avoid confidential information about the facility, production, products, or security being shared with the wrong people while suppliers are bidding. This protects sensitive information during the purchasing process.
rows.imp_rat: Confidential-information disclosure during bidding is a security matter tangential to the cleaning CFJ (relevance); consequences serious yet ceiling-capped here (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard confidentiality agreements and controlled bidding reliably limit unauthorized disclosure. Confidentiality controls add minor administrative steps during bidding. Managing NDAs and information control is a routine procurement competency. Disclosure incidents can cause competitive and security costs but are infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of qualified suppliers withdrawing because solicitation demands are disproportionate to the acquisition opportunity
rows.plain: Make the request for bids reasonable enough that capable suppliers do not give up because the effort seems too large for the opportunity. This preserves competition and gives the buyer more choices.
rows.imp_rat: Qualified suppliers withdrawing from disproportionate demands is a solicitation-design issue remote from the CFJ (relevance) with bounded competition consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Qualified supplier withdrawal due to onerous demands recurs in the narrow pharma CIP market. Reworking disproportionate solicitation demands to retain bidders adds time. Right-sizing solicitation demands to opportunity requires procurement judgement. Supplier withdrawals reduce competition and can raise prices.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether the selected CIP capability and documentation will satisfy validation and regulatory expectations
rows.plain: Know quickly whether the selected equipment-cleaning ability and its records will meet testing and official oversight expectations. This helps support approval that the cleaning process works as required.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty whether capability satisfies regulatory expectations is a validation-confidence issue distant from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences here (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being unable to defend the compliance basis for the CIP procurement decision under stakeholder scrutiny
rows.plain: The person wants to explain and defend why a purchase decision meets the required quality and safety rules when others question it. This helps show that the decision was responsible and compliant.
rows.imp_rat: Being unable to defend the compliance basis is a decision-defensibility matter distant from cleaning execution (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety arising from unresolved validation and regulatory interpretations
rows.plain: A buyer wants the required checks and rules to be clearly understood before moving forward. This helps ensure the purchase follows the rules.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over regulatory interpretations is a personal-state matter distant from the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about compliance-remediation exposure after CIP funds are committed
rows.plain: After money is committed to the cleaning system, there should be little uncertainty about any remaining risk or cost of fixing rule violations. This helps prevent unexpected compliance work later.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about remediation exposure after commitment is a risk-confidence matter tangential to circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of exposure felt when negotiated exceptions leave critical sourcing dependencies without clear accountability
rows.plain: Avoid uncertainty when special deal terms leave important supply needs without a clearly responsible person. This helps prevent disruptions caused by missed or neglected responsibilities.
rows.imp_rat: Exceptions leaving sourcing dependencies unaccountable is a commercial-clarity matter remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of uncertainty about whether commercial terms address critical CIP support gaps
rows.plain: Know quickly whether the purchase terms cover important gaps in the equipment-cleaning support. This helps the buyer avoid being left without needed help to keep operations running safely.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about terms addressing support gaps is a commercial-clarity matter tangential to cleaning (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while awaiting confirmation of sterile fill-finish continuity obligations
rows.plain: The person wants quick confirmation that the work needed to keep making and packaging germ-free medicine without interruption is covered. This helps protect the supply from unexpected breaks.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety awaiting continuity confirmation is a personal-state matter tangential to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of doubt caused by conflicting interpretations of CIP continuity protections
rows.plain: Avoid uncertainty when people disagree about protections that keep the cleaning process continuous and safe. Clear understanding helps prevent interruptions that could affect the work.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt from conflicting continuity interpretations is a clarity matter tangential to circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of uncertainty about whether the CIP deal remains within approved capital limits at commitment
rows.plain: Avoid uncertainty about whether the cleaning system purchase still fits within the approved spending limit when the order is placed. This helps prevent an unexpected funding problem.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about staying within capital limits at commitment is a governance-confidence matter peripheral to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when answering approver questions about CIP lifecycle expenditure
rows.plain: The buyer wants to avoid being caught off guard when approvers ask why money is needed over the life of the cleaning system. This matters because the spending should be easy to explain and show responsible cost control.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed on lifecycle expenditure questions is a personal-confidence matter remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of lost confidence when capital and lifecycle expenditure signals conflict during the go/no-go decision
rows.plain: Restore confidence quickly when the upfront price and the cost of using and maintaining something point to different choices. This helps people make a clear proceed-or-stop decision.
rows.imp_rat: Lost confidence from conflicting expenditure signals is a decision-state matter distant from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of anxiety arising from unresolved expenditure information needed to commit to the CIP deal
rows.plain: A buyer wants all spending information settled before agreeing to the proposed deal. Knowing the full cost helps them commit without fearing an unexpected expense.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety from unresolved expenditure information is a personal-state matter tangential to cleaning execution (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that accepted CIP financial tradeoffs will withstand later accountability
rows.plain: The buyer needs confidence that the agreed spending choices can be explained and defended later. Otherwise, finance or purchasing reviewers may question why the money was spent that way.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt tradeoffs will withstand accountability is a decision-confidence matter peripheral to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership attributes a CIP delivery or cleaning shortfall to the purchase executor's supplier selection
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that senior leaders blame the purchasing manager's choice of supplier if the project is delivered poorly or cleaning performance falls short. The supplier should be capable of meeting the agreed results.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a cleaning shortfall to supplier selection is a reputation matter distant from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded personal consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with quality and validation leaders when CIP terms expose sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: Avoid losing quality and equipment-checking leaders’ trust when the contract for the cleaning system does not clearly cover the needs of making sterile medicines. This matters because the supplier’s promises must protect the process and product.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when terms expose sterile requirements is a stakeholder-perception matter tangential to cleaning execution (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that finance leaders judge the purchase executor's CIP risk protections inadequate
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that finance leaders decide the buyer did not do enough to protect the company from risks in the contract. This matters because the contract should clearly protect the company from avoidable costs and problems.
rows.imp_rat: Finance judging risk protections inadequate is a stakeholder-perception matter peripheral to cleaning (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the purchase executor is bypassed in CIP supplier-remedy decisions because support obligations are disputed
rows.plain: The person who completes purchases should be included more often when deciding how a cleaning-equipment supplier must fix a problem. This matters because clear support responsibilities help ensure the supplier delivers the needed remedy.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed in remedy decisions is a role-authority matter distant from the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of engineering and manufacturing stakeholders second-guessing the purchase executor's contracting judgment when CIP supplier accountability is contested
rows.plain: Avoid engineers and manufacturing staff doubting the buyer's decision about who is responsible for problems with the cleaning supplier. Clear responsibility in the agreement helps everyone trust the contracting decision.
rows.imp_rat: Stakeholders second-guessing contracting judgment is a reputation matter distant from cleaning execution (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership or finance attributes a CIP capital-limit breach to the purchase executor's judgment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that top leaders or the finance team blame the buyer if equipment designed to clean itself goes over its spending limit. This requires keeping the purchase within the agreed amount.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a capital-limit breach to the executor is a reputation matter tangential to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded personal consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of credibility lost with finance when realized CIP lifecycle expenditure exceeds the approved financial case
rows.plain: Avoid losing finance’s trust when the actual long-term cost of the cleaning system is higher than the approved financial plan. This matters because the buyer must show that spending stayed within the agreed value.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when spend exceeds the case is a stakeholder-perception matter distant from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which senior leadership blames the purchase executor for acquiring CIP capability that misses sterile fill-finish cleaning requirements
rows.plain: Senior leaders should be less likely to blame the purchasing person for buying cleaning equipment that does not meet the needs of making sterile products and packaging them. This helps show that the purchase was matched to the actual cleaning requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Blame for missing cleaning requirements is a reputation matter tangential to executing the clean (relevance) with bounded personal consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of being second-guessed or bypassed in future CIP funding decisions because acquisition tradeoffs cannot be defended
rows.plain: The person wants future funding choices for a cleaning system to be seen as sensible and well supported, so others do not second-guess or bypass them. They need to show why the buying tradeoffs were worthwhile.
rows.imp_rat: Being second-guessed in future decisions is a reputation matter tangential to circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which proposed CIP expenditure exceeds the approved capital budget envelope
rows.plain: Keep the proposed spending on the factory improvement from exceeding the approved overall investment budget. This helps the project stay within the money available.
rows.imp_rat: Expenditure exceeding budget envelope is a budgeting error not central to the cleaning job (low relevance) with procedural impact (low severity); ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Overshoot recurs predictably because early estimates under-capture integration and validation scope. Budget-envelope checks happen at gated reviews so overruns are caught on schedule rather than late. Keeping proposed spend inside the envelope needs disciplined scoping judgement that varies by preparer. Envelope discipline is a core budgeting practice, but CIP proposals frequently creep past the approved envelope.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required CIP capital cost elements omitted from the budget
rows.plain: Include every required cost for the cleaning system in the budget. This prevents the project from running short of money later.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted capital cost elements is a budgeting-completeness error peripheral to residue removal (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Incomplete scoping means required elements are routinely left out of the initial budget. Cost-element checklists are reviewed within the normal submission timeline. Capturing every CIP capital element (utilities, tie-ins, validation) demands multidisciplinary knowledge that single preparers lack. Omitted cost elements surface as unbudgeted spend later, a common source of overrun.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variance between committed CIP investment cost and the approved estimate
rows.plain: Keep the final cost of the cleaning-system investment close to the amount that was approved. This helps prevent unexpected spending.
rows.imp_rat: Variance between committed cost and estimate is a financial-control error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with procedural impact (low severity); ceiling holds.
rows.sat_rat: Change orders and validation add-ons make cost variance a recurring rather than exceptional event. Variance is tracked at defined reporting milestones so it surfaces on a known cadence. Controlling committed-versus-estimate variance relies on project-control expertise unevenly applied to CIP work. Committed CIP cost frequently drifts above the approved estimate, directly eroding the approver's budget position.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency in CIP scope among supplier proposals
rows.plain: Make sure all supplier proposals include the same cleaning work and equipment. This makes their prices fair to compare and helps keep spending within the budget.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent scope among proposals is a comparison-clarity issue tangential to circulating agents (relevance) with bounded evaluation consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Supplier proposals commonly differ in scope, complicating fair comparison. Normalizing supplier scope across proposals adds procurement time. Ensuring consistent supplier scope needs procurement and technical skill. Inconsistent supplier scope makes price comparison misleading.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of credible CIP expenditure uncertainty not covered by budget contingency
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of possible cleaning-related spending that is not included in the budget’s backup funds. This makes the total cost easier to predict and manage.
rows.imp_rat: Uncovered expenditure uncertainty is a financial-planning error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded impact (low severity); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contingency is typically a flat percentage rather than risk-based, so material uncertainty routinely falls outside it. Contingency is set at submission time and rarely revisited quickly as risks emerge. Sizing contingency against credible uncertainty needs risk-quantification skill that most cases apply crudely. Under-sized contingency leaves real exposure uncovered and forces later supplemental funding.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of CIP expenditure incorrectly classified under capital budget rules
rows.plain: Make sure spending on cleaning equipment and production areas is placed in the correct budget category. This keeps financial records and approvals accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Misclassified expenditure under budget rules is an accounting error peripheral to physical cleaning (low relevance) with procedural consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established accounting policy makes classification fairly consistent, with occasional gray-area CIP items misfiled. Capitalization classification is done during standard review with little added delay. Correct capital-versus-expense classification depends on accounting judgement that finance partners generally supply competently. Misclassification distorts the budget and can trigger costly restatement, though errors are usually caught.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of CIP expenditure scheduled outside the approved funding period
rows.plain: Spend less money on the project outside the time period when funding has been approved. This helps keep payments within the agreed budget schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Expenditure scheduled outside the funding period is a financial-timing error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Schedule slippage means some CIP spend regularly falls outside the approved period. Spend scheduling against the funding period is a routine planning activity but slips are common. Aligning cash flow to the approved period needs project-scheduling competence generally available. Spend outside the funding period strands or reallocates capital inefficiently.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier pricing expires before investment authorization
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier’s quoted price does not expire before the purchase receives approval. Otherwise, the final cost may be higher or the purchase may need to be renegotiated.
rows.imp_rat: Pricing expiring before authorization is a commercial-timing matter peripheral to the CFJ (relevance) with recoverable cost consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Pricing occasionally expires before authorization when reviews run long. Aligning quote validity to the authorization timeline is a routine procurement task, but slow approvals still cause expiry. Managing quote validity windows needs standard procurement discipline. Expired pricing forces re-quoting, often at higher cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of CIP capital allocated to nonessential scope
rows.plain: Reduce the amount of investment money spent on features or work that are not necessary. This keeps the budget focused on what the project truly needs.
rows.imp_rat: Capital allocated to nonessential scope is a budgeting-efficiency error tangential to circulating agents (low relevance) with procedural impact (low severity); ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Value-engineering reviews catch some but not all nonessential scope before authorization. Scope scrutiny happens at review without major delay. Distinguishing essential from nonessential CIP scope requires engineering and value judgement. Nonessential scope inflates capital, and gold-plating is a recurring tendency in CIP designs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of shared infrastructure cost misallocated to the CIP investment
rows.plain: Make sure the cost of shared building systems is assigned fairly instead of being incorrectly charged to the cleaning-system investment. This keeps spending aligned with the right budget.
rows.imp_rat: Misallocated shared infrastructure cost is an accounting-accuracy matter remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded financial consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Allocation disputes recur but standard methods keep most cases reasonable. Allocating shared infrastructure cost is done in normal costing but debated. Correct cost allocation requires cost-accounting judgement usually available. Misallocated shared infrastructure distorts the CIP investment's true cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of material lifecycle costs omitted from the CIP investment case
rows.plain: Make sure the investment plan includes all important costs of the cleaning process throughout its useful life. This gives decision-makers a more honest view of what the investment will cost.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting lifecycle costs from the investment case is a financial-completeness error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Investment cases habitually focus on capital and skip ongoing consumables and maintenance, so lifecycle omission is chronic. Lifecycle costing is often deferred, adding rework once operating costs are questioned. Building a full material-lifecycle view needs specialized total-cost-of-ownership skill seldom applied rigorously. Omitted lifecycle costs understate true spend, leaving the approver exposed to later operating overruns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which projected CIP financial benefits lack credible evidence
rows.plain: There should be reliable evidence supporting the expected money savings from the cleaning process. Without it, the claimed financial benefits may not be believable.
rows.imp_rat: Projected benefits lacking credible evidence is a financial-case error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Benefit claims often rest on assertion rather than evidence, making them chronically unreliable at approval. Assembling credible benefit evidence extends case-preparation time. Substantiating projected benefits requires analytical rigor that many CIP cases lack. Unsupported benefits risk approving spend that never returns value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of baseline fixed-equipment cleaning cost inputs that are inaccurate

rows.sat_rat: Baseline cleaning cost inputs are frequently inaccurate or estimated. Gathering accurate baseline cleaning costs takes data-collection time. rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of financially material quality and compliance exposure excluded from the CIP investment case

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of implementation disruption costs omitted from CIP cash-flow projections

Quantifying sterile-fill failure exposure is slow and often incomplete at decision time. Estimating contamination-failure financial exposure demands rare sterile-manufacturing and quality expertise. Underestimated failure exposure hides very large potential recall and hold costs from the approver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of compliance-exposure mitigation assumptions unsupported by applicable evidence
rows.plain: Make sure claims about reducing the risk and cost of failing required rules are supported by relevant proof. This helps prevent the expected protection from being overstated.
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported mitigation assumptions is a case-credibility issue peripheral to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Mitigation assumptions frequently lack applicable evidentiary support. Sourcing supporting evidence for mitigation assumptions is slow. Validating mitigation assumptions requires quality/regulatory expertise. Unsupported mitigation assumptions leave exposure that costs money later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which residual compliance exposure is understated after the CIP investment
rows.plain: Make sure the remaining risk of breaking rules is not understated after investing in the cleaning system. The investment may reduce some problems but not remove every compliance obligation.
rows.imp_rat: Understated residual compliance exposure is a financial-assessment error remote from residue removal (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Residual compliance risk is systematically optimistic in CIP cases, so understatement is a persistent, unaddressed gap. Residual-exposure assessment lags because it depends on evolving regulatory interpretation. Judging remaining compliance exposure requires scarce quality/regulatory expertise the approver depends on others for. Understated residual exposure hides potential remediation and enforcement costs from the decision.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among finance, quality, regulatory, and procurement estimates of compliance-related financial exposure
rows.plain: Make sure Finance, Quality, Regulatory, and Procurement agree on the likely financial impact of meeting required rules. This helps the organization plan for compliance costs more accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement over compliance exposure estimates is an internal-estimating issue remote from cleaning (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Functions routinely disagree on compliance-related exposure, a persistent unresolved gap. Reconciling divergent exposure estimates across functions is slow. Aligning compliance-exposure figures needs rare joint finance/quality/regulatory expertise. Divergent exposure estimates leave the true financial risk unclear.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of regulatory or enforcement assumptions obsolete at CIP authorization
rows.plain: Use current information about possible legal or regulatory costs when the cleaning project is approved. This prevents decisions from relying on rules or enforcement expectations that are no longer current.
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete regulatory assumptions at authorization is an estimating-currency matter remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory assumptions can become stale between case build and authorization. Refreshing regulatory assumptions before authorization takes monitoring time. Keeping regulatory assumptions current needs specialized regulatory tracking. Obsolete regulatory assumptions can trigger costly late redesign.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of compliance-related exposure estimates not traceable to supporting evidence
rows.plain: Make sure every estimate of the risk or cost of failing required rules can be traced back to supporting proof. This makes the estimates easier to check and trust.
rows.imp_rat: Non-traceable compliance exposure estimates is a documentation-quality issue remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Compliance-exposure estimates are often not traceable to evidence. Building evidence traceability for exposure estimates adds time. Establishing traceable exposure estimates needs disciplined documentation skill. Untraceable exposure estimates weaken defensibility of the risk-based spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for required compliance evidence before the CIP funding decision
rows.plain: Make it quicker to gather the proof needed to understand compliance costs before deciding whether to fund the cleaning process. This helps decision-makers see the possible financial impact.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for compliance evidence before the funding decision is a schedule matter peripheral to circulating agents (relevance); consequences are decision delay (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Compliance-evidence availability is inconsistent, making the wait unpredictable. Waiting on compliance evidence before deciding regularly stalls the funding decision. Assembling required compliance evidence needs quality expertise in short supply. Decision delay defers benefits and can cause missed budget windows.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of avoided compliance cost counted across multiple benefit categories in the CIP case
rows.plain: Count savings from avoiding rule-related costs only once in the cleaning investment case. This prevents the expected benefits from being overstated.
rows.imp_rat: Double-counted avoided compliance cost is a benefit-integrity error peripheral to circulating agents (relevance) with bounded financial consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Benefit double counting occurs regularly and is not always caught. Detecting double-counted avoided-cost benefits adds review time. Spotting cross-category double counting requires financial-analysis skill. Double-counted avoided compliance cost overstates the return and misguides the decision.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of potential remediation, recall, and production-hold cost omitted from the compliance exposure assessment
rows.plain: Include the possible costs of fixing problems, recalling products, and stopping production when assessing compliance risks. This prevents the financial impact of a compliance problem from being understated.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted remediation/recall cost in the compliance exposure assessment is an estimation gap, not central to executing the cleaning job (relevance); consequences are financial and bounded here (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Remediation, recall, and hold costs are routinely left out of exposure assessments. Assembling remediation/recall cost scenarios delays the exposure assessment. Modeling recall and production-hold cost needs specialized risk expertise seldom on hand. Omitting these tail costs drastically understates the downside the capital is meant to avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that the funded CIP investment can withstand validation, data-integrity, and audit scrutiny
rows.plain: Make sure the planned cleaning-system investment can stand up to checks of its testing, records, and inspection history. This helps show that the spending and the system meet required standards.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt the investment withstands audit scrutiny is a confidence matter remote from the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while CIP investment records and validation evidence remain inconsistent
rows.plain: Reduce the worry caused by conflicting purchase records and proof that the cleaning system was checked and approved. Consistent records make the investment easier to defend during a review.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over inconsistent records is a personal-state matter remote from cleaning execution (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty when explaining alignment between the CIP investment rationale and validation requirements
rows.plain: Make the connection clear between spending on the cleaning system and the checks needed to prove the process works properly. This helps the organization defend the purchase during a review.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty explaining alignment with validation is a communication-confidence matter peripheral to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of personal exposure felt when CIP approval traceability is challenged by quality or audit stakeholders
rows.plain: Reduce the personal risk of being blamed when quality or auditors question the records showing how cleaning approval was tracked. Complete, easy-to-follow records help show why the approval was made.
rows.imp_rat: Personal exposure over approval traceability is a reputation matter remote from the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that total CIP investment fits the approved cost envelope at commitment
rows.plain: Make sure the full cost of the cleaning-system investment fits within the approved budget when the purchase is committed. This supports confidence in the business decision.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt that investment fits the cost envelope is a financial-uncertainty error peripheral to the CFJ (low relevance) with bounded consequence (low severity); capped at 1.0.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed when defending unsupported CIP savings assumptions to stakeholders
rows.plain: They want to avoid feeling exposed when asked to support savings claims about cleaning equipment or processes that do not have enough proof. They need reliable support for the expected savings.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed defending savings assumptions is a personal-confidence matter remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of confidence lost when CIP cost, benefit, and contingency figures conflict
rows.plain: Reduce loss of confidence when the estimated cleaning cost, expected benefit, and extra money set aside for surprises do not match. Consistent figures make the case for the project more convincing.
rows.imp_rat: Confidence lost when figures conflict is a decision-confidence matter distant from the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while material financial inputs for CIP commitment remain unresolved
rows.plain: The buyer wants the remaining financial information needed to commit to the cleaning system to be settled. This matters so the business decision is based on a clear understanding of the money involved.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety while inputs remain unresolved is a personal-state matter peripheral to circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty that CIP acceptance criteria contain commercial exposure before fund release
rows.plain: Make sure the conditions for accepting the cleaning system do not leave the company exposed to unexpected costs or financial claims before money is released. This helps protect the company during the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty over commercial exposure in acceptance criteria is a clarity matter remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of feeling exposed by gaps in supplier obligations when authorizing the CIP investment
rows.plain: The buyer wants to avoid being caught off guard by missing promises or responsibilities from suppliers when approving money for the cleaning investment. This matters because unclear supplier commitments could create problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed by supplier-obligation gaps is a personal-confidence matter peripheral to cleaning (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt that CIP warranty terms provide adequate protection before commitment
rows.plain: The buyer needs confidence that the supplier’s warranty provides enough protection before agreeing to the purchase. Unclear or conflicting warranty terms could leave the buyer responsible for problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Doubt over warranty adequacy is a commercial-confidence matter remote from the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of anxiety while CIP commercial exceptions remain unresolved before fund release
rows.plain: Reduce the worry that continues while unusual business terms for the cleaning system are still unsettled before money is released. Clear agreement helps people feel confident that funding can proceed.
rows.imp_rat: Anxiety over unresolved commercial exceptions is a personal-state matter peripheral to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of uncertainty caused by conflicting interpretations of contracted CIP terms
rows.plain: Reduce uncertainty caused by different readings of the contract terms for cleaning the equipment in place. This helps the buyer and supplier agree on exactly what cleaning work is required.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty from conflicting contract interpretations is a commercial-clarity matter peripheral to cleaning (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a CIP audit finding is publicly attributed to the buyer's approval before regulators or quality leadership
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that an audit finds a problem with the equipment-cleaning process and publicly blames the buyer for approving the purchase. The buyer needs clear evidence that the choice was carefully checked before approval.
rows.imp_rat: Public attribution of an audit finding is a reputation matter distant from cleaning execution (relevance); consequences serious yet ceiling-capped (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with validation and engineering leaders when the CIP investment rationale is challenged
rows.plain: Avoid losing trust with testing and engineering leaders when the reasons for investing in equipment cleaning are questioned. The proposal should clearly explain why the investment is needed and worthwhile.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost when rationale is challenged is a stakeholder-perception matter peripheral to circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that quality or compliance reviewers question the buyer's integrity over CIP approval traceability
rows.plain: Quality or compliance reviewers should not question the buyer’s honesty because the records showing who approved the cleaning work are incomplete or unclear. The approval trail should clearly show what happened and who made each decision.
rows.imp_rat: Reviewers questioning integrity over traceability is a reputation matter peripheral to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which the buyer is blamed before capital governance when the CIP financial basis fails scrutiny
rows.plain: The buyer should not be blamed before the organization reviews its spending controls when the financial case for a cleaning project is challenged. This helps identify the real cause of the problem fairly.
rows.imp_rat: Being blamed when the financial basis fails is a reputation matter remote from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which capital governance bypasses the buyer in subsequent sterile-manufacturing investment decisions
rows.plain: The group that oversees major spending should continue involving the buyer in later investments for germ-free production. This keeps important purchasing knowledge in the decisions.
rows.imp_rat: Governance bypassing the buyer in future investments is a reputation/role matter remote from the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that senior leadership attributes a CIP business-case miss to the purchase executor's judgment
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that senior leaders blame the purchasing manager's judgment if the business case for the cleaning project fails to deliver the expected value. The recommendation should be based on sound reasoning.
rows.imp_rat: Attribution of a business-case miss to the executor is a reputation matter tangential to the CFJ (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of credibility lost with capital governance when funded CIP lifecycle economics are missed
rows.plain: The person responsible for buying equipment wants to avoid losing the trust of those who oversee company spending when the expected lifetime financial benefits of a funded project are not achieved.
rows.imp_rat: Credibility lost with governance on missed economics is a stakeholder-perception matter remote from cleaning (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency with which capital governance second-guesses future CIP recommendations because prior assumptions appear unsound
rows.plain: The spending oversight group should not doubt later cleaning recommendations simply because earlier assumptions now seem weak. This helps future decisions rely on clear, well-supported information.
rows.imp_rat: Governance second-guessing future recommendations is a reputation matter peripheral to cleaning (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material decisions affecting approved CIP economics in which stakeholders bypass the purchase executor
rows.plain: Make sure important decisions that affect the approved financial results for the cleaning project are not made without involving the person responsible for the purchase. This keeps the approved case under proper control.
rows.imp_rat: Being bypassed in decisions affecting economics is a role-authority matter distant from circulating agents (relevance) with bounded consequences (severity).

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exported records corrupted or altered during export
rows.plain: Make sure production-cycle records are not damaged or changed while being exported. Accurate records are needed to rely on what happened during each cycle.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted exported records affect data integrity but are a peripheral records-handling error relative to physical cleaning; capped per the batch-5 importance ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established export formats and checksums make outright corruption uncommon, though occasional silent alteration during format conversion still occurs. Standard export tools complete data extraction quickly without significant delay. Modern export utilities require little specialized skill to run correctly, reducing dependence on experts. Export processes are cheap to run and rarely consume meaningful resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exported records whose identifiers and timestamps fail to preserve traceability between cycles and validation evidence
rows.plain: Make sure exported records keep the correct names and times so each production cycle can be linked to the evidence showing it was checked. This makes it possible to follow the history of each cycle.
rows.imp_rat: Traceability failures in exported record identifiers matter for validation evidence but are a documentation-side error below the batch-4 ranking; held at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Legacy CSIP systems often store records in formats that break identifier and timestamp linkage on export, so traceability failures are fairly common. Preserving traceable identifiers and timestamps rarely adds time when systems retain them natively but manual mapping slows edge cases. Maintaining validation-to-cycle traceability requires understanding of the data model and validation evidence, a specialized skill. Lost traceability can force expensive re-validation or investigation efforts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected downstream process, utility, and monitoring stakeholders omitted from the removal notice
rows.plain: Leave out fewer people responsible for later production steps, building services, or monitoring when announcing the machine’s removal. This helps everyone affected prepare for the change.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting downstream stakeholders from the removal notice is a communication defect at decommissioning; relevance and severity are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Stakeholder mapping relies on tribal knowledge and incomplete dependency records, so omissions from removal notices recur. Identifying all downstream stakeholders for the removal notice takes time to trace dependencies. Knowing which downstream processes and utilities depend on the system requires deep process knowledge. Omitted stakeholders can suffer unplanned outages with real cost, though notification itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between the decision to remove the cleaning system from service and notification of affected stakeholders
rows.plain: Notify the affected people as soon as the decision is made to stop using the cleaning system. This gives them time to respond to the change.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in notifying stakeholders of service removal is a communication/administrative timing issue with low stakes for cleaning efficacy; kept at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard distribution lists and CMMS triggers make notification fairly consistent, though occasional stakeholders are missed. Established change-control and decommissioning workflows push out service-removal notifications reasonably promptly, though manual routing still introduces delays. Notification requires coordinators who know the stakeholder map, but this is routine administrative work rather than scarce expertise. The communication itself costs little beyond staff time already budgeted into decommissioning projects.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of affected process lines, utilities, and monitoring responsibilities missing from the notification
rows.plain: Leave out fewer production lines, building services, or monitoring duties that will be affected by the change. This helps ensure every affected area is included in the notice.
rows.imp_rat: Missing notification of affected lines and monitoring responsibilities is an administrative coordination error with low centrality to the sterilization job and modest consequences; capped at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Interdependency documentation is typically incomplete, so notifications routinely miss some affected lines and responsibilities. Compiling the full list of affected process lines and monitoring responsibilities is a slow tracing task. Complete notification coverage depends on experts who understand all interconnections and responsibilities. Missed process lines or monitoring responsibilities can cause disruptions costly to remediate.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting removal dates, boundaries, or affected connections communicated to stakeholders
rows.plain: Give everyone the same information about when removal will happen, what area is included, and which connections will be affected. This prevents people from preparing for different work.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting removal dates communicated to stakeholders is a coordination ambiguity in decommissioning; capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Version control across removal plans is weak in typical practice, so stakeholders often receive contradictory dates and boundary definitions. Reconciling conflicting removal dates and boundaries takes repeated back-and-forth that slows the decommissioning schedule. Detecting conflicts across multiple documents requires an experienced planner to cross-check versions, which is error-prone manual work. Conflicting information wastes labor hours and can trigger costly rework or mistaken disconnections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of changes to the removal timing or scope not communicated before taking effect
rows.plain: Make sure people are told ahead of time when something will be removed and what will be removed. This gives them time to prepare.
rows.imp_rat: Uncommunicated changes to removal timing is a decommissioning-coordination error remote from cleaning execution, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Disposal projects typically use ad hoc email and meeting communication with no enforced gating, so unannounced timing or scope changes recur frequently. Change notifications during decommissioning are often issued late or after the fact because timing changes cascade faster than manual notice cycles can keep up. Ensuring changes are communicated before they take effect depends heavily on an experienced project coordinator's diligence rather than any systematic control. The cost of a missed pre-change notice is low to distribute but the downstream disruption it causes is expensive, and current practice rarely captures that.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of service, chemical supply, and remote monitoring contracts that remain active after system disposal
rows.plain: Prevent service, chemical-supply, and remote-monitoring agreements from continuing after the system has been thrown away or removed. This avoids paying for services that are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Contracts active after disposal are an administrative loose end with low relevance and severity to cleaning; both capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Contract inventories are often incomplete or siloed, so lingering active agreements are a common and costly oversight. Cancelling service and supply contracts involves administrative lead time and notice periods. Tracking all active contracts requires procurement records knowledge but little technical skill. Contracts left active after disposal directly waste money on services no longer needed, the core loss here.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contracts automatically renewing before cancellation is completed
rows.plain: Prevent a service agreement from renewing on its own before the cancellation is fully completed. This helps avoid paying for service that is no longer wanted.
rows.imp_rat: Contracts auto-renewing before cancellation is a purely commercial/administrative waste with no bearing on the cleaning job; both proxies at the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Contract-management systems with renewal alerts make missed cancellations fairly uncommon though not eliminated. Cancellation notice windows demand timely action, and calendaring tools mostly catch them. Managing contract cancellation is a clerical/administrative task needing little specialized skill. An auto-renewed contract wastes money directly, which is the felt loss when it slips through.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time cancellation requests remain pending because authorization or contract records are missing
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to cancel a service agreement when approval or contract information is missing. This helps the cancellation go through without avoidable delays.
rows.imp_rat: Cancellation requests pending due to missing authorization records is an administrative delay peripheral to the sterilization job, capped at 1.0 by batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Record gaps are common and there is no standard fast-track, so delays recur unpredictably. Cancellation requests routinely stall for days or weeks while authorization or contract paperwork is chased down. Resolving missing authorization needs administrative navigation of contracts and approvals rather than technical skill. Pending cancellations tie up assets and carrying costs but no direct material waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of service or supply coverage ending before final system shutdown activities are complete
rows.plain: Avoid ending service or supply support before all work to shut down the system is finished. Support may still be needed until the shutdown is complete.
rows.imp_rat: Service or supply coverage ending before final shutdown is a contract-timing error affecting decommissioning logistics; it barely touches the active cleaning/sterilization job and its consequences are manageable scheduling gaps.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage sometimes lapses before final activities finish because shutdown schedules routinely slip. Service and supply contracts are often scheduled to a target end date that shutdown activities can overrun. Coordinating coverage windows needs project-management attention rather than technical skill. Extending coverage or emergency re-engagement of vendors is costly when timing slips.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of charges disputed because the purchaser and provider use different cancellation dates
rows.plain: Avoid charges caused by the buyer and provider using different dates to end a service. This helps ensure the service stops on the intended date and prevents extra billing.
rows.imp_rat: Disputed charges over differing cancellation dates is a peripheral billing ambiguity with minor consequence, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Purchaser and provider records frequently diverge with no shared authoritative cancellation timestamp, so mismatches recur. Disputes over cancellation dates drag out billing reconciliation for weeks between parties. Resolving date discrepancies needs contract-administration knowledge that is often thinly staffed. Disputed charges tie up money and generate rework, directly wasting resources.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the technician disconnects the wrong utility or process connection
rows.plain: Make sure the technician disconnects the correct pipes, cables, or other connections. This helps prevent damage, spills, or unsafe conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Disconnecting the wrong utility is a technician error tangential to routine cleaning execution (low relevance) though potentially disruptive; the batch ceiling caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Labeling, P&IDs and lockout reduce errors, but wrong connections are still disconnected when documentation is stale. Correctly identifying connections before disconnection adds verification time. Avoiding wrong-utility disconnection depends heavily on technician familiarity with the specific plant layout. A wrong disconnection can cause damage or downtime with real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of utility or process connections left attached before system removal
rows.plain: Make sure all pipes, cables, and other service connections are detached before the system is removed. This helps prevent damage, leaks, and injuries.
rows.imp_rat: Connections left attached before removal is a decommissioning safety detail peripheral to cleaning function; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout-tagout and disconnection checklists are mature safety practice, so left-attached connections are relatively uncommon though not eliminated. Verifying all connections are detached before removal adds a checkout step but is quick. Identifying every utility and process connection requires familiarity with the installation drawings. A missed live connection can cause spills or damage with significant remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of salvaged components that fail serviceability requirements after removal
rows.plain: Make sure parts removed for reuse still meet the requirements for working properly and safely. This matters so faulty parts are not put back into service.
rows.imp_rat: Salvaged components failing serviceability after removal is a reuse-quality issue peripheral to the core job, with severity held at 1.0 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without in-service condition data, many removed components fail serviceability checks unpredictably, and no standard predictive practice covers this. Testing salvaged components for serviceability adds inspection time after removal. Judging whether valves, pumps, or heat exchangers meet reuse specs needs experienced technicians and test equipment. Failed salvage wastes the recovery effort but the salvage attempt itself is aimed at cost recovery.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing component identity, configuration, or maintenance history during salvage
rows.plain: Keep each salvaged part’s identity, setup information, and repair history with it. This helps people know what each part is and how it was maintained.
rows.imp_rat: Losing component identity during salvage is a decommissioning/records concern far from the active clean-and-sterilize job, so relevance and severity are both low.
rows.sat_rat: Component identity, configuration and history are routinely lost during salvage because there is no enforced capture practice at teardown. Recording component identity during salvage is a slow manual step easily skipped under teardown pressure. Preserving configuration and maintenance history requires asset-management discipline usually absent from salvage crews. Lost identity destroys resale/reuse value, but the information loss itself is the core failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serviceable components damaged during disassembly or handling
rows.plain: Avoid damaging usable parts while taking a system apart or moving its parts. This preserves parts that can be used again.
rows.imp_rat: Serviceable components damaged during disassembly is a handling defect peripheral to operating CIP/SIP; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Without detailed disassembly procedures, handling damage to serviceable components occurs regularly. Careful disassembly to protect serviceable parts takes longer than destructive teardown. Preserving components during disassembly requires skilled technicians who know sequence and handling methods. Damaged serviceable components lose resale or reuse value, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of component-removal delays caused by seized connections or restricted access
rows.plain: Remove reusable parts without long delays when connections are stuck or hard to reach. This helps recover useful parts safely and efficiently.
rows.imp_rat: Component-removal delays from seized connections affect maintenance access, not the core cleaning result; enforced ceiling keeps both at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Access and seizure problems are common and hard to anticipate, so schedules slip frequently. Seized fittings and restricted access routinely stall physical component removal with unpredictable duration. Freeing corroded connections demands experienced fitters and sometimes specialty tooling. Labor overruns and cutting/replacement of seized parts drive real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of connected sterile fill-finish production disruption caused by system retirement
rows.plain: Keep the production of sterile products and their final packaging from being disrupted for long when the equipment is retired. This helps the changeover happen with less interruption.
rows.imp_rat: Production disruption from system retirement is an end-of-life scheduling event peripheral to ongoing cleaning function; capped at 1.0 by the batch order.
rows.sat_rat: Planned tie-in isolation usually protects production, but unforeseen shared-utility dependencies sometimes cause disruption. Retiring a CIP system feeding sterile fill-finish can disrupt connected production unless carefully sequenced during planned shutdowns. Coordinating isolation without contaminating adjacent sterile operations requires experienced process and QA staff. Any production disruption in fill-finish is extremely costly, so isolation valving is planned to minimize it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required decommissioning records left incomplete at formal retirement
rows.plain: Complete every required record when equipment is formally retired, so the retirement is properly documented and can be verified later.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete decommissioning records at retirement is a closeout-administration error peripheral to the operational cleaning job, capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Decommissioning checklists exist but are inconsistently enforced, so records are routinely left incomplete at formal retirement. Compiling decommissioning records at retirement is time-consuming when signoffs trail behind physical work. Completing a full decommissioning dossier requires QA familiarity with retirement protocols and change-control. Incomplete records mainly cost later audit rework rather than immediate spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of regulated system materials sent to an incompatible disposal stream
rows.plain: Send materials from the controlled system to a disposal method that is safe and suitable for those materials. This helps prevent unsafe handling and improper disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Sending regulated materials to an incompatible disposal stream is a compliance/environmental concern peripheral to the CIP/SIP job, with severity capped at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Licensed hazardous-waste contractors and manifests handle most streams reliably, though ambiguous or mixed-material parts still get miscategorized. Sorting materials into correct regulated streams adds handling time but rarely bottlenecks the project. Correctly classifying stainless, elastomers, residual chemicals, and contaminated components requires waste-compliance knowledge not always on the decommissioning crew. Misrouting regulated waste triggers fines and rework, but licensed disposal vendors normally price this in.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous chemical residue remaining in the system after disposal
rows.plain: Leave as little dangerous chemical residue as possible in the system before it is thrown away or taken apart. This reduces the risk of exposing people or the environment to harmful chemicals.
