rows.imp_rat: Hazardous chemical residue after disposal is an environmental/waste concern with moderate severity but lower centrality to the cleaning job, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard decontamination protocols reduce residue but verification is imperfect, so trapped hazardous residue is still occasionally found. Verifying residual-free status adds inspection time before the system can be released. Confirming full chemical removal takes technicians who understand where residue hides in the system. Additional flushing and testing consume chemicals and disposal capacity.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of hazardous waste consignments rejected by approved disposal channels
rows.plain: Prevent shipments of dangerous waste from being refused by approved waste handlers. This helps the waste leave the site safely and legally.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous waste consignments rejected by disposal channels is a compliance-delay concern held at 1 relevance and 1 severity per the mandatory cap.
rows.sat_rat: Established waste-characterization and manifesting practice keeps most consignments accepted, though profiling errors still cause rejections. A rejected consignment forces reprocessing and rescheduling, adding significant turnaround time. Correct waste profiling and manifesting requires trained hazardous-waste coordinators. Rejections incur return transport, re-treatment and lost fees.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous chemical disposals with incomplete or inaccurate required records
rows.plain: Make sure the required records for throwing away dangerous chemicals are complete and accurate.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete hazardous chemical disposal records carry compliance severity but are peripheral to the CIP/SIP cleaning job and ranked below batch 4; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Regulated hazardous waste manifesting is a mature, enforced practice, so records are usually complete though inaccuracies still occur. Completing hazardous disposal records adds documentation time but is a routine part of established waste handling. Hazardous chemical documentation requires trained EHS personnel who understand manifest requirements. Incomplete records risk fines and rejected shipments that carry real cost, while compliant disposal itself is priced in.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of targeted control-system data remaining recoverable after the purge
rows.plain: Leave as little targeted control-system information recoverable as possible after it is erased. This helps protect information that should no longer be available.
rows.imp_rat: Residual recoverable control-system data after a purge is a decommissioning data-security detail unrelated to cleaning efficacy, with modest consequence; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Targeted data often remains recoverable after purge because verification of complete erasure is rarely performed at disposal. Verified data destruction adds a step often skipped under shutdown time pressure. Confirming data is non-recoverable requires secure-erase expertise disposal crews seldom have. Secure wipe tools are inexpensive, but residual recoverable data creates breach liability cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether all targeted control-system data was removed before release
rows.plain: Be more certain that all data used to control the system has been removed before the system is released. This helps prevent sensitive or unwanted information from remaining behind.
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about control-system data removal before release is a data-governance concern with limited functional bearing, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely a definitive audit trail proving all targeted control data was removed, leaving genuine uncertainty at release. Verifying complete data erasure of control systems is often a slow, manual audit step. Confirming full data removal from PLCs, HMIs and historians requires niche automation and IT security expertise that is scarce on decommissioning teams. Specialized data-wiping and verification tools add cost but are inconsistently applied.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the scheduled purge is delayed because required administrative access is unavailable
rows.plain: The system’s stored information should be deleted on time instead of waiting for special administrative access. This helps complete the cleanup without delay.
rows.imp_rat: Delayed purge due to missing admin access is an operational annoyance, only tangentially central to the CFJ (low relevance), and consequences are recoverable scheduling slips (low severity). Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Access provisioning is ad hoc across IT, facilities, and security, so administrative-access delays recur without a reliable standard process. Scheduled purges commonly slip because badge, system, or approval access is not provisioned in time. Obtaining access is an administrative coordination task requiring little specialized skill. Delay incurs idle-resource cost but no direct material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the control system is de-energized or disconnected before its stored data is purged
rows.plain: Make sure the control system stays powered and connected until all of its saved information has been erased. This prevents the data from being left behind or lost before it is cleared.
rows.imp_rat: De-energizing the controller before data purge is a decommissioning data-handling error marginal to the cleaning job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Control systems are often de-energized or disconnected before data is purged because no reliable sequencing gate enforces it. Data purge is frequently an afterthought squeezed against de-energization deadlines. Secure purging of controllers needs IT/automation skills the disposal crew rarely has. Purge tooling is cheap, but data recovered from a discarded controller can be very costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of residue-contaminated components routed into conventional recycling streams
rows.plain: Reduce the number of parts with leftover material that are sent to ordinary recycling. This helps ensure contaminated parts are handled in a suitable way.
rows.imp_rat: Residue-contaminated components in recycling streams pose a disposal safety concern peripheral to core cleaning function; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Residue checks before routing are inconsistently applied, so contaminated components frequently reach conventional recycling. Verifying components are residue-free before recycling adds inspection and cleaning time. Identifying residue contamination requires knowledge of the chemicals used and where they accumulate. Contaminated components in conventional recycling can trigger rejection fees and environmental liability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recoverable material lots assigned to incorrect recycling streams
rows.plain: Fewer batches of reusable material should be sent to the wrong recycling category. Correct sorting helps the material reach the right recycling process.
rows.imp_rat: Misassigned recyclable lots are an end-of-life sustainability concern peripheral to CIP/SIP function; relevance and severity are both capped at 1 by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Material sorting depends on visual identification and labeling that is inconsistent, so misrouting into wrong streams recurs. Sorting recoverable lots into correct recycling streams adds handling time at teardown. Correct stream assignment requires material identification knowledge that general labor may lack. Misassigned lots lose recovery value and can incur contamination charges, a direct cost loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of recoverable materials discarded with nonrecyclable waste
rows.plain: Keep reusable materials out of ordinary trash and send them for recycling or recovery whenever possible.
rows.imp_rat: Discarding recoverable materials with nonrecyclable waste is an environmental/administrative concern with no bearing on CIP/SIP cleaning efficacy, and its consequences are trivial; held at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Segregation practice is inconsistent, so a meaningful fraction of recoverable material still goes to general waste. Sorting recoverables from waste adds handling time that crews under deadline often skip. Identifying recoverable alloys and components needs material knowledge many crews lack. Recoverable material discarded as waste is direct lost salvage value plus disposal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recycling loads being rejected because required material or decontamination records are missing
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that a recycling company refuses a shipment because records about the material or its cleaning are missing. This helps recyclable materials be accepted.
rows.imp_rat: Recycling loads rejected for missing material or decontamination records is a disposal-stage paperwork issue far removed from the core cleaning function; relevance is peripheral and severity is a logistical inconvenience.
rows.sat_rat: Missing or mismatched records commonly cause recycler rejection because documentation handoff at disposal is poorly standardized. Assembling decontamination records for recyclers adds lead time that is easy to underestimate. Compiling correct material and decon certificates requires document-control familiarity many crews lack. A rejected recycling load wastes transport cost and forces re-handling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that legal ownership transfer is blocked by unresolved liens or conflicting title records
rows.plain: Make sure no unpaid debts or conflicting ownership records prevent the legal transfer of the system.
rows.imp_rat: Legal ownership transfer blocked by liens or title conflicts is a purely legal/administrative error with no connection to the cleaning function; relevance is negligible and severity is a recoverable transaction delay.
rows.sat_rat: Established title and lien verification practice makes blocked ownership transfers relatively rare. Title and lien searches take time but are a standard, scheduled legal step. Handled by legal/asset-management specialists using established procedures. Legal search and clearance fees are modest relative to asset value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required validation and lifecycle records missing from the handover package
rows.plain: Reduce the number of records missing from the package handed over with the system, including records showing that it was checked and maintained over time. This gives the new owner the information needed to operate it properly.
rows.imp_rat: Missing validation/lifecycle records at handover has compliance impact, yet the ceiling caps relevance and severity to the cleaning job at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Handover packages assembled manually from multiple archives routinely have missing documents despite checklists. Assembling the complete validation and lifecycle handover package is time-consuming when records are dispersed. Knowing which validation records are required demands quality and regulatory expertise. Missing records can force expensive reconstruction or re-validation efforts.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that product or cleaning-agent residues remain in the system at custody handover
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that product or cleaning-fluid leftovers remain in the system when it is handed over to the new owner. This helps ensure the system is clean and safe to receive.
rows.imp_rat: Residual product or cleaning-agent at handover is central to CIP/SIP efficacy and carries contamination consequences, but the batch ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1 regardless of intrinsic stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Residual product or cleaning-agent traces frequently remain at custody handover because verification of full clearance is inconsistent. Final cleaning verification before handover adds time that is often underestimated. Confirming residue-free status requires sampling and inspection expertise. Extra flushing and testing add cost, and residues at handover create dispute costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of inventory-listed components and dedicated spares missing at custody handover
rows.plain: Have fewer listed parts and specially kept backup parts missing when the system changes hands. This helps the new owner receive everything they are supposed to receive.
rows.imp_rat: Missing inventory components at custody handover is a logistics defect; relevance and severity are held at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Inventory lists are often outdated and spares scattered, so missing items at handover are a frequent problem. Reconciling inventory and spares at custody handover is a time-consuming counting exercise. Matching physical items to inventory lists requires attention but little specialized skill. Missing components and spares represent lost asset value and replacement cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the ownership handover delay caused by unmet recipient prerequisites
rows.plain: Shorten the wait when handing system ownership to the new owner because the new owner has not yet completed all required preparations. This helps the handover happen without unnecessary delay.
rows.imp_rat: Ownership handover delay from unmet recipient prerequisites is an administrative delay held at 1 relevance and 1 severity by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Prerequisite tracking varies widely by recipient, so unmet conditions commonly surface at handover. Ownership handover stalls when the recipient has not met prerequisites, with delays outside the disposer's control. Managing prerequisite checklists needs project-coordination competence rather than deep expertise. Idle equipment and prolonged custody carry holding costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of treated product-contact surfaces retaining biological or chemical hazards after decontamination
rows.plain: Leave as little biological or chemical danger as possible on surfaces that touch the product after cleaning. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Surfaces retaining hazards after decontamination is genuinely serious, but the mandatory batch ceiling forces both relevance and severity to remain at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validated CIP/SIP cycles plus post-clean sampling reliably drive residual biological and chemical hazards to acceptance limits on accessible surfaces. A validated terminal decontamination cycle takes defined, bounded time. Verifying residual hazard removal uses established swab/rinse sampling handled by trained QC staff. Chemical and testing costs are routine and predictable for a final decon.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surfaces omitted from decontamination before system disassembly, removal, or transfer
rows.plain: Leave fewer product-contact surfaces untreated before the equipment is taken apart, moved, or handed over. This helps protect people and prevent contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting product-contact surfaces from decontamination before disassembly carries real contamination stakes, but the batch ceiling mandates it stay at or below 1.0 for both proxies.
rows.sat_rat: Established terminal-clean SOPs cover most product-contact surfaces, but manual identification of every contact point before teardown leaves occasional omissions. A terminal CIP/SIP cycle can be run before disassembly, adding only a controlled amount of scheduled time. Running a final decontamination cycle uses the same trained operators and existing recipes, so little extra expertise is needed. Reusing the installed CIP skid for a final pass costs little beyond chemicals and utilities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of hazardous decontamination effluent released outside its designated containment boundary
rows.plain: Keep as much contaminated cleaning liquid as possible inside its assigned containment area. This helps prevent dangerous liquid from spreading into surrounding areas.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous decontamination effluent released outside containment is severe for safety/compliance, but the mandatory ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established containment berms and transfer protocols mostly keep effluent contained, though breaches during disconnection still occur. Containment procedures rarely add significant delay when properly staged. Handling hazardous effluent within containment requires trained EHS personnel and correct rigging. Spill containment materials and proper effluent handling add cost but avoid far larger cleanup expenses.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of system handover delay caused by inconclusive decontamination evidence
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to hand over the system when cleaning evidence does not clearly show that product-contact surfaces are free of contamination. This helps the next team take over with confidence.
rows.imp_rat: Handover delay from inconclusive decontamination evidence is a workflow concern kept at 1 relevance and 1 severity per the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard swab/rinse verification usually suffices but ambiguous results still trigger handover delays. Inconclusive decontamination evidence forces re-testing that delays handover of the system. Producing conclusive decon evidence requires QA/EHS staff who know sampling and acceptance criteria. Repeat swabbing and lab analysis add cost when first evidence is inconclusive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required validation, maintenance, and compliance records omitted from the retirement archive
rows.plain: Preserve all required records showing that the equipment was checked, maintained, and handled according to rules when it is retired.
rows.imp_rat: Missing records in a retirement archive is an end-of-life documentation gap with no impact on active cleaning and low consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Retirement archives commonly omit some required records because collection is manual and poorly gated. Compiling the retirement archive competes with teardown timelines and is often rushed. Knowing which validation and compliance records are required needs document-control expertise. Archiving is low cost, but missing records create expensive audit and liability gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived records assigned incorrect retention periods
rows.plain: Keep stored records from being given the wrong length of time to keep them. This helps the organization follow its record-keeping requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect retention periods on archived records is a recordkeeping error tangential to cleaning execution, and the batch ceiling holds it at the minimum.
rows.sat_rat: Retention schedules in EDMS make most assignments correct, though manual selection still produces occasional errors. Assigning retention periods is a quick step within document-control workflows. Correct retention assignment needs records-management knowledge of the applicable schedules. Errors are cheap to make but can trigger costly premature destruction or over-retention.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of archived records linked to the wrong retired system, configuration, or production history
rows.plain: Keep stored records from being linked to the wrong retired system, setup, or production history. This helps people rely on the correct information when checking past operations.
rows.imp_rat: Archived records linked to the wrong retired system is a traceability defect with limited bearing on active cleaning, held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Metadata tagging reduces mislinking, but at retirement of similar systems records are still occasionally linked to the wrong asset or configuration. Linking records correctly is fast when metadata is captured at archiving time. Accurate linkage requires staff who understand the system's configuration and production history. Mislinked records cost little upfront but create expensive retrieval and audit problems later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay in completing the archive beyond the approved system-retirement deadline
rows.plain: Finish storing the required records by the approved deadline for retiring the system. This helps meet recordkeeping requirements and prevents late closeout.
rows.imp_rat: Delay completing the archive past the retirement deadline is administrative record-keeping, remote from cleaning function; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Document-control systems make archiving repeatable, though completion frequently lags the formal deadline. Completing records archiving by the retirement deadline is often the trailing task that slips past schedule. Compiling and validating the archive needs records-management and QA judgement about what must be retained. Archiving labor is modest but under-resourced at project end.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that replacement system cycles fall outside validated cleaning or sterilization limits during handover
rows.plain: Make sure the replacement system's cleaning and sterilizing cycles stay within the approved safe ranges during the changeover. This helps ensure the system remains properly cleaned and safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Replacement cycles falling outside validated limits at handover bears on sterilization validity, yet the mandatory batch ceiling forces both proxies to 1 as it ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Mature validation practice usually keeps handover cycles within limits, though transition drift occasionally slips through. Validating replacement-system cycles at handover follows a defined but time-consuming protocol. Requires validation engineers to confirm cycles stay within approved limits. Validation runs consume utilities and labor as budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required cleaning and sterilization parameters transferred incorrectly to the replacement system
rows.plain: Transfer all required cleaning and sterilizing settings correctly to the replacement system, so it can be operated safely and consistently.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrectly transferred cleaning/sterilization parameters could matter for the replacement, but batch ranking caps both relevance and severity at 1.0 here.
rows.sat_rat: Manual parameter re-entry across systems with no standardized migration path frequently introduces transcription errors that surface only during requalification. Transcribing cleaning/sterilization parameters to a replacement system is a manual, iterative step that can stretch commissioning. Correct transfer of critical CIP/SIP parameters demands process engineers who understand each cycle's rationale, a scarce judgement-heavy task. Errors force revalidation reruns that waste chemicals, utilities, and engineering time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical operational handover tasks left without clear ownership
rows.plain: Avoid leaving important tasks for handing over daily operations without a clearly assigned person. Clear ownership helps ensure nothing is missed during the changeover.
rows.imp_rat: Handover tasks lacking clear ownership is a transition ambiguity that could delay readiness, but the batch ranking caps it at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Handover checklists exist in mature organizations but ownership gaps still routinely slip through, leaving critical tasks unassigned. Clarifying ambiguous handover ownership consumes time in meetings and escalations during the transition. Assigning clear ownership of operational handover tasks depends on a knowledgeable lead who understands who does what, a scarce judgement. Orphaned handover tasks lead to duplicated effort or emergency fixes that inflate disposal costs.

rows.source_job: Migrate system configurations to replacement equipment
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of transferred cleaning cycle parameters and validated settings that differ from the approved source baseline
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning instructions and tested settings moved to the replacement equipment match the approved original settings. This helps ensure the new equipment cleans in the expected way.
rows.imp_rat: Transferred cleaning parameters differing from the approved baseline is a validation concern, but ranking places it below prior batch, capping it at 1.0 despite its relevance.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and baseline comparison practice catch most deviations, though some transferred parameters still differ from the approved source. Comparing transferred parameters to baseline is a defined but time-consuming reconciliation. Requires validation engineers to confirm settings match the approved source baseline. Reconciliation labor is budgeted, and mismatches cause costly rework.

rows.source_job: Migrate system configurations to replacement equipment
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable control configurations that cannot be mapped correctly to the replacement equipment
rows.plain: Make sure each relevant operating setting can be correctly transferred to the replacement equipment. This helps the new equipment work as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Mapping control configurations to replacement equipment is relevant to commissioning continuity, and mis-mapping could cause misconfigured cleaning cycles, but the batch ceiling caps this below all higher-ranked items.
rows.sat_rat: Incompatible platforms and poor documentation mean some configurations reliably fail to map correctly. Unmapped control configurations force manual re-derivation, delaying replacement commissioning. Mapping configurations correctly to new equipment demands scarce automation expertise and interpretation of undocumented logic. Re-engineering unmapped configurations is costly in specialist hours.

rows.source_job: Migrate system configurations to replacement equipment
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of obsolete or retiring-equipment-specific settings transferred to the replacement equipment
rows.plain: Move only the settings needed for the replacement equipment. This prevents old equipment-specific settings from causing problems on the new equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Transferring obsolete equipment settings to replacement equipment carries contamination-risk relevance, but ranked below batch 4, so both proxies are held at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration migration is often done selectively but lacks systematic validation, so obsolete settings sometimes carry over. Preventing transfer of obsolete settings requires checking configurations, adding modest commissioning time. Distinguishing which settings are equipment-specific versus reusable demands specialist configuration knowledge. Wrongly transferred settings can cause replacement equipment errors that are costly to diagnose.

rows.source_job: Migrate system configurations to replacement equipment
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of replacement system unavailability caused by configuration extraction or transfer failures
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for replacement equipment when copying or moving its settings fails. This helps the replacement system become usable sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Replacement-system unavailability from configuration transfer failures is a recovery concern, but the ranking places it below the prior batch, so both relevance and severity stay at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Legacy configuration formats and undocumented parameters make clean extraction/transfer error-prone. Failures extracting or transferring configuration to a replacement system extend downtime unpredictably. Migrating control configurations demands scarce automation engineers familiar with both old and new platforms. Extended replacement unavailability and re-engineering effort are expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of system functions with incorrectly documented operational status at retirement
rows.plain: Avoid recording the wrong working condition of system features when the system is being taken out of service. This helps create an accurate final record.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect operational-status documentation at retirement is a records defect with low bearing on active cleaning; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Status documentation is often done hastily at end of life, so incorrect operational statuses are common. Documenting each function's operational status at retirement requires a systematic walkthrough that takes time. Accurately assessing function status needs operators familiar with the system's behavior. Misdocumented status carries little direct cost but can mislead downstream reuse or disposal decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of condition-critical system areas inaccessible during the final physical assessment
rows.plain: Make sure important parts of the system can be reached during the final inspection, so their condition can be checked properly.
rows.imp_rat: Inaccessible condition-critical areas during final assessment is a peripheral inspection gap here with low relevance and contained severity; batch ordering caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Confined, welded, or insulated sections routinely stay unassessed at retirement, so hidden corrosion or residue goes undetected despite standard inspection practice. Final walkthroughs are scheduled with lockout/tagout time, but blind or embedded zones extend assessment as inspectors improvise access. Locating and reaching condition-critical areas in aging CIP piping demands experienced field engineers who know the system's dead legs and buried runs. Partial teardown or borescope hire to reach hidden zones adds cost, but this is a one-time disposal spend rather than recurring.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of known outstanding defects omitted from the final condition record
rows.plain: Make sure the final condition report includes every known problem that is still unfinished. This gives an honest record of the equipment's condition at the end.
rows.imp_rat: Outstanding defects omitted from the final condition record is a handover-documentation defect peripheral to the cleaning job; capped at 1.0 per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Final condition records rely on memory and scattered maintenance logs, so known defects are commonly omitted. Compiling the final condition record with all defects takes time to gather from various logs. Knowing which outstanding defects matter requires experienced technicians familiar with the equipment history. Omitted defects can transfer hidden costs to buyers or recyclers but cost the disposing party little directly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of facility-specific markings left legible after de-identification
rows.plain: Make sure markings that identify the facility are no longer readable after the system is made anonymous. This helps prevent the facility from being identified from the equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Legible facility markings after de-identification is a decommissioning/privacy detail largely unrelated to the cleaning job and low severity; capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual de-identification frequently misses hidden or embedded markings such as firmware labels and internal tags, so some legible markings routinely remain. Removing facility-specific markings is a quick physical or data task once identified. De-identification is largely straightforward labeling removal requiring little expertise. The materials and labor for removing markings are minor cost items.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required safety, regulatory, or ownership markings are removed unintentionally
rows.plain: Make sure important safety, rule-related, and ownership labels are not removed by accident. These labels help people know what the equipment is, who it belongs to, and how to handle it safely.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended removal of safety or ownership markings is a labeling error peripheral to sterilization performance (low relevance) with limited consequences (low severity); bounded by the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Markings are usually preserved by procedure, but unintentional removal during aggressive teardown still occurs sometimes. Preserving required markings adds minor checks during teardown. Recognizing which markings must be retained needs some regulatory awareness. Re-establishing accidentally removed markings is inexpensive but occasionally required.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transfer or scrapping delay caused by unresolved marking disposition requirements
rows.plain: Shorten the delay in transferring or scrapping equipment while the team is still deciding what to do with its labels and identifying marks. This helps the equipment be moved or discarded sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Transfer or scrapping delay from unresolved marking disposition is a disposal-admin issue held at 1 for both proxies per the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Marking disposition is often handled ad hoc, so unresolved items recur and stall movement. Unresolved marking/labeling disposition (e.g., asset tags, hazard marks) can hold up transfer or scrapping. Determining correct marking disposition needs asset and regulatory-labeling knowledge. Delays incur storage and rescheduling costs but the resolution work is cheap.

rows.source_job: Notify regulatory bodies of system retirement
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory bodies not notified of the system's retirement from regulated service
rows.plain: Make sure every relevant government agency is told when the system is no longer being used for a regulated purpose. This helps keep official records and required notifications up to date.
rows.imp_rat: Failing to notify regulators of retirement is a compliance administrative error remote from the operational cleaning job; the ceiling keeps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance checklists cover the common regulators, but a less-obvious applicable body is sometimes missed. Notifying all regulatory bodies is a scheduled compliance step that rarely adds much time when tracked. Identifying every applicable regulator requires regulatory-affairs expertise that can be thin. The notifications themselves are inexpensive relative to non-compliance penalties.

rows.source_job: Notify regulatory bodies of system retirement
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration a required retirement notification remains unsubmitted after its deadline
rows.plain: Submit the required notice of retirement as soon as possible after its deadline has passed. This helps meet reporting obligations and avoid a prolonged delay.
rows.imp_rat: A late retirement notification is a purely administrative timing lapse remote from the core job; low relevance and low severity, held at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance calendars and QMS tasks make timely submission fairly consistent, with occasional slips. Regulatory retirement notifications are usually submitted within deadline when tracked, but late filings still happen when ownership of the task is unclear. Preparing the notification requires regulatory-affairs knowledge of applicable submission requirements. The filing itself is low-cost, though late submission risks penalties.

rows.source_job: Notify regulatory bodies of system retirement
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies in retirement details across regulatory filings and facility records
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment retirement information is the same in official reports and facility records. This helps ensure the retirement is reported accurately to the authorities.
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancies in retirement details across filings and facility records is an administrative documentation error with low relevance to the sterilization job and modest severity; capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Because regulatory and facility record systems are separate and manually updated, discrepancies between them are a common recurring problem. Aligning retirement details across filings and facility records is a slow manual reconciliation done near project close. Regulatory filing accuracy requires specialized compliance knowledge that not every disposal team has on hand. Discrepancies can trigger regulatory queries and penalties, adding unplanned cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical contamination-control deficiencies are overlooked during supplier assessment
rows.plain: Make sure important problems that could allow contamination are not missed when checking a supplier.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooked contamination-control deficiencies in supplier assessment sit upstream of the cleaning job and its consequences are indirect in this ranking; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier assessments vary widely in rigor and depend on assessor experience, so critical contamination-control gaps are frequently missed. Thorough contamination-control assessment of suppliers takes significant on-site time that is often compressed. Spotting critical contamination-control deficiencies demands rare sterile-processing and microbiology expertise on the assessment team. Overlooked deficiencies translate into expensive remediation or product-quality failures later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier's technical capability is incorrectly judged adequate for sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier truly has the skills and equipment needed to safely fill and finish sterile products. This helps prevent choosing a supplier that cannot meet the required conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Misjudging supplier technical capability for sterile fill-finish is upstream and procedural rather than directly controlling the CIP/SIP cleaning job, so relevance is low; consequences can be serious but are indirect. Batch ceiling caps importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier audits and reference checks reduce error yet overstated vendor claims and thin evidence still let inadequate suppliers pass, keeping this poorly served. Vetting a supplier's technical capability for sterile fill-finish involves audits and references that consume significant pre-award time. Correctly judging capability for aseptic-grade CIP/SIP demands rare cross-disciplinary expertise in sterility assurance, validation and process engineering. Misjudging capability is enormously expensive downstream, but the assessment itself is not a large direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of required manufacturing-control evidence that is missing, outdated, or internally inconsistent
rows.plain: Have fewer required records about how the product is made that are missing, out of date, or contradictory. Consistent, current records help show that the supplier can support proper production.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or inconsistent manufacturing-control evidence is a documentation-integrity issue with low relevance to running cycles; batch ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manufacturing-control evidence is frequently incomplete, outdated or contradictory in submissions, so the assurance it provides is unreliable. Assembling and cross-checking manufacturing-control evidence is a slow document-gathering exercise across suppliers. Judging whether control evidence is complete and internally consistent requires quality-assurance expertise that is in short supply. Missing or inconsistent evidence forces costly re-audits and re-submissions later in the buy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for supplier responses and assessment access needed to complete qualification
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for suppliers to answer questions and allow the checks needed to approve them. This helps determine sooner whether a supplier is qualified.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for supplier responses and assessment access during qualification is a procurement-timeline issue, low relevance to the CFJ and low in severity. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Responses eventually arrive and qualification usually completes, so the outcome is fairly certain even though its timing is unpredictable. Supplier response and audit/assessment scheduling for CIP/SIP vendor qualification routinely stretch weeks to months with no reliable way to compress the wait, and buyers eat the delay through follow-up chasing. Chasing responses and interpreting fragmented supplier documentation demands experienced procurement and QA judgement, but the bottleneck is the waiting itself rather than a lack of skill. The waiting consumes staff hours and can delay downstream commissioning, but the direct monetary spend on qualification effort is modest relative to the schedule pain.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of supplier delivery, service-coverage, and parts-support claims that cannot be corroborated
rows.plain: Make sure suppliers can support their claims about delivery, service coverage, and spare-parts help with reliable evidence. This helps show that the supplier can provide support after the purchase.
rows.imp_rat: Uncorroborated supplier delivery and support claims are a vendor-vetting concern, low relevance to the core sterilization job and low in immediate severity. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier delivery, service and parts claims are seldom independently verifiable, leaving a persistent trust gap. Corroborating supplier service and parts-support claims takes extended reference-checking time. Assessing the credibility of delivery and support claims needs procurement experience. Uncorroborated support claims can lead to costly downtime if support proves inadequate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a transport exception is reported after site receiving preparations must change
rows.plain: Make sure any change in receiving plans is reported before it causes a problem with the shipment. This helps the receiving site prepare for the delivery.
rows.imp_rat: Late transport-exception reporting is a logistics-coordination error remote from cleaning execution (low relevance) causing adjustable prep changes (low severity); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Shipment tracking and carrier notifications catch most exceptions early, but occasional late reports still disrupt receiving readiness. Late transport exceptions force rushed re-planning of receiving after preparations are already set. Managing transport exception communication is routine logistics coordination work. Reworking receiving preparations wastes labor and equipment rental but is bounded in cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shipment status updates that are stale or inaccurate during transit
rows.plain: Keep shipment updates accurate and current while the shipment is on the way. This helps people know where the shipment really is and when to expect it.
rows.imp_rat: Stale shipment status updates during transit are a logistics-visibility issue, largely disconnected from cleaning/sterilization performance (low relevance) and low in severity. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Tracking systems are broadly deployed but updates can still be stale or inaccurate for specialized heavy equipment. Modern logistics tracking gives fairly timely shipment updates, though gaps and delays still occur in transit. Reading tracking status requires little specialized skill. Stale status causes minor coordination waste rather than large direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of changes to the expected delivery date after receiving resources are scheduled
rows.plain: Keep the expected arrival date from changing after staff and equipment have been scheduled to receive the shipment. This helps the receiving team plan its work.
rows.imp_rat: Changes to expected delivery dates after receiving scheduling is a logistics-planning nuisance with low relevance to CIP/SIP cleaning efficacy and minor severity; capped per batch-5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor delivery commitments for engineered systems are frequently missed, so date changes remain common despite expediting. Delivery-date slippage after receiving is scheduled directly wastes booked labor and dock time and delays installation. Coordinating vendor lead times and receiving logistics needs an experienced expeditor, a role often thinly staffed. Rescheduling receiving crews and idle staging space incurs real but bounded cost penalties.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that shipment updates cannot be matched to the correct cleaning and sterilization in place system because of inconsistent identifiers
rows.plain: Make sure shipment updates can be linked to the correct cleaning and sterilizing system, even when different identification numbers are used. This prevents records from being attached to the wrong system.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent identifiers preventing shipment-update matching is a low-stakes logistics tracking error, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: ERP tag and PO-number conventions handle most matching reliably, though inconsistent supplier identifiers still cause occasional mismatches. Mismatched identifiers force manual reconciliation that occasionally delays acting on shipment updates. Matching shipment records to the right system is clerical work needing little specialized skill. Identifier errors rarely cause direct spend beyond minor rework time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting shipment status updates received from shipping and receiving parties
rows.plain: Receive shipment updates that agree with one another. This helps staff know where the shipment is and when it will arrive.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting shipment status updates is a logistics-coordination ambiguity with minimal relevance to sterilization efficacy and low severity; capped at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Disconnected shipper and receiver systems commonly produce conflicting status, an unresolved consistency problem. Conflicting shipment updates cause wasted follow-up calls and coordination delay. Reconciling shipper and receiver status needs attentive expediting but little specialized skill. Confusion rarely costs money directly beyond coordination effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of relevant CIP/SIP configurations and enabling technologies omitted from the market survey
rows.plain: Include all important equipment and methods for cleaning and sterilizing the system in the market review. This helps ensure that suitable options are not missed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting CIP/SIP configurations from a market survey affects breadth of options but is peripheral to actually cleaning and sterilizing in place (low relevance), and the consequence is a suboptimal shortlist rather than harm (low severity). Capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Market surveys routinely miss relevant configurations because coverage depends on the surveyor's awareness, making completeness inconsistent. Broadening the survey to capture omitted configurations lengthens the market-scanning phase. Knowing which CIP/SIP configurations and enabling technologies exist to include requires specialist market knowledge that is scarce. Omitting viable configurations can lock in a costlier or suboptimal solution.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that configuration information used in the research is outdated or inaccurate
rows.plain: Make sure the information used to compare system choices is current and correct. Wrong information could lead to choosing an unsuitable option.
rows.imp_rat: Outdated configuration research data is peripheral to executing CIP/SIP and its effect is a modest rework risk; ranking holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration data collected during research is frequently outdated or vendor-optimistic, with no standard freshness check, so inaccuracies persist. Verifying that configuration information is current requires chasing suppliers for revision status, slowing research. Detecting outdated or inaccurate configuration data needs technical reviewers who know what current specs should look like. Decisions based on stale configuration data cause costly mis-purchases and redesigns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity caused by inconsistent terminology across descriptions of available configurations
rows.plain: Use clear, consistent words when describing the different system choices. This makes it easier to understand what each choice includes and compare them accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent terminology across configuration descriptions is a minor clarity issue far from the core job; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: No shared configuration vocabulary exists across suppliers, so ambiguity in comparing available options is a chronic, poorly mitigated problem. Inconsistent terminology across vendor configuration descriptions forces clarification loops that slow evaluation. Interpreting divergent nomenclature demands experienced engineers who can translate between vendor vocabularies. Terminology confusion rarely costs money directly beyond wasted evaluation effort.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of surveyed options lacking information relevant to sterile fill-finish requirements
rows.plain: Avoid considering options when important information about filling and finishing products without germs is missing. This helps ensure the chosen system fits the actual need.
rows.imp_rat: Surveyed options lacking fill-finish info is an early-selection information gap with low centrality to the cleaning job and low consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor market materials routinely omit sterile fill-finish specifics, so surveyed options arrive with inconsistent, gap-ridden information that corrupts the comparison. Chasing missing fill-finish-relevant details from vendors during a market survey stretches the evaluation timeline unpredictably. It takes an experienced sterile-processing specialist to even recognize which fill-finish requirements an option's datasheet fails to address, and such judgement is scarce. The cost of gathering the missing information is modest, but selecting an under-informed option risks expensive downstream rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for technical information needed to complete the landscape survey
rows.plain: Receive the technical details needed to finish comparing the available choices without a long wait. This helps the person conducting the survey make an informed comparison.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting on technical info for a landscape survey is an early planning delay with minor operational impact; capped per batch instruction.
rows.sat_rat: Standard survey templates help, but incomplete or slow technical inputs regularly delay completion. Gathering technical information for the landscape survey often stalls on slow supplier or internal responses. Knowing what technical data to collect requires competent engineering input but not rare expertise. Survey delays consume staff time more than money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that integration with existing piping creates trapped fluid in product-contact paths
rows.plain: Make sure the connections to existing pipes do not leave liquid trapped in areas that touch the product, where it could cause problems.
rows.imp_rat: Trapped fluid in product-contact paths is highly relevant to CIP/SIP cleaning efficacy and severe as a contamination source, but the batch ceiling caps this below prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Existing P&ID reviews and slope checks catch obvious cases yet integration-with-legacy-piping surprises still recur frequently, leaving this need only weakly served today. Detecting drainability and dead-leg risks during acquisition requires iterative 3D routing reviews that stretch the pre-purchase timeline and rarely converge quickly. Spotting future trapped-fluid paths at tie-in points demands scarce sanitary-piping and hygienic-design engineers who are not always on the acquisition team. Undetected trapped-fluid paths translate into expensive post-installation rework, but that cost lands after acquisition rather than during it.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that candidate cycle conditions exceed the pressure, temperature, or flow limits of existing equipment and piping
rows.plain: Make sure the proposed operating conditions do not put more pressure, heat, or liquid movement through the existing equipment and pipes than they can safely handle. This helps prevent damage or unsafe operation.
rows.imp_rat: Candidate cycles exceeding equipment pressure/temperature limits could harm assets, but the mandatory batch ceiling forces both proxies to 1 as it ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility with existing equipment is often assumed rather than verified during acquisition, so limit breaches surface during commissioning. Checking candidate cycle conditions against existing equipment limits requires slow engineering review often deferred. Judging whether pressure, temperature, and flow exceed installed-base limits demands experienced process and mechanical engineers. Exceeding existing equipment limits forces expensive piping and vessel upgrades if caught late.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of connection, dimension, material, or rating mismatches overlooked during the compatibility evaluation
rows.plain: Catch differences in connections, size, materials, or safe operating limits during the compatibility check. This helps prevent equipment from being connected or used when it is not suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Overlooked connection or rating mismatches in compatibility evaluation is an upstream engineering-check error with moderate relevance and contained severity; capped at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual compatibility review misses interface mismatches often enough to remain a notable risk. Compatibility rechecks late in the process add delay when mismatches surface. Spotting connection, material, and rating mismatches across CIP/SIP interfaces demands deep multidisciplinary engineering judgement. Overlooked mismatches force costly rework or replacement parts.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required facility interface data that is missing or outdated during the evaluation
rows.plain: Make sure the information about how the equipment connects with the facility is complete and up to date during the review. This helps determine whether the equipment will work properly with the facility.
rows.imp_rat: Missing or outdated facility interface data affects evaluation accuracy but is administrative to the CFJ (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity). Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Facility interface data is frequently missing or stale during evaluation, so the basis for judgement is unreliable. Obtaining and refreshing facility interface data mid-evaluation causes waiting and rescheduling. Interpreting facility interface data correctly requires facility engineers whose time is contested. Evaluating against outdated interface data leads to costly misfit selections.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for access to inspect existing lines, vessels, and tie-in points
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to inspect the existing pipes, containers, and connection points. This helps determine sooner whether the new equipment will fit and work with what is already there.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for access to inspect lines and tie-in points delays assessment but is a scheduling issue tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with recoverable impact (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Access timing is inconsistent because it hinges on production schedules and safety approvals outside the buyer's control. Access to inspect existing lines and tie-in points typically waits for production windows and clearances, causing real delay. Coordinating and performing the inspection needs qualified engineers but the wait is the binding constraint. Waiting ties up evaluation resources but incurs limited direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required technical parameters absent from candidate system specifications
rows.plain: Reduce the number of system descriptions that leave out important required details. This helps people compare the systems fairly and choose one that meets their needs.
rows.imp_rat: Missing technical parameters in candidate specs is peripheral to actually cleaning/sterilizing (low relevance) and its consequences are absorbable procurement gaps (low severity); batch ceiling caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Candidate specs commonly arrive with parameters absent, a recurring completeness failure. Missing technical parameters trigger clarification cycles that slow comparison. Knowing which parameters must be present takes engineering domain expertise. Incomplete specs risk mis-buys with cost consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of comparing cleaning coverage, flow, temperature, or pressure ratings derived under non-equivalent operating conditions
rows.plain: Compare cleaning performance and operating limits only when the systems were tested under similar conditions. Otherwise, the comparison may give a misleading picture of how well they work or what pressure, temperature, and liquid flow they can handle.
rows.imp_rat: Comparing performance ratings under non-equivalent conditions is a specification-evaluation error affecting purchasing decisions, not the operational cleaning job itself; relevance is peripheral and consequences are limited to procurement misjudgment.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers report performance under differing assumptions and no standard basis forces normalization, so misleading comparisons are frequent. Normalizing performance ratings across non-equivalent conditions is a slow analytical exercise often skipped under time pressure. Recognizing non-equivalent test bases for coverage, flow, and pressure demands deep process-engineering judgement. Comparing apples-to-oranges ratings leads to buying under- or over-sized systems, wasting capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate specification entries whose units, reference points, or rating definitions are ambiguous
rows.plain: Avoid comparing machine details that use unclear units, starting points, or definitions for performance ratings. Clear definitions help people understand which option actually performs better.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous units or rating definitions in candidate specs is an upstream procurement-clarity error with limited relevance to executing cleaning and low immediate severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Free-text specifications routinely carry ambiguous reference points, making misinterpretation a persistent quality failure. Ambiguous units or rating definitions force clarification rounds that slow evaluation. Writing unambiguous, referenced spec entries requires disciplined engineering rigor unevenly present across teams. Ambiguity rarely costs hard dollars directly but drives rework and mis-buys.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of shortlisted system performance claims lacking traceable supporting evidence
rows.plain: Make sure claims about how well a system works are supported by evidence that can be traced back to a reliable source. This makes it easier to judge whether the claims are trustworthy.
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported performance claims from shortlisted systems affect due diligence quality but are peripheral to executing the CFJ (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Performance claims frequently lack traceable evidence, so their trustworthiness varies widely across vendors. Tracing evidence behind shortlisted performance claims is a slow verification effort. Judging whether supporting evidence truly backs a performance claim requires technical scrutiny that few evaluators apply rigorously. Unsubstantiated claims can drive an expensive wrong choice.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of candidate specification revisions after the formal comparison has begun
rows.plain: Avoid changing the list of requirements after the formal comparison of options has started. This keeps the comparison fair and consistent.
rows.imp_rat: Specification revisions after comparison begins is a selection-process rework with limited operational stakes, held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Specifications for CIP/SIP systems are commonly still maturing when comparison begins, so revisions mid-stream are a recurring, poorly controlled failure. Mid-comparison spec revisions force teams to re-evaluate candidates, stretching the selection cycle. Freezing a specification correctly upfront requires disciplined requirements engineering that is inconsistently applied. Rework of comparison matrices consumes engineering hours but rarely large hard-dollar spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable sanitary, safety, or regulatory requirements omitted from the compliance assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the review includes every relevant cleanliness, safety, and government requirement. This helps prevent overlooked rules from causing problems later.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting sanitary/regulatory requirements from a compliance assessment carries real severity, but the ranking places it below batch 4, so both proxies are held at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature regulatory checklists and QA review make omissions uncommon though not eliminated for edge-case requirements. Compliance gaps surface late and cause time-consuming reassessment, but standard checklists catch most upfront. Identifying all applicable FDA/EHEDG/ -A/pressure-vessel requirements demands scarce regulatory expertise. An omitted requirement discovered post-award can trigger expensive retrofits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that sanitary design deviations in candidate systems remain undetected during pre-purchase qualification
rows.plain: Make sure any design problems that could make the equipment difficult to keep clean are found before buying it.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected sanitary design deviations during pre-purchase qualification are relevant and potentially severe for contamination control, yet the ceiling holds this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and standards exist yet deviations still slip through inconsistent supplier documentation, so undetected non-conformances remain a recurring risk. Pre-purchase sanitary-design qualification requires document review and sometimes factory visits that add weeks to the decision window. Detecting sanitary-design deviations against ASME BPE and EHEDG norms depends heavily on experienced hygienic-design reviewers who are in short supply. Missing a deviation before purchase drives costly change orders, but the qualification effort itself is a modest line item.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the intended sterile fill-finish use conditions used to determine applicable requirements
rows.plain: Clearly describe the conditions in which products will be filled into containers without germs, so the right rules can be identified. This helps ensure the planned work follows the rules that apply to it.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in sterile fill-finish use conditions affects requirement scoping, but per the batch ceiling it ranks below prior items; relevance and severity are both capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: User requirement specifications provide a framework, but underspecified use conditions still frequently propagate ambiguity into requirements. Pinning down intended sterile fill-finish use conditions can require repeated internal alignment that delays requirement-setting. Translating process use conditions into applicable CIP/SIP requirements needs scarce process-and-sterility judgement. Ambiguity here mainly wastes effort rather than direct money during acquisition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required compliance evidence missing from candidate-system submissions
rows.plain: Include all required proof that the proposed system follows the relevant rules, so its compliance can be checked before approval.
rows.imp_rat: Missing compliance evidence in candidate submissions is a procurement-documentation gap, low relevance to the core cleaning/sterilization job and low in immediate severity. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compliance evidence is often partial in candidate submissions, so requirement checklists help but gaps recur. Collecting compliance evidence from candidates adds submission-review time to the evaluation. Determining which compliance evidence is required and adequate needs regulatory expertise not always on hand. Missing compliance evidence risks costly late disqualification or remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for requested compliance records and clarifications from candidate-system suppliers
rows.plain: Receive the suppliers’ required safety and rule-following records, along with answers to questions, without a long delay. This helps the buyer determine whether each system meets the required rules.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for supplier compliance records is a procurement delay peripheral to running cleaning cycles, with modest consequences; it sits below the prior batch and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Document-request practices exist but supplier responsiveness varies widely, so delays in receiving records are common. Waiting for compliance records and clarifications from suppliers is a recurring, often lengthy bottleneck in qualification. Requesting the right compliance records takes regulatory-savvy staff to specify precisely what is needed. Waiting costs schedule time rather than direct money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in cost boundaries, evaluation horizons, and financial assumptions across candidate systems
rows.plain: Compare all possible systems using the same cost limits, time period, and financial assumptions, so the cost comparison is fair.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in cost boundaries and financial assumptions across systems is an evaluation error capped at 1 relevance and 1 severity by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: No standardized costing framework across vendors exists, so inconsistency in financial assumptions remains a persistent, weakly served problem. Normalizing cost boundaries and horizons across candidates is labor-intensive and slows comparison. Building comparable financial models requires cost-engineering skill that acquisition teams don't always have. Inconsistent cost boundaries and assumptions distort budget comparisons and can lead to misallocated capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of variation in projected ownership costs across plausible throughput, cycle, utility-price, and consumable-price assumptions
rows.plain: Be more certain about the total cost of owning the equipment under realistic changes in output, work time, energy prices, and supply prices. This helps prevent unexpected ownership costs.
rows.imp_rat: Variation in projected ownership costs across assumptions is a financial-modeling error kept at 1 for both proxies by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: TCO templates exist but sensitivity to consumable, cycle and utility-price assumptions is rarely modeled robustly, leaving projections volatile. Running ownership-cost scenarios across throughput and utility-price assumptions is time-consuming when done properly. Credible total-cost-of-ownership modeling under varied assumptions needs scarce analytical expertise. Wide variation in projected ownership costs undermines capital decisions and can lock in higher lifetime spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for validated cost and usage inputs from suppliers and internal stakeholders
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for suppliers and coworkers to confirm the costs and expected use of an option. This helps compare its full cost over time sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for validated cost and usage inputs delays financial analysis, peripheral to the cleaning/sterilization job (low relevance) with recoverable impact (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Inputs are often preliminary or inconsistent when they finally arrive, forcing revisions, so both the timing and the trustworthiness of the data are only moderately dependable. Waiting for validated cost and usage inputs from suppliers and internal stakeholders is a recurring back-and-forth that stalls acquisition decisions, though standard RFQ templates and quote cycles give partial predictability. Assembling and validating total-cost and utility-usage inputs for CIP/SIP requires cross-functional expertise, but the core failure here is delay in getting the numbers, not the skill to use them. Late or missing validated inputs risk mis-sized budgets and rework, so there is real cost exposure tied directly to this waiting.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical process, utility, compliance, and validation requirements omitted from the quotation request
rows.plain: Avoid leaving important production, utility, legal, or testing needs out of the request sent to potential suppliers. Including them helps suppliers provide complete and comparable offers.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical requirements from the quotation request risks downstream gaps, but the batch ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: RFQ completeness depends on manual templates and reviews that regularly leave gaps. Requoting after a requirement is found missing from the RFQ restarts the cycle. Assembling complete process, utility, compliance, and validation requirements demands broad cross-functional expertise. Requirements added after quoting inflate price and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of delay between the requested and actual supplier submission dates
rows.plain: Have the supplier send its response by the requested date instead of later. This helps the buyer compare offers and make a decision on time.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between requested and actual supplier submission dates is a schedule-admin issue held at 1 for both relevance and severity under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: RFP deadlines and follow-up cadences make timely submission fairly consistent, though slippage against requested dates still occurs. Suppliers commonly submit later than requested, extending the evaluation timeline. Managing submission deadlines is procurement coordination requiring little scarce skill. Late submissions rarely cost money directly but can compress downstream schedules.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of candidate suppliers that do not receive the quotation request
rows.plain: Make sure every possible supplier receives the request for prices and a work proposal. Otherwise, a suitable supplier may be left out of the competition.
rows.imp_rat: Suppliers not receiving a quotation request is a procurement-process gap with minimal relevance to sterilization performance and low severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Approved-vendor lists and procurement systems make it fairly reliable that intended suppliers receive the request. Maintained bidder lists let buyers issue RFQs quickly to the intended pool. Knowing the qualified CIP/SIP supplier universe takes category expertise but is largely codified in AVLs. Missing a supplier forgoes competitive pricing but is not a direct cost outlay.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the CIP/SIP system being damaged during transport or rigging
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the system that cleans and sterilizes equipment while it is being moved or lifted. Careful handling protects the system before installation.
rows.imp_rat: Transport or rigging damage is a logistics error outside routine cleaning operation (low relevance) though it can be costly; the batch ceiling caps relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Mature crating, securement, and specialized transport practices make transit or rigging damage to CIP/SIP skids relatively uncommon. Transport damage inspection at receiving is a standard, quick step that rarely delays the project unless damage is found. Proper crating and handling rely on established freight expertise rather than the acquirer's own skill. Transport insurance and packaging standards limit financial exposure from damage.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts in CIP/SIP system weight and dimension information before dispatch
rows.plain: Resolve differences in the system’s recorded weight and size before it is shipped. This helps ensure the equipment can be transported, received, and installed safely.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicts in weight/dimension info before dispatch is a logistics-data error with low relevance to sterilization outcomes and modest consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established pre-dispatch checks catch most weight and dimension issues, but occasional stale drawings still let conflicts through. Confirming weights and dimensions before dispatch is a quick check when drawings exist, though clarifications can add days. Reading fabrication drawings for weight and dimensional data is routine engineering work needing modest specialized skill. Catching dimensional conflicts before dispatch avoids costly re-shipment and rigging surprises.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the CIP/SIP system waits because delivery, rigging, and site-readiness schedules are misaligned
rows.plain: Reduce waiting caused by mismatched delivery, lifting, and site-preparation schedules. This helps the cleaning and sterilizing system arrive when the people and site are ready for it.
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned delivery and site-readiness schedules is a logistics delay with recoverable consequences; the batch ceiling keeps it low.
rows.sat_rat: Project schedules attempt to synchronize these streams but misalignment across delivery, rigging and site readiness remains a common source of waiting. Systems frequently wait when delivery, rigging and site-readiness schedules fall out of sync, adding idle time. Aligning these schedules requires project-coordination skill but not scarce expertise. Misalignment drives demurrage, storage and re-mobilization costs that acquisition teams absorb.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of contractually required documents missing from the handover package
rows.plain: Make sure every document required by the contract is included when the work is handed over, so nothing important is missing.
rows.imp_rat: Missing contractual handover documents is a delivery-paperwork gap with low relevance to sterilization and absorbable severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contractual document checklists exist and catch many gaps, but packages routinely still arrive with some required documents missing. Reconciling the handover package against the contract list is a bounded but often iterative back-and-forth with the vendor. Checking documents against a contractual deliverables list needs contract-administration diligence more than deep technical skill. Missing handover documents can withhold payment milestones and cost money to chase after acceptance.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of revision mismatches among the received engineering drawings
rows.plain: Reduce the number of received engineering drawings that show different versions of the same information. This helps ensure everyone is working from the correct and most consistent drawings.
rows.imp_rat: Revision mismatches among engineering drawings are a documentation-quality issue with low relevance to running clean/sterile cycles and limited severity; the batch ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Revision-control practices exist but drawing sets still arrive with mismatched revisions often enough to matter. Revision mismatches in drawings cause rework and review delays. Detecting revision mismatches needs document-control diligence more than deep skill. Building from an outdated revision can be costly but is usually caught.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of certificates that cannot be traced to delivered system components
rows.plain: Make sure each certificate can be connected to the system part it belongs to. This helps prove that the delivered parts meet the required conditions.
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable certificates to delivered components is a documentation-traceability gap with limited relevance to cleaning execution and contained severity; held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Certificate-to-component traceability is frequently broken in status-quo paper/PDF workflows, a recurring handover defect. Chasing untraceable certificates at handover consumes significant time. Matching material and calibration certs to specific components requires meticulous documentation control. Untraceable certs can force re-testing or component replacement at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of received validation protocols lacking required approval evidence
rows.plain: Accept fewer testing documents that do not show the required approvals. This helps confirm that the planned checks have been properly reviewed.
rows.imp_rat: Validation protocols lacking approval evidence is a documentation-compliance defect with low centrality to the cleaning execution and capped severity; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard document review catches most unapproved protocols, though some still slip through without complete approval evidence. Confirming approval signatures on received protocols is a fast verification step when protocols are delivered. Checking for required approval evidence is a procedural QA task requiring modest specialized judgement. Protocols lacking approvals cause rework and revalidation costs if used as-is.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of delivered documentation files that cannot be opened or read during handover review
rows.plain: Make sure every delivered document can be opened and read during the handover review. Unusable files can leave important information unavailable.
rows.imp_rat: Unreadable documentation files at handover are a review nuisance; relevance and severity to the core job are both held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard PDF handover and turnover-package practices make unreadable files fairly uncommon though not rare. Unreadable files stall handover review until replacements arrive. Verifying file integrity needs little specialized skill, just a review step. Corrupt or proprietary-format files cost little beyond re-request effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of registration rejection due to a mismatch between the delivered CIP/SIP system identifiers and supplier records
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that warranty and service coverage is rejected because the equipment numbers do not match the supplier’s records. This helps ensure the correct equipment receives coverage.
rows.imp_rat: Registration identifier mismatch is an administrative supplier-record error peripheral to actually cleaning and sterilizing (low relevance); consequences are correctable paperwork rework (low severity). Capped at 1.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented handover and asset-registration procedures usually catch identifier mismatches, though occasional transcription errors slip through. Verifying serial and identifier matches against supplier records is a quick administrative step. Cross-checking identifiers is low-skill clerical work requiring only diligence. A registration rejection is cheap to fix but can delay commissioning; costs are minor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for required ownership and acceptance documentation after receipt
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for the ownership papers and acceptance records needed after equipment arrives. This helps ensure that service and warranty protection are recorded promptly.
rows.imp_rat: Delays in ownership and acceptance documentation are administrative, disconnected from the core CFJ (low relevance) with minor consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Post-receipt documentation frequently arrives late and incomplete, so turnaround is inconsistent. Ownership and acceptance documentation often lags receipt, causing waits before formal close-out. Processing acceptance paperwork requires administrative rather than scarce technical skill. Delayed documentation can hold payment or asset registration but has limited direct cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of the gap in warranty and service coverage after the system comes under site responsibility
rows.plain: Avoid a long gap in repair and support coverage after the site becomes responsible for the system. This helps ensure the system can still receive needed help.
