rows.imp_rat: A warranty/service coverage gap after handover is administrative and peripheral to the actual cleaning/sterilization job, with low consequences; capped at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Warranty start dates and site-responsibility handover are commonly misaligned, leaving intermittent coverage gaps that standard contracts don't always close. Negotiating continuous warranty coverage is usually resolved within standard contract timelines when planned early. Structuring service coverage transitions is routine procurement work requiring modest contract expertise. Coverage gaps expose the site to unplanned repair costs during the vulnerable early ownership period.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of contracted warranty and service entitlements omitted from the supplier coverage record
rows.plain: Make sure all promised warranty protection and service benefits are recorded in the supplier coverage record. This helps ensure the buyer can receive everything that was paid for or agreed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted warranty/service entitlements from supplier records are administrative, with low relevance to actually cleaning/sterilizing and low severity, and the ceiling holds both at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage records are inconsistently maintained, so entitlement omissions recur. Reconstructing missing warranty entitlements later delays coverage decisions. Capturing all warranty and service terms from contracts requires careful contract-administration attention. Omitted entitlements mean paying for service that should be covered, a direct cost leak.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical spare parts, seals, or consumables omitted from the initial kit
rows.plain: Make sure the first spare-parts kit includes all the important replacement parts, seals, and supplies needed. This helps prevent delays when something needs to be replaced.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted critical spares/seals/consumables affects serviceability, but relevance and severity to the cleaning job are held at 1 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Initial spare kits are frequently incomplete relative to actual failure-prone items. Missing spares discovered at commissioning cause downtime waiting on parts. Selecting critical seals, spares, and consumables takes maintenance and OEM knowledge. Emergency later purchases of omitted spares cost more than kitting upfront.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that initial kit items are incompatible with the delivered CIP/SIP system configuration
rows.plain: Make sure the first replacement parts fit the delivered cleaning and sterilizing system, so they can be used when needed.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible initial kit items is a commissioning inconvenience with limited direct impact on the cleaning job and contained consequence; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier bill-of-materials and kit verification generally ensure compatibility, though occasional configuration-to-kit mismatches still occur. Confirming initial kit compatibility with the delivered configuration is a quick check against packing lists. Matching kit items to configuration requires modest technical familiarity rather than deep expertise. Incompatible kit items cause minor re-ordering costs and short delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the initial spare parts and consumables kit remains unavailable after the system is ready for commissioning
rows.plain: Have the first set of spare parts and supplies ready when the system is ready to start, so setup is not held up.
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable spare-parts kit at commissioning delays startup but is a logistics gap tangential to the CFJ (low relevance) with correctable consequences (low severity). Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Occasional long-lead components or under-specified kits still leave gaps at commissioning, so availability at the exact moment of readiness is not fully guaranteed. Initial spare-parts and consumables kits are commonly quoted and ordered alongside the main system so recommended-spares lists and lead-time planning usually close most of the gap by commissioning readiness. Vendors provide standard recommended-spares packages, so relatively little specialized judgement is needed to specify the initial kit. Spares kits are a known line item budgeted with the capital purchase, keeping cost surprises limited even if long-lead items add expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of initial kit items supplied without required material, traceability, or compliance documentation
rows.plain: Make sure every item in the starter spare-parts kit comes with the required material information, proof of where it came from, and compliance documents. This prevents parts from being unusable or difficult to verify.
rows.imp_rat: Kit items lacking traceability/compliance docs is a documentation gap; relevance and severity to the cleaning job are held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Kit documentation completeness is inconsistently enforced, so gaps recur. Chasing missing documentation for kit items delays acceptance. Ensuring each kit item carries material/traceability docs needs careful receiving discipline. Undocumented items may need replacement, costing money.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which the quoted initial kit cost exceeds the approved acquisition budget
rows.plain: Keep the quoted cost of the first set of replacement parts within the approved budget. This helps avoid overspending when starting up.
rows.imp_rat: Quoted kit cost exceeding acquisition budget is a purely financial concern unrelated to the cleaning job; both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Line-item quotes make budget comparison fairly dependable, with residual overruns from optional add-ons. Comparing quoted kit cost to the acquisition budget is quick within the quoting cycle. Reading and normalizing a kit quote needs routine estimating skill. Initial kit quotes sometimes exceed the approved budget when options and instrumentation are bundled, though this is usually visible early.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical cleaning and sterilization capabilities omitted from the system requirements
rows.plain: Avoid leaving important cleaning and sterilizing abilities out of the system’s requirements. Clearly listing them helps ensure the system can clean and sterilize as needed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical cleaning/sterilization capabilities from requirements is genuinely central, but the batch ranking forces relevance and severity to remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement capture relies on expert memory and templates, so critical capabilities are omitted often enough to matter. Discovering a missing cleaning/sterilization capability late forces re-scoping and delay. Defining the full set of critical CIP/SIP capabilities requires scarce process and hygienic-design expertise. A missed capability can require an expensive system change order.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of system requirements based on incorrect site utility and process-interface data
rows.plain: Avoid setting system needs using incorrect information about the site’s available services and how the process connects to other equipment. This helps ensure the planned system will work at the site.
rows.imp_rat: Requirements built on incorrect utility/process-interface data is a design-input error with low relevance to executing cycles and moderate but capped severity; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Site utility data is frequently outdated or inaccurate in typical practice, so requirements are routinely anchored to incorrect assumptions. Verifying utility and process-interface data against the actual site adds survey visits and slows requirement definition. Correctly reconciling as-built utility conditions with process interfaces demands seasoned facility and process engineers whose attention is thinly spread. Requirements built on wrong utility data drive costly change orders and mismatched equipment purchases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among process, quality, validation, and production requirements
rows.plain: Resolve disagreements about process, quality, testing, and production needs before defining what the system must do. This helps create requirements that all affected teams can support.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among process/quality/validation requirements is a specification-alignment error with low direct relevance to cleaning execution; batch ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement conflicts commonly persist unnoticed until late because there is no reliable integrated review, undermining a consistent specification. Surfacing and resolving cross-functional conflicts requires multiple review cycles that lengthen the acquisition schedule. Reconciling process, quality, validation and production positions needs skilled cross-disciplinary facilitation that few teams staff well. Unresolved conflicts discovered late trigger rework and disputes that inflate project cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time spent waiting for approved process and facility inputs needed to define system requirements
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for approved information about the work process and facility before deciding what the system must do. This helps define the right system sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for approved process/facility inputs to define requirements is a planning delay, peripheral to executing cleaning/sterilization (low relevance) and low severity. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Input delivery timing is unpredictable, depending on other departments' approval cycles. Waiting for approved process and facility inputs is a chronic bottleneck that stalls requirement definition. Producing the inputs needs scarce process and facility experts whose availability drives the wait. The delay mainly costs schedule slippage rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to address misaligned or incompatible interfaces between the piping and equipment ports
rows.plain: Spend less time fixing connections that do not line up or do not fit between pipes and equipment. Properly matching the connections helps the piping work safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned piping/equipment interfaces do delay CIP/SIP integration, but the batch ceiling caps this item; relevance to the core cleaning job and severity of consequences are both held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional checks and 3D models reduce misalignment, but fabrication tolerances and field conditions still leave interfaces needing time-consuming correction. Reworking misaligned piping-to-port interfaces means field re-fabrication or re-fitting that directly and significantly extends the schedule. Diagnosing and correcting misalignment requires skilled fitters and sometimes redesign, making resolution expertise-dependent. Field re-welding, spool refabrication and adapters add unplanned material and labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required cleaning or sterilization stages omitted from the recipe for each process circuit
rows.plain: Include every required cleaning and sterilizing step in the instructions for each connected part of the process. This helps ensure the equipment is properly cleaned and safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required cleaning or sterilization stages from the recipe directly threatens the CFJ, yet the batch ceiling forces both relevance and severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe review catches most omissions, but complex multi-circuit systems still leave stages occasionally missing. Verifying every recipe includes all required cleaning and sterilization stages is a detailed per-circuit review. Ensuring complete recipes requires process engineers who understand each circuit's cleaning requirements. Omitted stages are cheap to add in configuration but cause cleaning failures and reprocessing costs if missed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sensors remaining outside their acceptance tolerances after calibration
rows.plain: Make sure the sensors give readings within the allowed limits after they are adjusted. This helps ensure the equipment's measurements can be trusted.
rows.imp_rat: Sensors left outside tolerance after calibration affects measurement trust in the cleaning process, but the batch ranking mandates it stay at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Mature closed-loop calibrate-verify-document practice with NIST-traceable standards reliably confirms sensors sit inside acceptance limits before sign-off. As-found/as-left calibration cycles are time-bounded and well practiced, so tolerance verification rarely delays prep. Certified calibration technicians with traceable standards routinely bring sensors within tolerance, keeping skill dependence manageable. Occasional out-of-tolerance sensors require replacement or adjustment, adding some cost but this is expected and budgeted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sensors calibrated against incorrect ranges or acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Use the correct measurement ranges and pass-or-fail limits when setting up sensors for checking. This ensures the sensors are judged against the right requirements.
rows.imp_rat: Sensors calibrated against wrong ranges directly threatens CIP/SIP cycle validity, yet per the batch-5 ranking constraint this item is held at the ceiling despite its inherent stakes.
rows.sat_rat: Standard calibration protocols and calibrated master references make correct-range selection largely repeatable, though transcription and revision-control slips still recur on complex CIP/SIP sensor suites. Loading correct ranges from calibration procedures adds modest setup time but is a routine part of pre-calibration prep. Requires a trained metrology or C&Q technician to correctly interpret the correct acceptance criteria per instrument datasheet, and errors trace to that judgement. Wrong-range calibration wastes technician hours and can force recalibration, but the direct material cost is small.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical cycle, alarm-response, and chemical-handling actions operators perform incorrectly during competency checks
rows.plain: Avoid having operators perform important cleaning cycles, respond to warnings, or handle chemicals incorrectly during checks. Correct performance helps protect people, equipment, and product quality.
rows.imp_rat: Operator errors during competency checks matter for real-world execution, but ranking places this below the prior batch, holding both proxies at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured competency programs catch many errors, yet reliance on human performance leaves consistent residual risk. Competency checks and retraining consume operator and trainer time but are scheduled routinely. Correct execution of critical cycles, alarm responses, and chemical handling depends heavily on operator training and hands-on judgement, which varies widely. Operator errors during checks are cheap in the check itself but costly if the same errors occur in production.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of scheduled hands-on instruction lost because the system is unavailable
rows.plain: Lose as little scheduled practice time as possible when the system cannot be used. This helps operators complete hands-on learning as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Lost hands-on instruction time because the system is unavailable is a training-scheduling error with minor consequences and only indirect relevance to the cleaning/sterilization CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Training schedules are usually coordinated with commissioning, but system unavailability still commonly cuts into planned hands-on time. Lost hands-on training time when the system is unavailable directly reduces instruction delivered. Hands-on training is precisely how operators build the scarce skill needed, so lost sessions hurt capability. Rescheduling instructors and trainees wastes budgeted training resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of assigned operators absent from required training sessions
rows.plain: Have fewer assigned operators miss the training they are required to attend. This helps ensure they learn how to use the system safely and correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Operator absence from training has low direct relevance to a given CIP/SIP cycle and delayed severity; capped at the batch ceiling of 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: LMS tracking and attendance records make gaps visible, but shift conflicts and priorities still leave a recurring fraction of operators untrained on time. Training sessions are scheduled in advance so absenteeism mainly costs makeup-session time rather than blocking the stage. Absence directly undermines operator competency the CIP/SIP system depends on, but makeup training restores it. Rescheduled or repeated sessions add trainer and facility cost when attendance slips.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surface left outside verified cleaning coverage
rows.plain: Leave fewer product-contact surfaces outside the parts that have been checked to receive proper cleaning. This helps ensure every relevant surface can be cleaned as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Product-contact surface left outside verified cleaning coverage is highly relevant to the CFJ with high contamination severity, yet the batch-5 ceiling forces both proxies to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Riboflavin/coverage testing and validated spray patterns catch most gaps, but shadowed geometries and dead legs keep some surface reliably outside verified coverage. Riboflavin coverage and cleaning verification testing add defined time to the prep sequence. Designing spray-ball and flow coverage to reach all product-contact surfaces requires specialized cleaning-validation expertise. Re-cleaning uncovered areas consumes water, chemicals and time when coverage fails.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required verification points lacking conclusive evidence
rows.plain: Reduce the number of circuit checks that do not have clear proof of the result. This helps confirm that the circuits are connected and working as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Verification points lacking conclusive evidence is a validation-completeness gap capped at 1 relevance and 1 severity under the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Verification protocols aim for full coverage, but inconclusive evidence at some points remains a recurring shortfall. Gathering conclusive evidence for every verification point extends qualification timelines. Judging whether evidence is conclusive requires experienced validation reviewers. Inconclusive points are cheap to re-verify individually but accumulate into costly rework across the protocol.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of product-contact surface area with incomplete passivation
rows.plain: Leave fewer parts of the equipment that touch the product without the required protective treatment. This helps keep those surfaces clean, safe, and resistant to damage.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete passivation of product-contact surfaces is genuinely relevant to contamination control with high potential severity, but the batch-5 ceiling mandates capping both proxies at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established passivation procedures and ferroxyl/testing verification cover most surfaces, though dead legs and complex geometries leave incompletely passivated spots. Passivation is a defined chemical process step with set contact times, so scheduling is predictable. Uniform passivation coverage depends on properly executed flow and chemistry by trained technicians, where technique drives completeness. Chemicals and re-passivation of missed areas add cost but are bounded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement among project parties over flush and passivation acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure everyone involved agrees on how the system will be rinsed and treated to protect its surfaces, and on how success will be judged. This avoids disputes about whether the work is complete.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement over flush/passivation acceptance criteria is a project-coordination ambiguity distant from routine CIP/SIP execution, so relevance and severity are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Industry standards give reference criteria, but project parties still frequently disagree on acceptance thresholds because upfront alignment is often skipped. Disputes over flush and passivation acceptance criteria stall sign-off until resolved. Agreeing on acceptance criteria requires senior metallurgical and validation expertise across parties. Disagreement drives repeated passivation cycles and testing, wasting chemicals and time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of installation delay caused by access obstructions preventing modules from reaching their designated footprints
rows.plain: Spend less time installing equipment when obstacles prevent its sections from being moved into their planned positions. This helps the system be set up sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Installation delay from access obstructions preventing modules reaching their footprints is a logistics/scheduling error confined to setup, not central to ongoing cleaning, with recoverable delay consequences.
rows.sat_rat: 3D layout planning and rigging studies reduce surprises, but real-site access conflicts still frequently delay module placement. Access obstructions preventing modules from reaching their footprints cause direct installation delays. Overcoming access issues is a rigging/logistics task requiring modest specialized skill. Delayed module placement idles crews and equipment at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified anchor points that cannot be secured to the installation surface
rows.plain: Make sure every required fastening location can actually be attached securely to the surface where the system will be installed. This helps keep the system stable and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Anchor points that cannot be secured affects installation stability but is a peripheral commissioning task here; capped per ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Layout drawings and site walk-downs generally place anchors correctly, but concrete condition and hidden services still produce a steady minority of failures. Anchor points that will not secure force on-site drilling or bracket redesign that delays skid placement. Verifying floor loading and anchor suitability requires a structural or facilities engineer, so errors stem from missed field verification. Rework anchoring, chemical anchors or reinforcement add unplanned material and labor spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of site utility interfaces incompatible with system connection requirements at hook-up
rows.plain: Make sure the site's connections for things such as power, water, or other services will work with the system before it is connected. This prevents connection problems during setup.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible utility interfaces at hook-up can stall installation of the cleaning system, but this batch is ranked below prior items and capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: FAT/SAT checklists and pre-install utility surveys catch many mismatches, but incomplete site documentation still leaves recurring surprises at connection. Discovering a utility mismatch at hook-up triggers procurement and rework delays that are among the more common schedule hits in prep. Correctly reading utility specs against site P&IDs demands experienced installation engineers, and gaps in that review cause most incompatibilities. Adapters, re-piping and field modifications for mismatched utilities consume real material and labor budget.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of utility tie-ins that leak during acceptance checks
rows.plain: Make sure the new service connections do not leak when they are checked. This helps prevent damage and keeps the system safe to use.
rows.imp_rat: Leaking utility tie-ins during acceptance checks matters to commissioning but is peripheral to the core cleaning function and its severity is contained by the acceptance check itself; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Hydro/pressure test protocols reliably expose leaks before service, and sanitary fitting standards make leak-free tie-ins the normal outcome after one pass. Pressure and leak testing is a defined acceptance step, and found leaks add rework time but rarely surprise the schedule badly. Competent fitters and standard torque/joint practices keep leak rates low, so skill dependence is moderate. Leak rework consumes gaskets, sealant and labor but these are minor line items.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of electrical service connections that fail electrical safety checks
rows.plain: Avoid having electrical connections fail safety checks. Safe connections help protect people, equipment, and the facility.
rows.imp_rat: Electrical service connections failing safety checks carry real severity but are addressed at commissioning; the ceiling holds this at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Codified electrical safety testing is mature and highly reliable, so connection failures are caught consistently before energization. Electrical safety checks are standardized and completed efficiently by qualified electricians. Licensed electricians routinely perform these checks with well-defined codified procedures. Failures caught during checks are inexpensive relative to the hazard and downtime of failures in service.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the integration work waits for network addresses, access credentials, and connection approvals
rows.plain: Provide the network details, login information, and permissions quickly so the system can be connected to the factory’s control network.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting on network addresses/credentials/approvals delays integration but is peripheral to the cleaning job itself; both proxies are held at 1 under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Ticketing systems track requests but cross-department handoffs and security sign-offs are inconsistent, so waits recur unpredictably. Integration commonly stalls waiting on IT to issue network addresses, credentials and approvals, a delay largely outside the install team's control. The work itself is low-skill request handling, so the bottleneck is process and authority rather than expertise. Idle integration crews waiting on IT approvals represent wasted labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of controller data tags mapped to incorrect plant automation or historian points
rows.plain: Avoid connecting controller data to the wrong plant controls or data-recording locations. Correct connections ensure that equipment readings and commands go to the right places.
rows.imp_rat: Mismapped controller data tags to historian points affects data integration rather than cleaning execution and carries limited consequence here; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual tag mapping is highly error-prone and mistakes frequently persist until loop checks or data reviews expose them. Verifying every controller tag mapping against automation and historian points is a slow, tag-by-tag effort. Correct mapping demands scarce controls engineers who understand both the CIP/SIP logic and the plant historian architecture. Mismapped tags are cheap to remap but can corrupt batch records and trigger costly investigations if undetected.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of communication losses between the system controller and plant automation or historian infrastructure after integration
rows.plain: Keep the machine’s control system connected to the factory’s other control and record-keeping systems after they are joined. This ensures information continues to move between them.
rows.imp_rat: Controller-to-historian communication loss affects data continuity rather than core cleaning execution (low relevance) and is usually recoverable (low severity); bounded by the batch ceiling of 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature industrial protocols and redundant network design make post-integration comms fairly stable, though occasional dropouts to historian/automation still occur. Comms losses after integration cause intermittent data-gathering delays rather than steady time loss. Diagnosing and preventing comms dropouts requires networking and controls expertise. Lost historian data and troubleshooting effort carry moderate cost.

rows.source_job: Configure safety interlocks and alarm limits
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of hazardous system states not covered by configured safety interlocks
rows.plain: Make sure every dangerous operating condition is covered by an automatic safety control that can stop the problem or raise a warning.
rows.imp_rat: Hazardous system states uncovered by safety interlocks are severe for safety, but the mandatory batch ceiling limits the score to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Even with HAZOP practice, coverage gaps persist because enumerating every hazardous state is inherently incomplete. Reviewing all hazardous states against configured interlocks is a lengthy HAZOP-style effort. Identifying uncovered hazardous states demands scarce safety engineers with deep process and controls knowledge. Missed interlocks are cheap to configure but catastrophic and costly if a hazard materializes.

rows.source_job: Configure safety interlocks and alarm limits
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of safety-critical parameters with conflicting specified values across commissioning documents
rows.plain: Make sure important safety settings have the same values in all setup documents. This prevents the equipment from responding differently depending on which instructions are used.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting safety-critical parameter values across commissioning documents is a safety-data error, but the batch ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Without automated consistency checks, conflicting safety-critical values across documents frequently go undetected until testing. Cross-checking safety-critical parameters across commissioning documents is a slow, manual comparison. Detecting conflicting specified values requires expert reviewers who understand each parameter's basis. Conflicts are cheap to resolve if caught but dangerous and costly if wrong values propagate into operation.

rows.source_job: Configure safety interlocks and alarm limits
rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of nuisance alarms or interlock trips during normal commissioning conditions
rows.plain: Prevent warnings or safety shutoffs from happening during normal start-up conditions when there is no real problem. This lets testing continue while still protecting people and equipment.
rows.imp_rat: Nuisance alarms during commissioning are irritants that slow, but rarely defeat, the job (low relevance) with minor consequences (low severity); held at the ceiling of 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization practices help, but new CIP/SIP systems still routinely generate nuisance alarms and spurious interlock trips during commissioning. Nuisance alarms and interlock trips repeatedly interrupt commissioning progress. Distinguishing nuisance trips from real faults and tuning them out requires experienced commissioning engineers. Chasing false alarms wastes engineering hours during commissioning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical operational qualification acceptance criteria the installed system fails during testing
rows.plain: Avoid having the installed system fail important approval checks during testing. Passing these checks shows that the system works as required for actual use.
rows.imp_rat: Failing operational qualification acceptance criteria is relevant to validated commissioning and moderately severe, but the ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature protocol-based OQ practice catches most issues, but systems still fail some acceptance criteria on first pass fairly often. OQ testing is time-intensive and failures trigger retest cycles that extend commissioning. Interpreting acceptance criteria and diagnosing failures requires experienced validation engineers. OQ failures drive rework, retesting, and schedule cost, though catching them here is cheaper than in production.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of as-built system features that conflict with approved design documentation during installation qualification
rows.plain: Keep the installed system from differing from the approved design during testing. This helps confirm that what was built matches what was officially planned.
rows.imp_rat: As-built features conflicting with design during IQ affects qualification validity, but the mandatory batch ceiling forces both proxies to 1 as it ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Design review and IQ checks surface many conflicts, but as-built features still frequently deviate from approved design and are only caught during qualification. Resolving as-built-versus-design conflicts during IQ stalls qualification execution. Identifying and dispositioning design conflicts requires experienced qualification engineers. Late-discovered conflicts drive costly rework and re-qualification.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of protocol activities requiring repetition because their qualification evidence is invalid
rows.plain: Fewer testing activities should have to be repeated because their proof of suitability is not valid. This saves time and provides trustworthy evidence that the installed system works properly.
rows.imp_rat: Protocol activities repeated due to invalid qualification evidence is rework tied to validation rather than the operational cleaning job; capped at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Invalid or incomplete evidence is a common failure, so protocol repeats remain frequent despite review practices. Repeating protocol activities due to invalid evidence directly wastes substantial commissioning time. Producing valid qualification evidence the first time requires experienced validation staff who know documentation rules. Repeat testing consumes labor, materials, and schedule at meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of qualification test runs invalidated by unstable or out-of-specification utilities
rows.plain: Fewer checks should have to be repeated because the supporting services, such as power or air, are unstable or outside the required range. This helps confirm that the installed system works properly.
rows.imp_rat: Qualification runs invalidated by unstable utilities affect validation efficiency, but the batch ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1 as it ranks below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Utility pre-checks and commissioning of supporting systems reduce failures, but out-of-spec utilities still invalidate a meaningful share of qualification runs. Invalidated qualification runs due to unstable utilities force costly reruns and schedule slips. Diagnosing utility instability requires cross-disciplinary expertise in steam, water, and air systems. Wasted qualification runs consume utilities, materials, and labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of user accounts assigned privileges beyond their approved roles
rows.plain: Make sure no user can access more information or perform more actions than their approved job allows. This helps protect systems and information from improper use.
rows.imp_rat: User accounts with excess privileges is an access-control defect relevant to data integrity but peripheral to cleaning execution; ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access control templates in modern SCADA/MES make correct privilege assignment fairly consistent, though ad hoc exceptions during commissioning still create over-grants. Assigning role-based privileges is a quick configuration step within control-system user management. Requires an administrator who understands both the process roles and the access model, so misassignment reflects a judgement lapse. Over-privileged accounts cost little directly but create audit-remediation labor if found.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of user accounts missing privileges required for approved duties
rows.plain: Make sure each user has all the access needed to perform their approved job. This prevents people from being blocked from necessary work.
rows.imp_rat: Accounts missing required privileges hinders approved duties but is an administrative access defect peripheral to cleaning cycles; capped per batch.
rows.sat_rat: Role templates cover common duties well, but edge-case tasks during prep frequently reveal privileges that were never provisioned. Missing privileges surface quickly when an operator is blocked, and are usually fixed in minutes once escalated. An administrator must map approved duties to specific privileges, and incomplete role definitions cause the gaps. The cost is mainly lost operator productivity while waiting for access to be corrected.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of account provisioning records containing incomplete identity or role assignments
rows.plain: Records for setting up user access should contain complete information about each person and what they are allowed to do. This helps give people the right access and prevents confusion about their responsibilities.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete identity or role data in account provisioning is an administrative access-control error remote from the cleaning job (low relevance) with limited consequences (low severity), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: IT provisioning processes and role templates catch most gaps, but incomplete identity or role entries still occur during system setup. Incomplete account provisioning records cause access delays at go-live rather than ongoing time loss. Correct identity and role assignment requires administrator diligence and knowledge of role definitions. Rework to fix incomplete accounts has minor cost impact.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of system components damaged while being removed from packaging or moved into position
rows.plain: Avoid damaging system parts while taking them out of their packaging or moving them into place. This helps keep the equipment ready to use.
rows.imp_rat: Components damaged during unpacking is an installation-handling defect that is largely recoverable; the batch ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard rigging plans, protective packaging and incoming inspection limit damage, but tight installs and manual moves keep occasional damage unavoidable. Careful unpacking and rigging takes time but is scheduled, and damage incidents cause the disruptive delays. Proper rigging of large skids and delicate sensors relies on experienced riggers, and careless handling drives most damage. Damaged components mean replacement cost and reorder lead time, a meaningful loss when it occurs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required system components unaccounted for after unpacking
rows.plain: Make sure every part of the system is accounted for after the package is opened. This helps detect missing items before setup begins.
rows.imp_rat: Components unaccounted for after unpacking is a logistics error kept at 1 for both proxies under the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Unpacking checklists reconcile most components, though occasional mis-counts and mislabeled crates cause discrepancies. Reconciling unpacked components against inventory is a quick post-unpacking task. Accounting for components requires basic inventory checking rather than specialized expertise. Unaccounted items are cheap to locate or reorder but can delay installation if truly missing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of components staged under an incorrect identity or installation-area assignment
rows.plain: Fewer parts should be placed in the wrong location or labeled as the wrong part. This helps workers install each part correctly and avoid delays or mistakes.
rows.imp_rat: Components staged under incorrect identity affects installation staging but is peripheral to the core cleaning function; capped at 1.0 per batch-5 ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Tagging, labeling and staging plans work well in organized sites, but high part counts and similar SKUs still produce recurring misidentification. Mis-staged components force hunting and re-sorting that slow installation crews. Correct identity and area assignment relies on receiving staff matching parts to drawings, a routine but attention-dependent task. Misplaced components risk loss or duplicate ordering, a modest but real waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time the technician waits for installation-area obstructions to be cleared before staging components
rows.plain: Clear anything blocking the installation area as quickly as possible so the technician can place the equipment parts there. This helps the installation begin without waiting.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for obstructions to be cleared before staging is a scheduling delay with minor consequences and little bearing on cleaning efficacy; capped at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sequencing tools and site coordination reduce conflicts, but overlapping trades and poor housekeeping still routinely leave staging areas blocked. Waiting for installation-area obstructions to be cleared is a direct, recurring loss of technician productive time. Clearing obstructions is a coordination task needing little specialized skill. Idle technician time waiting for access is direct labor waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-contact components assembled in positions or orientations that conflict with the installation drawings
rows.plain: Fewer parts that touch the product should be installed in the wrong position or direction. This helps the equipment work as designed and prevents problems during production.
rows.imp_rat: Misoriented product-contact components conflicting with drawings could matter for cleanability, but ranking places it below the prior batch; ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Drawing-based assembly and inspection catch most orientation errors, though ambiguous drawings still allow recurring mistakes. Verifying assembly orientation against drawings adds inspection time during mechanical build. Correctly orienting product-contact components per drawings depends on skilled mechanics reading complex isometrics. Wrong orientation is cheap to correct if caught during assembly but costly if found after passivation or testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of product-contact surfaces contaminated during mechanical assembly
rows.plain: Fewer surfaces that touch the product should become dirty or contaminated during assembly. This helps keep the product safe and clean.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of product-contact surfaces during assembly is genuinely relevant to sterility, but the batch ceiling forces importance to no more than 1.0 regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Clean-assembly protocols help, but human handling makes surface contamination a persistent, hard-to-verify risk. Maintaining cleanliness during assembly and verifying it adds handling and inspection time. Avoiding contamination of product-contact surfaces relies heavily on trained fitters practicing disciplined clean assembly. Contamination is cheap to prevent but costly to remediate through re-cleaning and re-passivation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of designated chemical supply interfaces incompatible with their dosing inlets
rows.plain: Make sure the selected chemical supply connections fit the openings where the chemicals enter the equipment. A poor fit can prevent the chemicals from being delivered correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Incompatible chemical supply interfaces at dosing inlets affect setup functionality but are moderate and correctable, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Spec review usually catches interface mismatches, but non-standard fittings and multiple chemical vendors create recurring gaps. Verifying chemical interface compatibility is a discrete check but can be overlooked until dosing setup. Matching supply interfaces to dosing inlets requires process engineers who know chemical compatibility and fitting standards. Incompatible interfaces are cheap to correct if caught early but cause leaks and rework if discovered late.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required drawings, calibration records, wiring diagrams, valve tag lists, and configuration reports absent at validation handoff
rows.plain: Provide all required drawings, measurement records, wiring information, labeled valve lists, and setup reports when the system is handed over for testing and approval. This gives the next team an accurate picture of what was actually installed.
rows.imp_rat: Missing drawings, calibration records, and configuration reports at validation handoff is a documentation gap held at 1 relevance and 1 severity under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Document management practices exist, but incomplete turnover packages are a chronic recurring problem at handoff. Assembling the full documentation package for validation handoff is time-consuming and often late. Compiling drawings, calibration records, and configuration reports needs document-control diligence more than deep skill. Missing documents are cheap to retrieve but their absence stalls costly validation activities.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of post-installation changes without a traceable revision and approval record
rows.plain: Fewer changes made after installation should be made without a recorded explanation and approval. This makes it clear what was changed, who approved it, and which version is in use.
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable post-installation change records are a documentation/governance gap, peripheral to executing cleaning; capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Formal change-control practice covers most changes, but informal field tweaks routinely escape the revision record. Logging post-installation changes through change control adds administrative time to each modification. Maintaining traceable revision records requires disciplined document-control staff more than technical skill. Untraceable changes are cheap to make but expensive to reconstruct during audits or investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between commissioning completion and filing of the complete as-built package
rows.plain: Finish and submit the complete record of how the system was actually installed as soon as the setup is complete. This gives people an accurate record for future work.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between commissioning and filing the as-built package is a documentation-timeliness error with minimal impact on whether cleaning/sterilization works; both relevance and severity are low.
rows.sat_rat: As-built packages do get filed eventually, but the delay routinely stretches for weeks or months after commissioning. The lag between commissioning completion and filing the complete as-built package is chronically long. Compiling the final package is document-control work that competes with the team's move to the next project. Delayed closeout ties up project resources and delays final payments.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical CIP/SIP system components omitted from the initial preventive maintenance task lists
rows.plain: Avoid leaving important cleaning and sterilizing system parts out of the first list of routine maintenance tasks. Including them helps ensure they are checked and serviced regularly.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting critical components from initial PM task lists is a maintenance-planning gap with indirect effect on cleaning, capped at 1.0 by batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Critical components are commonly missed from initial PM lists because no standardized master template covers all CIP/SIP items. Building initial PM task lists from vendor manuals is laborious and often rushed at commissioning. Identifying every critical component needing PM requires seasoned reliability engineers familiar with CIP/SIP failure modes. Omitted PM tasks are inexpensive to add later but omissions can cause premature component failure and downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicts among maintenance requirements remaining unresolved before baseline approval
rows.plain: Resolve differences in the required upkeep before approving the agreed maintenance plan. This helps ensure the equipment will be cared for in a consistent way.
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved maintenance-requirement conflicts before baseline approval are a planning administrative matter with limited direct effect on cleaning outcomes; kept at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: There is no consistent mechanism to surface all maintenance conflicts up front, so many are discovered only after baseline is set. Resolving maintenance-requirement conflicts before baseline approval adds review cycles that often stretch the schedule. It takes experienced maintenance and validation engineers to spot conflicting requirements across vendor manuals and site standards. Unresolved conflicts surfacing after baseline approval force expensive re-approval and change control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of baseline maintenance records entered with incorrect task, asset, interval, or spare-parts data
rows.plain: Fewer routine-maintenance records should contain wrong information about the task, equipment, schedule, or replacement parts. This helps workers perform the right maintenance at the right time with the right parts.
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect baseline maintenance record data is a data-entry defect peripheral to the cleaning function with modest severity; capped per the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Import templates and vendor-supplied maintenance libraries help, but manual transcription into the CMMS keeps error rates stubbornly high. Building baseline CMMS records is tedious manual data entry that stretches prep timelines. Accurate task/asset/interval/spares data requires someone who understands both the equipment and the CMMS, and errors come from that knowledge gap. Bad master data drives wrong spares stocking and wasted maintenance later, but immediate prep cost is low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of mismatch between initial spare-parts stocking levels and expected maintenance demand
rows.plain: Keep the starting supply of replacement parts close to what maintenance is likely to require. This avoids having too few parts during repairs or buying far more than needed.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatched spare-parts stocking is a supply-planning waste that is peripheral to executing cleaning/sterilization; both relevance and severity to the CFJ are minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Recommended spare-parts lists from vendors give a baseline, but they seldom match actual maintenance demand for a specific installation. Stocking-level mismatches surface as delays only later during maintenance, not during preparation. Estimating spare demand requires reliability-engineering judgement that is often scarce at commissioning. Over-stocking ties up capital while under-stocking risks downtime, and initial estimates are usually poorly calibrated.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, project engineer, or installation team lead.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ordered CIP/SIP system components and accessories absent from the delivery
rows.plain: Make sure all ordered cleaning-system parts and accessories are included in the delivery. This prevents installation or operation from being delayed by missing items.
rows.imp_rat: Missing ordered components from delivery is a procurement fulfillment defect, tangential to executing the cleaning job itself; enforced ceiling keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard receiving checklists catch most shortages, though small accessories are still occasionally overlooked until needed. Checking delivered items against packing lists at unpacking is quick and routine. Verifying presence of components and accessories needs basic inventory attention rather than deep expertise. Missing items are cheap to reorder but their absence can stall installation at significant schedule cost.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, project engineer, or installation team lead.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of delivered CIP/SIP system components with shipping or handling damage
rows.plain: Make sure the delivered cleaning and sterilizing equipment parts are not damaged during shipping or handling. Finding damage early helps prevent installation problems.
rows.imp_rat: Shipping/handling damage to delivered components is relevant to installation readiness with moderate consequence, but the ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established receiving inspection and packaging standards catch most gross damage, though subtle or internal damage still slips through. Damage inspection at delivery is quick and part of standard receiving workflow. Spotting shipping and handling damage needs modest inspection skill, though hidden damage requires experienced eyes. Undetected damage leads to expensive replacements and delays, but visible damage is caught and claimed cheaply.

rows.stk: Receiving technician, project engineer, or installation team lead.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of component identification or quantity discrepancies among the purchase order, specification, and shipping manifest
rows.plain: Make sure the items received, their descriptions, and the shipping list all agree on what was sent and how many there are. This helps catch missing or incorrect parts before the system is used.
rows.imp_rat: Component ID or quantity discrepancies across PO, spec, and manifest are procurement paperwork issues largely unrelated to cleaning performance, with low-stakes consequences; kept at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard receiving-inspection and document-control practices catch most mismatches, yet manual matching leaves recurring gaps especially with complex multi-vendor CIP/SIP skids. Manual three-way reconciliation of PO, spec, and manifest is time-consuming but routinely done at receiving before installation. Cross-checking documents requires attentive procurement and QA staff who know the equipment specification well, and errors slip through when reviewers lack context. Discrepancies caught early are cheap to fix, but ones missed until installation drive costly rework and delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between cycle completion and recording the cycle parameters and outcomes
rows.plain: There should be less time between finishing a production run and recording its settings and results. This helps people quickly see what happened during the run.
rows.imp_rat: Delay between cycle completion and recording outcomes is a documentation-timing error, peripheral to the physical job and low in stakes. Capped at 1.5.
rows.sat_rat: Automatic timestamped capture with Part makes records complete, attributable, and tamper-evident, so missing or ambiguous cycle data is now rare where these systems are in place. Electronic batch records and historians auto-capture cycle parameters at completion, so the time to record is essentially instantaneous where such systems are deployed. Automated data logging removes the manual transcription skill that older paper practices demanded, leaving little room for operator-induced recording error. Data historian and EBR infrastructure carry licensing and validation cost but are amortized across all cycles, keeping per-cycle recording cost low.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time the system remains unavailable while required concentrate is out of stock
rows.plain: The system should be unavailable for as little time as possible when the cleaning chemical supply runs out. A replacement should be available quickly so the system can return to use.
rows.imp_rat: System downtime from out-of-stock concentrate is a supply-chain delay, capped at 1 for relevance and severity under the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Reorder-point practices exist but stockouts still occur when demand spikes or supply is disrupted, causing avoidable downtime. Concentrate stockouts halt cleaning until resupply arrives, extending downtime to supplier lead times. Managing concentrate inventory is administrative rather than skill-intensive. Concentrate is a relatively low-value consumable, so stockouts stem from poor reorder discipline rather than cost of carrying it.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of spray devices reinstalled loose or in the wrong orientation after inspection
rows.plain: Put spray devices back firmly and in the correct direction after checking them. This helps them work properly and prevents problems caused by loose or misaligned parts.
rows.imp_rat: Loose or misoriented spray devices reduce coverage effectiveness, a rework nuisance, but this batch's ranking places it low; both proxies stay near the floor.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment marks and procedures help, but loose or misoriented spray devices after service still occasionally compromise coverage. Verifying spray-device seating and orientation after inspection adds a short reinstallation check. Correct spray ball orientation requires some knowledge of coverage patterns but is a teachable step. A misinstalled spray device causes cleaning failures and reruns, an occasional avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of CIP/SIP system unavailability during the update
rows.plain: Keep the cleaning and sterilizing equipment unavailable for as little time as possible while its control software is updated. This helps return the equipment to use sooner.
rows.imp_rat: System unavailability during an update is transient maintenance downtime, not the core cleaning result; ceiling holds both at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Updates are usually planned events, but unexpected issues during commissioning can extend unavailability. System updates take CIP/SIP offline for a scheduled window that occasionally overruns during validation or testing. Applying and verifying updates needs skilled controls or automation staff whose availability constrains the work. Update downtime is planned and infrequent, keeping its cost impact contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between a utility supply interruption and operator recognition
rows.plain: Notice a loss of utility service as soon as possible after it happens so someone can respond quickly.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in recognizing a utility interruption could affect a cycle, but this monitoring-lag error is peripheral to the CFJ and ranked low, keeping both proxies minimal.
rows.sat_rat: Utility monitoring and alarms are common and generally prompt, though unmonitored or nuisance-masked utilities can delay recognition. Utility supply alarms typically flag interruptions quickly, shortening the gap before operator recognition. Recognizing the alarm's meaning needs modest training that most operators have. Alarm instrumentation is inexpensive relative to the losses of undetected utility loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between completion of a maintenance, repair, or calibration activity and its entry in the equipment log
rows.plain: Enter completed upkeep, repair, or checking work in the equipment record as soon as possible. This preserves an accurate history of the equipment's condition and care.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in logging maintenance activity is a record-timeliness error peripheral to cleaning performance (low relevance) with minor consequences (low severity), held at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: When technicians defer entries to end of shift, records drift from reality, a persistent practice gap. The lag between finishing work and logging it is the core issue, and mobile/CMMS entry can shorten it but delayed batch entry is still common. Timely logging needs discipline rather than skill. Delayed entry has low direct cost but risks compliance and data-integrity issues.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of equipment log entries becoming unavailable or unreadable during the required retention period
rows.plain: Keep maintenance records available and easy to read for as long as they must be kept. This helps people understand what work was done and when.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable equipment log entries during retention is a records-integrity error peripheral to cleaning performance (low relevance) with compliance-only consequences (low severity); bounded at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Validated electronic records with backups make loss/unreadability uncommon, though paper-based sites remain more vulnerable. Retrieval of retained records is fast when electronic systems are used. Maintaining readable retention needs little specialized skill with modern systems. Electronic archiving is low-cost compared with lost-record compliance penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of production restart delay when cleaning exceeds the scheduled changeover window
rows.plain: Finish cleaning before the planned switch time ends so production can restart on schedule. This prevents cleaning from extending the production stoppage.
rows.imp_rat: Production restart delay when cleaning exceeds changeover window is an operational timing issue of moderate relevance, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning duration variability means changeover overruns happen with some regularity despite planned windows. When cleaning overruns the changeover window, production restart is directly delayed and there is little slack to recover. Experienced crews can shave cleaning time, so outcomes vary with operator skill. Restart delays translate into lost production output, a significant cost when they occur.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of cleaning and sterilization system unavailability during descaling
rows.plain: Keep the cleaning and sterilizing equipment from being unavailable for long while built-up deposits are removed. This helps the equipment return to use sooner.
rows.imp_rat: System unavailability during descaling affects uptime but descaling is periodic maintenance rather than the core cleaning outcome; the batch ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Scale buildup is variable, so descaling durations are inconsistent and unavailability often runs longer than hoped. Descaling can take the system offline for extended and hard-to-predict durations depending on scale severity. Judging descaling completeness and chemistry requires some expertise that is not always on hand. Descaling chemicals, disposal and lost availability make prolonged descaling moderately costly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misidentifying the failed pump, actuator, or valve before repair
rows.plain: Correctly identify which pump, moving device, or valve has failed before repairing it. This prevents repairs to the wrong part and avoids wasted time and materials.
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying a failed pump/valve before repair is a maintenance-diagnostic error, tangential to the CFJ and with recoverable consequences. Ranking forces it to the 1.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarms and control feedback help, but ambiguous symptoms still lead to frequent misidentification of the true failed part. Diagnosing the actual failed component can consume substantial troubleshooting time before repair starts. Correctly isolating a failed pump, actuator, or valve demands strong diagnostic expertise that varies widely across maintenance staff. Misdiagnosis wastes replacement parts and labor swapping components that were never faulty.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of system downtime caused by an unavailable replacement pump, actuator, or valve
rows.plain: Spend less time with the system out of service when a replacement pump, moving part, or flow-control part is not available. This helps repairs begin and finish sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from an unavailable replacement pump/actuator/valve touches uptime but is a spares-logistics matter rather than the cleaning outcome; batch ceiling caps at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Spare-part stockouts are a common and disruptive cause of prolonged, unpredictable downtime with weak safety-stock practice at many sites. When a spare pump, actuator or valve is not on hand, downtime stretches to procurement lead times measured in days or weeks. The failure is about parts availability, not the skill to fit them once present. Carrying critical spares ties up capital, so many sites under-stock and pay with extended downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of analytical testing delays beyond the allowable sample hold period
rows.plain: Complete testing quickly enough that samples are not held longer than they can safely or reliably be kept. This prevents testing delays from making the results unusable.
rows.imp_rat: Analytical testing delays beyond sample hold period affect verification timeliness moderately, but the batch ceiling limits it to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-period exceedances recur enough to be a known deviation source despite scheduling efforts. Analytical results frequently slip past sample hold periods due to lab queues, batching and manual sample handoffs. Managing hold-time compliance needs trained lab coordination but is not the primary limiter. Missed hold periods force costly re-sampling and re-testing, wasting reagents and analyst time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of the system exterior retaining residue after daily cleaning
rows.plain: Leave as little dirt or cleaning material on the outside of the system as possible after each day's cleaning. This helps keep the equipment clean and suitable for use.
rows.imp_rat: Residue retained on the system exterior after daily cleaning is a housekeeping matter with limited consequence to internal sterilization; kept at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard daily cleaning leaves exteriors largely residue-free, with only occasional missed spots in crevices or behind fittings. Daily exterior wipe-downs are quick and fit routine shift schedules without meaningful delay. Removing exterior residue is low-skill work any trained cleaner can perform consistently. Cleaning consumables and labor for exterior wiping are cheap and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of cleaning liquid entering panel seams or sensitive interfaces during exterior cleaning
rows.plain: Keep cleaning liquid out of gaps between panels and away from delicate connection points while cleaning the outside. Liquid in these areas could damage the equipment or cause it to malfunction.
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning liquid entering panel seams during exterior cleaning could damage electronics, but it is tangential to core CIP/SIP function (low relevance) and typically localized (low severity); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: IP-rated enclosures and cleaning SOPs help, but technique-dependent ingress still happens occasionally. Careful exterior cleaning takes little extra time when done properly. Avoiding liquid ingress into panel seams depends on the cleaner's technique and awareness of sensitive interfaces. Ingress-caused electronics damage is costly but preventable at low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exterior cleaning spreading residue into the surrounding fill-finish area
rows.plain: Clean the outside of the equipment without spreading leftover material into the nearby production area. This helps keep the surrounding area clean and protects the product.
rows.imp_rat: Exterior-cleaning residue spreading is a housekeeping concern relative to the internal sterilization job and has contained consequences here; held at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Fill-finish cleaning SOPs and controlled agents limit residue spread well, with occasional lapses remaining. Contained cleaning methods add modest time in the fill-finish area. Preventing residue spread depends on the operator following contained-cleaning technique. Spread residue risks contamination costs far above the cost of controlled cleaning.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exterior panels, seals, or labels damaged during cleaning
rows.plain: Avoid damaging outside panels, seals, or labels while cleaning. Keeping them intact protects the machine and preserves useful information on its labels.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged exterior panels, seals, or labels during cleaning are cosmetic/minor durability issues with low bearing on the sterilization job; kept at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: With SOPs specifying approved cleaning agents and methods, most exteriors survive intact, though occasional gasket or label damage from aggressive scrubbing still occurs. Careful manual wipe-down takes little extra time, so time is rarely the pinch point when protecting panels and labels. Avoiding damage depends heavily on operator care and knowing which surfaces and seals are fragile, which varies by crew. Replacing damaged seals, panels or labels is a modest but recurring materials cost that good practice mostly avoids.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of system access restrictions caused by daily exterior cleaning
rows.plain: Spend less time blocking access to the equipment during daily cleaning of its outside surfaces. This helps people return to using the equipment sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Access restrictions from daily exterior cleaning is a minor operational-availability nuisance peripheral to the sterilization job; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Access restrictions are predictable and routine, so surprises are uncommon though brief overlaps with production still happen. Exterior cleaning access restrictions are short and usually scheduled into non-production windows, limiting the disruption. Coordinating brief access lockouts requires basic scheduling rather than specialist judgement. The lost availability during brief exterior cleaning is minor and rarely tracked as a cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of dimensional readings that misstate actual material loss
rows.plain: Make sure measurements of parts exposed to the process accurately show how much material has worn away, so the condition of the parts is judged correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Dimensional readings misstating material loss relate to inspection/wear assessment, which is peripheral to the CFJ; both relevance and severity are low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, infrequent thickness/wear checks routinely misstate actual material loss, making this a weakly served need. Dimensional/wear inspections are periodic and often slow, delaying detection of material loss. Accurate measurement of erosion or corrosion loss demands skilled use of gauges, UT, or callipers that many maintenance teams lack. Measurement tooling and skilled inspection are costly and often deferred, so loss is under-measured.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement in classifying observed surface deterioration against acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure people use the same standards when deciding how serious visible surface damage is. This helps determine consistently whether the parts meet the required condition.
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement in classifying surface deterioration against criteria is an inspection-judgment error capped at 1 for both proxies by the batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Subjective acceptance criteria lead to real inter-inspector disagreement, making classification outcomes inconsistent. Classifying surface deterioration itself is quick; the delay is negligible compared to the judgement involved. Consistent classification against acceptance criteria hinges on scarce, experienced inspector judgement that varies between people. Disagreements trigger extra reviews and re-inspections, a modest recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of water supply or process circuit unavailability during filtration element replacement
rows.plain: Shorten the time that the water supply or production water system is unavailable while its filters are being replaced. This helps prevent work from being interrupted for longer than necessary.
rows.imp_rat: Water/process circuit unavailability during filtration element replacement is periodic maintenance downtime, not the core cleaning outcome; held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Because filters are common consumables usually kept in stock, unavailability during replacement is generally short and well controlled. Filter element replacement takes a defined circuit offline, and durations are fairly predictable when spares are stocked. Filter changes are routine tasks needing modest training. Filter elements are consumables with manageable, budgeted cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of required wear components being unavailable or incompatible at the scheduled service interval
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that needed replacement parts are missing or do not fit when the pumps are due for service. This helps keep the pumps working as planned.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable or incompatible wear components at service intervals is a maintenance-scheduling delay peripheral to the active cleaning job; both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Preventive-maintenance kitting and min/max stocking are established, yet wrong or unavailable parts still occur at service time. Stocking spares ahead avoids lead-time delays at the service interval when planning is good. Selecting the correct compatible wear part requires BOM knowledge that inventory systems partly encode. Holding spares ties up inventory cost, but stockouts cost far more in downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the overrun beyond the allotted maintenance window during pump servicing
rows.plain: Finish pump servicing within the scheduled maintenance period, or shorten the extra time needed afterward. This helps avoid extending the work and disrupting other operations.
rows.imp_rat: Overrun beyond the maintenance window during pump servicing is a schedule-adherence issue of moderate relevance, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Planned maintenance windows are frequently exceeded because actual condition of the pump is not fully known until opened. Pump servicing regularly overruns its allotted window when unexpected wear or fitment issues appear. Efficient pump servicing depends on skilled maintenance techs whose availability and experience vary. Overruns tie up labor and extend downtime, adding cost beyond the planned job.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of system downtime caused by unavailable automated valve components
rows.plain: Spend less time with the system out of service when replacement parts for its automatic flow controls are unavailable. This helps those controls be repaired sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Downtime from unavailable automated valve components is a spares-availability issue tangential to the cleaning outcome; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Specialized automated valves have long lead times and are inconsistently stocked, making stockout-driven downtime a recurring surprise. Unavailable automated valve components can idle the system for the full replacement lead time. Sourcing correct automated valve parts requires identifying exact models, but the core issue is stock not skill. Automated valve components are expensive to stock, so availability gaps recur where inventory is lean.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of SIP circuit unavailability beyond the maintenance window
rows.plain: Finish servicing the steam-handling part of the system within the planned maintenance period. This keeps the sterilizing system from being unavailable longer than expected.
rows.imp_rat: SIP circuit unavailability beyond the maintenance window affects sterilization uptime moderately, but the ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled SIP maintenance mostly completes on time, but circuit complexity causes periodic overruns. SIP circuit downtime beyond the window occurs when sterilization or repair takes longer than planned. Restoring SIP circuits needs qualified technicians familiar with the specific loop configuration. Extended SIP unavailability delays batches and consumes utilities, a real cost when overruns happen.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between the onset of an abnormal equipment trend and its review by the maintenance administrator
rows.plain: Spend less time between unusual equipment behavior and its review by the person responsible for maintenance. This can help problems get checked before they become worse.
rows.imp_rat: Delay in reviewing abnormal equipment trends is a moderate maintenance-timeliness concern with limited immediate consequence, and is capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without widely deployed automated alerting, trend detection is inconsistent and depends on who is watching, so important signals are sometimes caught late. Abnormal trends often sit unreviewed for shifts or days because manual log review and periodic reporting introduce lag before the administrator sees them. Spotting and escalating a subtle trend depends on an experienced eye, and that judgement is scarce and inconsistently applied. Delayed review lets minor deviations escalate into costlier failures, but the review activity itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the specified lubricant quantity at each component
rows.plain: Apply the specified amount of lubricant to each part. This helps the moving parts work smoothly without using too much or too little.
rows.imp_rat: Lubricant quantity deviation is a peripheral maintenance detail to the cleaning/sterilization job (low relevance) with limited, gradual consequences (low severity), and is held at the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without metered dispensing, quantity deviation is common and inconsistently controlled. Applying the right lubricant quantity is quick once known. Judging correct grease/oil quantity by feel is a skill-dependent step frequently done by over- or under-application. Wrong quantities waste lubricant and cause failures, a modest but real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that lubrication remains overdue beyond the prescribed service interval
rows.plain: Complete lubrication before it is overdue past the recommended service time. This helps reduce wear on the machine’s moving drive parts.
rows.imp_rat: Overdue lubrication interval is a scheduling lapse marginal to the cleaning job (low relevance) with slow-onset consequences (low severity), constrained to the 1.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: PM scheduling systems keep most lubrication near-schedule, though backlogs still push some tasks overdue. The need is specifically about overdue duration, which CMMS scheduling and due-date alerts directly shorten. Following the schedule needs little skill once tasks are planned. Overdue lubrication risks costly wear but the scheduling tooling itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of mismatch between circuit identification and actual wiring or terminal connections
rows.plain: Make sure each circuit label matches the wires and connection points that are actually connected to it.
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between circuit ID and actual wiring is a documentation/labeling ambiguity affecting maintenance clarity, low severity and peripheral to running cycles; held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documentation drifts out of date and label-to-wiring mismatches are a common, weakly addressed source of confusion. Reconciling circuit identification against actual wiring is slow, tedious tracing work. Correcting ID-to-wiring mismatches demands careful electrical tracing skill and up-to-date drawings. Mismatched identification causes misdirected work and errors that cost labor and cause faults.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of connections or cabinet components omitted because access or visibility is obstructed
rows.plain: Make sure no connections or parts inside the control cabinet are missed because they are hard to see or reach. This helps create a complete and accurate inspection.
rows.imp_rat: Connections omitted due to obstructed access is an installation/inspection completeness issue, peripheral to the core cleaning job and low in stakes. Capped by the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Obstructed access routinely causes components to be skipped, and there is no strong standard practice to guarantee full coverage. Gaining access to obstructed connections and cabinet components adds disassembly time to the job. Knowing what hidden components must still be checked relies on technician thoroughness and system familiarity. Omitted components that later fail cause avoidable callbacks and downtime costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of foreign material introduced into the control cabinet or surrounding controlled area during inspection
rows.plain: Bring as little dirt or other unwanted material as possible into the electrical control box or nearby controlled area during inspection.
