rows.imp_rat: Foreign material introduced during control-cabinet inspection is tangential to the wash/sterilize job and its consequences are minor and contained; batch ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cleanroom and tool-control practices reduce foreign-material introduction, but lapses during inspection still occur. Contamination control during inspection adds minor time overhead. Avoiding foreign material in control cabinets depends on technician housekeeping discipline. Introduced contaminants can cause costly faults but prevention is cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree to which drained chemicals remain outside waste acceptance limits after neutralization
rows.plain: Make sure the drained chemicals meet the limits for what can be accepted as waste after they are made less harmful. This helps them be handled and disposed of safely.
rows.imp_rat: Drained chemicals exceeding waste acceptance limits after neutralization is an environmental-compliance concern peripheral to the core cleaning/sterilization job; severity exists but ranking places it below prior batch, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: pH and parameter checks against waste-acceptance criteria are mature, so out-of-limit batches are the exception rather than the rule. Neutralization and testing before discharge adds a manageable, scheduled delay. Neutralizing drained chemicals to acceptance limits needs operators competent in titration and pH control. Neutralizing agents and lab testing consume resources routinely budgeted for chemical disposal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of caustic, acid, or sanitizer remaining in tanks and lines after drainage
rows.plain: Remove as much strong cleaning chemical as possible from tanks and pipes after they are drained. This reduces the leftover chemicals that could create a safety or handling risk.
rows.imp_rat: Residual caustic/acid/sanitizer after drainage directly threatens product safety and cleaning validity, both relevant and severe, but the mandatory ceiling holds it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Purge and rinse-to-drain procedures with conductivity/pH verification usually leave low residual, though dead legs and low points can retain caustic or acid. Final drain-and-flush cycles add time but are a standard, scheduled part of CIP system decommissioning. Operators trained on CIP chemistry routinely execute drain-down, so little specialized judgement is needed. Extra rinse water and neutralizer consume modest resources already accounted for in shutdown budgets.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required batch, cleaning validation, and cycle records omitted from the export
rows.plain: Include all required production, cleaning-check, and operating records in the exported files, so the complete history can be reviewed.
rows.imp_rat: Omitting batch/cleaning-validation records from export is a records-transfer defect with low centrality to the job and capped severity; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: GMP retention rules and validated historians make record export largely dependable, yet fragmented or obsolete systems cause required records to be missed. Pulling batch, CV, and cycle records for export is slow when data spans legacy historians and paper logs. Knowing which records are required for retention and where they live demands QA and validation expertise. Archiving and export effort is modest labor cost with little material spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of vendor references that do not represent comparable sterile fill-finish installations
rows.plain: Use supplier references from installations that are genuinely similar to the sterile product-filling work being considered. This makes the references more useful for judging the supplier's experience.
rows.imp_rat: Non-comparable vendor references weaken reference-checking but are administrative to the CFJ (low relevance) with modest consequences (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Vendors often supply references that are not comparable installations, so reference validity is inconsistent. Finding truly comparable sterile fill-finish references takes considerable searching time. Judging whether a reference installation is genuinely comparable requires domain expertise. Non-comparable references give false confidence that can prove expensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to receive responses from reference contacts for comparable cleaning and sterilization in place installations
rows.plain: Get answers from customers who have used similar systems for cleaning and sterilizing equipment without taking it apart more quickly. This helps the buyer assess the vendor’s past work.
rows.imp_rat: Slow reference-contact responses cause procurement delays only, peripheral to actual CIP/SIP operation (low relevance) and low severity. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Response latency is highly variable because it depends on unpaid contacts' goodwill and availability. Waiting on busy reference contacts to respond routinely stretches into weeks with no reliable acceleration. Little special skill is needed to make the contact, only persistence. The delay costs mainly staff follow-up effort rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that confidentiality restrictions prevent access to decision-critical details about comparable installations
rows.plain: Make sure privacy rules do not block access to important details about similar installations. Without those details, it may be harder to judge whether the supplier and equipment are suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Confidentiality limits on comparable-installation data are peripheral to actually cleaning and sterilizing in place, and the consequence is a minor research inconvenience; capped at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Confidentiality restrictions routinely block access to peer installation data, and there is no standard mechanism to overcome this, leaving decisions under-informed. Obtaining decision-critical details on comparable installations stalls when NDAs block access, delaying due diligence. Working around confidentiality barriers to extract useful benchmarks demands well-connected experts with industry relationships. Lacking comparable-installation data leads to poorer purchasing decisions with downstream cost consequences.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material vendor claims unsupported by evidence from case studies and reference accounts
rows.plain: Make sure important claims made by a supplier are backed by real examples and customer references. Evidence helps people judge whether the supplier can deliver what it promises.
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported vendor claims are a procurement-evidence concern with low relevance and severity to the cleaning job; capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Vendor claims are often accepted with thin evidence, leaving a persistent verification gap. Verifying vendor claims via references takes time few buyers invest. Critically assessing material claims against real case studies requires experienced technical judgement. Unverified claims can lead to buying underperforming materials, a downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of conflict between reference customers' accounts and vendor case-study claims about cleaning and sterilization performance
rows.plain: Make sure customer experiences agree with the supplier’s claims about how well its equipment cleans and sterilizes. This helps buyers judge whether the claims match real results.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between reference accounts and vendor case-study claims is a procurement-evidence issue held at 1 for both proxies by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Reference checks are common practice but conflicts between customer accounts and glossy case-study claims persist and are hard to resolve reliably. Chasing reference customers and reconciling them against case studies adds substantial time to due diligence. Judging conflicts between references and vendor claims requires experienced evaluators who can probe past marketing. This reconciliation costs mostly time, not direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the site assessment cannot be scheduled within the pre-purchase decision window
rows.plain: The site visit should be arranged before the time allowed for making the purchase decision runs out. This gives the buyer the information needed to decide.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to schedule a site assessment within the decision window is a procurement-timing error of low relevance, capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established site-visit practices make scheduling generally dependable, with delays mainly when key stakeholders' availability collides. Scheduling a site assessment inside the pre-purchase window usually works but supplier and internal calendar conflicts periodically push it late. Arranging the assessment is coordination-driven and needs little scarce expertise. Travel and staff time for a site visit are predictable and modest against the capital scale.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that facility access and safety clearances remain unapproved on the assessment date
rows.plain: Make sure all permissions to enter the facility and required safety approvals are granted before the assessment day.
rows.imp_rat: Unapproved access/safety clearances on the assessment date is a scheduling delay peripheral to cleaning performance with low stakes in this batch; held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established site-access procedures usually deliver clearances on time, but occasional administrative delays still leave clearances pending on the date. Facility access and safety clearances routinely become the schedule bottleneck when approvals lag the assessment date. Securing clearances is administrative coordination requiring persistence more than specialized skill. Unapproved clearances waste mobilized assessor time and travel costs when visits are aborted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required information about the installation space, utilities, and access route missing when the assessment scope is finalized
rows.plain: Make sure the assessment team has complete information about the available space, needed services such as power and water, and the route to the equipment. This helps them prepare a site visit safely and efficiently.
rows.imp_rat: Missing installation-space and utility info at scope finalization is a pre-project data gap, peripheral to actual CIP/SIP function (low relevance) with correctable impact (low severity). Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Required installation-space and access information is commonly incomplete when scope is fixed, weakening decision consistency. Gathering space, utility and access-route information before scope finalization often waits on site surveys. Assessing installation space and access routes needs experienced site-survey judgement. Incomplete space and access data leads to costly rigging and installation surprises later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of necessary facility and installation stakeholders unavailable during the assessment
rows.plain: Make sure the people needed to assess the site are available when the assessment takes place. Their input helps identify facility and installation needs correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable stakeholders during assessment causes coordination gaps but is peripheral to core cleaning; both proxies capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Stakeholder availability is variable, so some necessary parties routinely miss assessments. Absent stakeholders stall assessments until sessions are rescheduled. Convening the right facility and installation experts requires coordination but not rare skill. Rescheduling wastes labor time more than hard cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the CIP/SIP system capital approval package being rejected because required documentation is incomplete
rows.plain: Avoid having the request for funding the cleaning and sterilizing equipment rejected because required papers are missing. A complete set of documents helps approval go through.
rows.imp_rat: Capital approval package rejection is a documentation/administrative error remote from cleaning execution (low relevance) with recoverable delay consequences (low severity), capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard capital-request templates and review gates catch many gaps, yet incomplete documentation still causes recurring rejections. Assembling complete capital-approval documentation is time-consuming and iterative when templates are unclear. Preparing a compliant approval package needs staff familiar with both capital-governance and technical documentation requirements. Rejected packages cause rework and delay-related carrying costs but limited direct spend.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration the CIP/SIP system capital approval request remains pending between required procurement and finance reviews
rows.plain: Get the major equipment approval request through purchasing and finance reviews sooner. This helps the request avoid sitting between the two review steps.
rows.imp_rat: Capital approval requests pending between reviews is a financial-process delay far from cleaning-cycle execution; the ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Established capital-approval workflows make timing fairly predictable, though pending time between reviews still varies with committee schedules. Capital approval requests can sit between procurement and finance reviews, adding weeks depending on gate cadence. Preparing the approval package needs standard financial-justification skill. Approval delay itself costs little directly beyond opportunity cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of discrepancies in CIP/SIP system scope and cost information across approval documents
rows.plain: Make sure the documents used for approval agree about what the cleaning and sterilizing system includes and how much it costs. This supports an accurate purchasing decision.
rows.imp_rat: Scope/cost discrepancies across approval documents create ambiguity, but the ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: With scope and cost duplicated across separate documents, discrepancies are a chronic, poorly controlled failure. Reconciling scope/cost discrepancies across approval documents delays sign-off. Keeping figures consistent across multiple documents requires disciplined document control. Inconsistent cost figures risk approving the wrong budget number.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of CIP/SIP system approval being blocked because the acquisition exceeds the authorized capital budget
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that approval for the cleaning and sterilizing system is stopped because its purchase costs more than the approved budget. This helps the purchase move forward within authorized spending limits.
rows.imp_rat: Acquisition approval being blocked by exceeding the capital budget is a procurement/administrative error with no bearing on whether the system actually cleans or sterilizes; relevance to the CFJ is minimal and severity is a recoverable business setback.
rows.sat_rat: Formal capital-authorization processes make outright budget-driven blocks reasonably predictable, but late scope changes still occasionally exceed authorized limits. Capital budget checks are embedded in standard approval workflows and rarely add delay when followed. Budget alignment is routine finance and project-management practice not demanding specialized skill. Established capital-planning gates catch most budget overruns before approval, though scope creep still triggers occasional overruns.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of CIP/SIP system capital approval package resubmissions caused by scope or quoted-cost changes
rows.plain: Avoid having to send the investment request back for approval because the work or price has changed. This helps the purchase move forward without repeated reviews.
rows.imp_rat: Capital approval resubmissions is an administrative rework loop distant from the cleaning job, held at the minimum per instruction.
rows.sat_rat: Quotes for engineered CIP/SIP systems remain volatile until late in scoping, so status-quo packages frequently prove inaccurate and must be resubmitted. Resubmission cycles add weeks to the capital approval timeline as revised packages requeue through finance committees. Building an accurate capital package for complex CIP/SIP scope demands seasoned project-engineering and estimating judgement that few staff possess. Scope creep and vendor requoting during acquisition routinely inflate the capital figure and trigger costly rework of the approval package.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement between purchaser and seller over CIP/SIP system scope and acceptance criteria
rows.plain: Make sure the buyer and seller agree on what the cleaning and sterilizing system includes and how its completion will be judged. This prevents disputes about what was purchased and whether it meets expectations.
rows.imp_rat: Purchaser-seller disagreement over CIP/SIP scope and acceptance criteria is a contracting error, capped at 1 for both proxies per the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: URS and acceptance-test templates reduce disputes, yet meaningful scope and criteria disagreements between buyer and seller still recur. Resolving scope and acceptance-criteria disagreements consumes negotiation time before contract signing. Defining unambiguous CIP/SIP scope and acceptance criteria demands validation and engineering expertise that many buyers lack. Unresolved scope disputes cause costly change orders later, but the negotiation itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which the final contract price exceeds the purchaser's approved project budget
rows.plain: Keep the final purchase price within the buyer’s approved project budget. This prevents the completed purchase from exceeding available funds.
rows.imp_rat: Contract price exceeding budget is a commercial overrun with no direct link to the cleaning/sterilization function; both proxies held at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Competitive bidding and budget gates constrain overruns most of the time, but surprises on custom sanitary scope still push price over budget. Reconciling final price to budget happens within negotiation cycles that occasionally extend the timeline. Cost negotiation uses standard procurement skill rather than scarce expertise. Final contract prices often exceed approved budgets when scope grows or utilities integration is underestimated, and buffers are frequently thin.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical CIP/SIP system failures excluded from warranty coverage
rows.plain: Make sure serious failures in the system used to clean and sterilize equipment are covered by the warranty, so the customer is not left without support for important problems.
rows.imp_rat: Critical failures excluded from warranty is a commercial-coverage error with low direct relevance to executing cycles; batch ranking caps it at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Warranty terms commonly carve out critical CIP/SIP failure modes in ways buyers discover only after the fact, leaving coverage inconsistent. Clarifying warranty exclusions during negotiation adds contracting time but is not the main pain. Parsing which critical failures fall outside warranty coverage demands legal and technical scrutiny that many buyers underweight. Excluded critical failures expose the owner to large unplanned repair costs after purchase.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time contracted delivery falls after the sterile fill-finish project need date
rows.plain: Avoid having the promised delivery date fall after the date the sterile production project needs the purchase. Late delivery could delay the project.
rows.imp_rat: Delivery slipping past the project need date is a scheduling issue with limited direct bearing on the cleaning/sterilization function; capped at 1.0 by the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Lead-time quotes and milestone contracts help, but slippage against need dates remains common for custom sanitary systems. Contracted delivery frequently falls after the sterile fill-finish need date because engineered CIP/SIP skids have long, variable lead times. Negotiating and sequencing delivery against project needs requires planning skill but not deeply scarce expertise. Late delivery drives expediting fees and project-delay costs that acquisition teams often absorb.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required purchaser approvals remain outstanding at the signing deadline
rows.plain: Make sure all required buyer approvals are finished before the signing deadline. This allows the purchase to be signed on time.
rows.imp_rat: Outstanding purchaser approvals at signing deadline is an administrative procurement error with low functional relevance and mild consequence, held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard e-signature and procurement gate tools make on-time approval fairly repeatable, though last-minute outstanding sign-offs still surface on complex capital buys. Approval workflows have defined stage-gates but signatures often lag against the signing deadline when signatories are traveling or unavailable. Chasing approvals is administrative coordination rather than scarce expertise, so the skill burden is modest. Missed approval deadlines can force re-quoting or price escalations, but the direct cost impact is usually contained.
